sections.title: FORWARD
sections.text:
3 Ver. 25 FORWARD The Bonnar Bible was formerly known as the “How Do I” document, first created in 2018 to answer commonly asked questions. Starting as a basic collection of hints, the “How Do I” evolved to include various chapters including an introduction to reel mowers through to the various stages of a restoration, maintenance issues, pictorial references and much, much more. It outgrew the “How Do I” description and the name was changed to reflect the broader content. Whilst this is predominantly a Scott Bonnar Model 45 guide, most topics will also assist with other models e.g., Models 33, 38, 40 and others. A separate chapter has been included for issues specific to those other models. Scott Bonnar Restoration (SBR) members can and should help themselves by: • Taking pictures from different angles before and during the disassembly of your project. These will be an invaluable reference come the time you reassemble your project. • Referring to the files section and refer to the parts diagrams; and • Most importantly - USE THE SEARCH FUNCTION AND SEARCH THE PAGE.

The advice, tips and suggestions contained within the Bonnar Bible are as offered by SBR members and are provided and reproduced in good faith. So stated, there may be other ways or variations which have not been provided. If you have any doubt as to the advice or suggestions given, you should seek further guidance. If you lack the skills or confidence to undertake any aspect of the restoration, rebuilding or repairs, you are also advised to seek assistance. Should you proceed, do so within the limits of your competence and confidence in the advice you are acting on. The sourcing of parts or services is up to you, from which ever supplier you choose. Where a specific brand, product or supplier is mentioned, no recommendation or endorsement should be inferred.

The Bonnar Bible is exclusive to the SBR page. It is by SBR members and for SBR members. It is not intended for sharing outside the SBR page. The SBR page is a closed group, so please respect the fact that people have posted in a closed group that is not open to all and sundry. Download it, but do not share it outside the intended SBR page audience.

sections.title: CHAPTER 1 – INTRODUCTION TO REEL MOWERS
sections.text:
19 Ver. 25

CHAPTER 1 – INTRODUCTION TO REEL MOWERS

sections.title: What is a Reel Mower?
sections.text:
20 Ver. 25 What is a Reel Mower? Are you thinking of, or have you bought your first reel mower? Before you embark on the Scott Bonnar adventure it is worthwhile gaining a basic understanding of what a reel or cylinder mower is. Simply put it is a mower that uses multiple spinning blades mounted on a reel assembly, rotating on a horizontal shaft to cut grass between the reel and cutting bar or blade in a scissoring motion. Individual grass blades are cut rather than slashed as with a rotary mower. To understand the cutting method of these mowers, think of how a pair of scissors cuts paper. Two sharpened steel edges make contact in a slide-like motion. The cutting action is intended and works best on low or short grass producing a healthier cut grass. Reel mowers may be manual or pushed along (or pulled as in gang mowers) or powered usually by petrol or electric motors. How does a reel mower work? • Power from the motor is output through a primary clutch which enables the engine to be started without being under load. • Engaging the primary clutch provides power to the reel and drive mechanisms. • The drive mechanism, using belts or chains and pulleys and/or sprockets (depending on model) now has power directed to: o Operate and spin the reel (note: cutter speed and roller speed are not synchronized.) o Engaging the landroll clutch via the handlebar located lever now provides drive to the rear roller and the mower is propelled forwards. The operation of a reel mower is not complex. It is the adjusting or fine tuning of clutches, chain or belt tensions, reel to bed knife clearance and the level of the mower which causes problems. These issues and more will be addressed throughout this guide. Just remember, they are simple machines which work logically and through problem solving issues can be overcome. Basic components of a Reel Mower The following are the basic components of a reel mower. Depending on the make and model, they come in various shapes, sizes, styles and configurations. • Bed knife— the stationary cutting component. This is a fixed horizontal blade that is mounted to the chassis/frame of the mower. • Blade reel/cylinder— consists of numerous helical blades that are attached to a rotating shaft. Generally, they come in 6, 8,9, 12 and 20 blade configurations. The higher the number the better the cut. • Catcher – to collect cut grass clippings and usually hangs off the side plates. • Chassis or frame— the main structural component of the mower onto which the other parts of the mower are mounted. Consists of side plates and either a solid deck or rails connecting the plates and the base for the engine and drive components to be mounted • Clutch assemblies – come in various types and configurations to engage power from the motor to the drive gears. Model dependent, some are cork on metal, others are metal on metal and even rubber on metal. • Drive gears – to propel the rear drive rollers and the spinning reel. • Front roller – leads the cutting unit and is used to set the cutting height up/down. • Grass Deflector – to direct grass cuttings from the reel to the catcher. Some are adjustable to direct the cuttings lower or higher into the catcher.

21 Ver. 25 • Handles — used to push and guide the mower and provides a mounting point for engine and clutch controls. • Motor— 2 or 4 stroke internal combustion or electric. • Rear drum or roller— used to propel the mower. Combined with the cutting action, the heavy rear rollers help to produce the stripe effect. May be single, double or triple. Multi drum rear rollers have an internal differential set up to assist in turning the mower. The below pictures illustrate the major components. Please refer to the Files section of the Scott Bonnar Restorations page for detailed Parts Diagrams.

Handlebars Engine Reel Front Roller Mounting rail Side plate Primary clutch Grass deflector Height adjuster Side plate PTO or drive shaft Model 45 – major parts, Credit Brandon Jule SBR. Throttle control Clutch lever Chain cover

22 Ver. 25

Twin Rail Model 45 – major parts, Credit Michael Devereaux SBR Grass Catcher Grass Deflector Clutch halves Clutch lever Thrust Pad Clutch lever Rear roller Deflector knob Rail Clutch fork assembly PTO or drive shaft Clutch Cone

23 Ver. 25

Model 45 Solid - main parts under the chain cover. Credit Steve Berghofer, SBR

If you want more information on reel mowers, Toro have an excellent document titled “Reel Mower Basics”2. I commend this document, it will provide you with a basic knowledge of reel mower operating theory, set-up, adjustment, and maintenance. In recent years there has been a resurgence in the popularity of reel mowers. People are keen to get the stripe effect on their lawn, something the lower cutting and heavier reel mower produce. Turn the clock back to the 1950’s and 1960’s prior to the emergence of rotary mowers, reel mowers whether manual or petrol powered were popular. These were the days when Scott Bonnar was a popular brand. Unfortunately, Scott Bonnar as a company is no more but the legacy of the company lives on in the form of the restored mowers. This guide is about keeping that legacy alive.

2 https://cdn2.toro.com/en/-/media/Files/Toro/Commercial/education-technical-references/service-training- guides/09168sl_5-4-2018.ashx?la=en&hash=1531E3CD624D41D0E5C07157493AFECD0FF67BEE accessed 18 July 2019. Front roller adj. assembly Cutter sprocket Large inter. Sprocket Motor sprocket Drum sprocket Chain adjuster/tensioner Hand nut or height adjuster

sections.title: CHAPTER 2 – IDENTIFICATION
sections.text:
24 Ver. 25

CHAPTER 2 – IDENTIFICATION

sections.title: Brief History (UPDATED)
sections.text:
25 Ver. 25 Brief History (UPDATED) Scott Bonnar & Co, founded by Malcolm "Mick" and Scott Bonnar was first registered on 20 January 1920. Scott Bonnar’s first machine shop in Chapel Street, Southwark opened in 19203. The business included a foundry making brass products e.g., tapware. In 1923 they made their first electric lawn mower, adding an electric motor to an earlier Thomas Green and Son (Leeds) chain mower. Quiet and efficient it was used at the Glenelg Bowling Club with great success. They were an Atco Petrol Mower agent until 1924-25, when, because they were concentrating on electric mowers the English manufacturers took the agency from them. In 1926 they were the first company to produce electric lawnmowers in Australia4. In the 1930’s they produced the ESBE brand rotary pumps, bean slicers and a sidewheel mower together with but a range of quality reel/roller mowers namely the Bon, the Standard, the Dux, and the Putting Green Mower5. They then started making petrol mowers and expanded to making gang mowers which were

3 G.Grainger/J.Andrewartha (2023). From Wool Scouring to Washing Machines: West Torrens Historical Society 4 Ibid 5 https://outdoorking-forum.com.au/forum/ubbthreads.php/topics/77118/model-bon-a-handmower- c1930s.html?fbclid=IwY2xjawEhQjVleHRuA2FlbQIxMAABHRhw5DBjCvfAgtqD5Z1oPPHhNTttERFN0HWPPXaeiHL2dB YW_t4s_1aW8A_aem_U-3u22yG1iOv9XRFlK_0iA#Post77118 accessed 8/8/2024. Model 45 Brochure Credit Michael Lane, SBR Scott Bonnar Thebarton Factory. Credit From Wool Scouring to Washing Machines: West Torrens Historical Society

26 Ver. 25 popular for parks and ovals.6. Success followed the company for the next fifty years when they were supplying mowers to ninety nine percent of the 4000 bowling greens in Australia. Scott Bonnar moved to 33-47 Holland Street, Thebarton in 19367. The Adelaide factory had showrooms in all states and exported successfully to South Africa8 Mr. Scott Bonnar left the business in 1947 due to ill health and died in 1961 aged 68years. After the later passing of Mr. M.C Bonnar the company CEO was his son, Fax. Fax Bonnar ran the company up until when Electrolux sold it to Challenge Corporation's company Morrison Industries out of New Zealand in 1974. The Model 17 Supercut was first released in 1950, the Model 19 Bonmow in 1953, the first 30” Queen mower was produced in 1958 (a marked change from the previous 24” version). The Model 33 was produced in 1959-60. The Model 33 was initially released as a “Bonmow,” but Scott Bonnar dropped the Bonmow name, and the mower simply became known as the 16" Model 33. The first Model 33's were all- chain driven, at some point they moved to being belt driven to the reel. The later Lawn King variant moved back to being all chain drive and had many parts later found on the Model 45 (e.g., 17” rear rollers; primary clutch and the chain idler). In 1960 – 61 the Model 40 was introduced. It was known as a “terrace mower”. The Model 46 Scarifier was introduced in the early 1960s when Scott Bonnar were expanding their professional range. The Model 45 was released in 1968, it has been described as the morphing of the Models 33 and 40.

6 Bonnar, M.C. (1971). The Memoirs of MC Bonnar. Adelaide: ES Wigg & Sons. 7 G.Grainger/J.Andrewartha (2023). From Wool Scouring to Washing Machines: West Torrens Historical Society 8 https://www.carrickhill.sa.gov.au/events/exhibitions/the-blade-australias-love-affair-with- lawn?fbclid=IwAR0rolc8FBfSIWTg2Gqf8xWVIIJfjgkXdpqLAKBZ86z5yLGL0EVbJ4N112M. Accessed 22 April 2021 Scott Bonnar premises, Gouger St, Adelaide circa 1956. Credit Bruce Banks, SBR Scott Bonnar Head Office and factory, Thebarton, South Australia, Credit Electrolux 1970 Annual Report

27 Ver. 25 The growth in the greens mower business of Scott Bonnar can be seen when in 1968 there were approximately 4000 bowling greens in Australia and 90% of them used Scott Bonnar machines. Scott Bonnar Ltd produced more than just lawn mowers, they manufactured and sold a range of lawn and greens care products. They made their own fertilizer spreaders as well as being distributors for Dutschke, Hodge and Kaye products. In 1970 the Swedish company Electrolux paid 165 cents per share for the whole of the company’s shares, carrying on as Scott Bonnar Ltd. The sale transaction also included the purchase of the subsidiary company Scott Bonnar Sales Pty Ltd9. Electrolux at that time had the Flymo brand of lawnmowers1011 with the sales of their air cushion mowers transferred to Scott Bonnar. The Scott Bonnar brass factory producing taps and plumbing fittings ceased circa 1974.

The Model 45 was first released with the Kirby- Tecumseh engine (on the 17” & 20” but not the 14”) and then with the 2.5HP Briggs (Model 450520 17") in late 1973/1974 which later became the Model 450589, 17" with the larger Briggs 3HP engine. Solid decks were produced from 1968-70, twin rails in all sizes started in 1973. Solids were listed in the product range up till 1975 but were gone by the 1978 product line. The Kirby engines stayed on the 14” models only through the early 1970’s on the twin rails and changed over to the orange Briggs and Stratton engines around mid-1970’s and discontinued the 14” around 1977-78. In 1973, the New Zealand company Challenge Corporation Ltd acquired Scott Bonnar. Peter Morison (SBR member and former Scott Bonnar employee) advises then came the 590 rear catcher cylinder mower followed by the 430 Diplomat rear catcher with a separate cutter clutch. During this time Scott Bonner started casting the 520 based on the northern side of the road/factory separate from the cast iron foundry where Alan Crisp (soccer writer for the Advertiser Newspaper) looked after the foundry. They brought with them the Toro franchise. Stan Rapson was the Managing Director during that time until it was sold to Rover in December 1980. Ron

9 https://www.electroluxgroup.com/wp-content/uploads/sites/2/1980/04/Electrolux-Annual-Report-1970- English.pdf accessed 25 May 2021 10 IBID 11 Bonnar, M.C. (1971). The Memoirs of MC Bonnar. Adelaide: ES Wigg & Sons. Below: Scott Bonnar Catalogue Credit Jesse Walton, SBR

28 Ver. 25 Collier, Design Engineer and Bill Henderson Manufacturing Manager also from NZ took over those roles during this time12.. Hence the connection with Morrison Mowers and the cross badged Model 690 and the Rotary Hoe. 1973 coincides with “cost savings” being introduced into Scott Bonnar products such as the twin rail 45’s and the change to alloy ID plates. Even the award winning Model 430 (and 590) appears to have fallen victim to cost cutting. Gone were the sprockets and chain drives, replaced with belt drives, plastic drive gears and even ratchet boxes just pop riveted into the rear roller. These were probably the last of the “true” Scott Bonnar designed mowers. Around this time it appears the model numbering system went from consecutive two digit numbers to three digit model numbers. There were the Model 320 and 420 rotary mowers and the Model 430 reel mower and so on. The Challenge Corp takeover also saw the cross badging of Morrison products as Scott Bonnars. And visa versa. The model 690 number was applied to more than just the reel mower; it had the secondary model numbers of 0376 and 0608. There was the Model 690 (0608) mini hoe; the Model 690 (0894 and 0915) rotary mowers. Furthermore, there were cross badged Toro machines also with the 690 Model number. These being the Model 690 (0860) ride on mower and the Model 690 (0886) garden tiller. The Design Award Winning Model 590 was introduced in 1975/76. It was a landmark version offering a reel mower with a rear catcher enabling users to get into those “tight” corners. “Scott Bonnar” manufactured Model 45 machines continued in production until 1980. In 1980 Scott Bonnar was taken over completely by Rover Mowers (Aust) Pty Ltd, based in Brisbane. The company name was then changed to Rover-Scott Bonnar Ltd. with the mowers being ID badged Rover- Scott Bonnar. The cast iron factory continued until 1981. Then in 1983 the Thebarton factory of Scott Bonnar Ltd in South Australia was closed. Production moved to Eagle Farm, Queensland. Even after this shift, hammertone models were still produced for a time as they used available SA parts stock. Sometime in 1984 they then started manufacturing the powder coated green “Kermits”, but still decal branded the mowers as Scott Bonnar. These “Kermits” were manufactured from circa 1984 to around 1994-95 when the “Red Rovers” replaced the Scott Bonnar name for good. Model 45 Rovers were only produced in 17” versions. Rover was subsequently bought by MTD Products Australia. Rover lawn mowers were manufactured in Eagle Farm until 1 April 2007 (Brisbane factory closure) when MTD Products Australia moved production to China. SBR member Andy Enright (worked as Rover Mowers spare parts Warehouse supervisor) advises when Rover moved their operations to China, the Ride-on and Cylinder mowers were put out to different manufacturers in China, New Zealand and South Africa to have them made at a reasonable price. Unfortunately, no one was found and most of the dies and foundry castings were destroyed.

12 https://www.facebook.com/groups/1801521490103765/permalink/3481404385448792/ Accessed 5/10/2023

sections.title: How do I identify what mower I have?
sections.text:
29 Ver. 25 The cease in production of the Model 45 may have been attributed to competition from cheap imported Star brand Chinese copies. With the copies selling for about $990 compared to the Rover Model 45 for about $1850, the days of the genuine Model 45 were numbered. IP Australia records reveal Scott Bonnar had various Patents registered from 1937 - 1975. A couple appear to be for the Diplomat and the later Queen (Model 30), but none identified relating to the likes of models 33, 40 or 45. In any event the Patents have well and truly ceased, and the specifications are open to the public. Rover Mowers have various Patents, one appears to be for the Thoroughbred, but none for the Model 45. “Scott Bonnar” is still a registered trademark. Both the words “Scott Bonnar” and the Scott Bonnar pentagon are trademarks registered to MTD Products Inc who own Rover.

How do I identify what mower I have? Now that you have gained an understanding of reel mowers, you may want to know more about your mower, such as its size, age and importantly what is the model number.

What Model is it? The easiest way to identify the model of a mower is to find the ID plate usually located on the rear rail or to the rear edge of a solid deck. The ID plate will identify the model number. Where the ID plate (see ID Plates, below) has either a Mower or Serial Number the first two digits denote the model number. Refer to Annex 1 for a pictorial reference of various models.

Difference between Solid and Twin Rails The popular Model 45’s come in two configurations, commonly referred to as solid deck and twin rail. The correct term as per the parts diagrams is “engine table” for the “solid” and “mower frame assembly” for the “twin rail”. A solid deck is one plate and twin rails as the term indicates have two rails where the engine bolts onto. The solid was the first of the models, then to cut costs the twin rail was introduced. During the 1980’s it was realized the rails were bending and cracking, so they were upgraded to a thicker gauge steel with a deeper drop on the rails.

30 Ver. 25

Left - Solid Deck. Right - Twin rail, Credit Glenn Russel, SBR There are other distinctive differences between the solids and twin rails. Chain Covers for solids are mounted with a horizontal configuration whereas twin decks are vertically mounted. Solid decks bolt onto the side plates whereas the twin rails are welded onto the side plates and form a complete one piece chassis. Other differences are the chain tensioners, clutch cable mounts, clutch fork assembly and behind the chain tensioner on a twin rail there is a dust cover whereas the solid does not have one. Solid front roller end caps had the metal riveted ends whereas twins had black plastic caps. Model 33 and 40’s are solid decks.

Below are various pictures to illustrate the differences

Vertical mounts - Twin rail chain cover Credit Justin Silvestro, SBR Horizontal mounts - Solid deck chain cover Credit Lee Willcock, SBR

31 Ver. 25

Solid Deck Twin Rail

Chain tensioner/guide Credit Kym Schrapel, SBR

Chain tensioner/guide Credit Bluey Cullen, SBR

Bolted on Clutch cable bracket Credit Rhys Harris, SBR

Welded clutch cable bracket Credit Brett Clancy SBR

Bolted on clutch fork assembly with riser plate between deck and assembly, Credit Michael Nietz, SBR

Welded clutch fork assembly and dust cover, Credit Mic David, SBR

Rover Mowers have various Patents, one appears to be for the Thoroughbred, but none for the Model 45. “Scott Bonnar” is still a registered trademark. Both the words “Scott Bonnar” and the Scott Bonnar pentagon are trademarks registered to MTD Products Inc who own Rover.

How do I identify what mower I have? Now that you have gained an understanding of reel mowers, you may want to know more about your mower, such as its size, age and importantly what is the model number.

What Model is it? The easiest way to identify the model of a mower is to find the ID plate usually located on the rear rail or to the rear edge of a solid deck. The ID plate will identify the model number. Where the ID plate (see ID Plates, below) has either a Mower or Serial Number the first two digits denote the model number. Refer to Annex 1 for a pictorial reference of various models.

Difference between Solid and Twin Rails The popular Model 45’s come in two configurations, commonly referred to as solid deck and twin rail. The correct term as per the parts diagrams is “engine table” for the “solid” and “mower frame assembly” for the “twin rail”. A solid deck is one plate and twin rails as the term indicates have two rails where the engine bolts onto. The solid was the first of the models, then to cut costs the twin rail was introduced. During the 1980’s it was realized the rails were bending and cracking, so they were upgraded to a thicker gauge steel with a deeper drop on the rails.

30 Ver. 25

Left - Solid Deck. Right - Twin rail, Credit Glenn Russel, SBR There are other distinctive differences between the solids and twin rails. Chain Covers for solids are mounted with a horizontal configuration whereas twin decks are vertically mounted. Solid decks bolt onto the side plates whereas the twin rails are welded onto the side plates and form a complete one piece chassis. Other differences are the chain tensioners, clutch cable mounts, clutch fork assembly and behind the chain tensioner on a twin rail there is a dust cover whereas the solid does not have one. Solid front roller end caps had the metal riveted ends whereas twins had black plastic caps. Model 33 and 40’s are solid decks.

Below are various pictures to illustrate the differences

Vertical mounts - Twin rail chain cover Credit Justin Silvestro, SBR Horizontal mounts - Solid deck chain cover Credit Lee Willcock, SBR

31 Ver. 25

Solid Deck Twin Rail

Chain tensioner/guide Credit Kym Schrapel, SBR

Chain tensioner/guide Credit Bluey Cullen, SBR

Bolted on Clutch cable bracket Credit Rhys Harris, SBR

Welded clutch cable bracket Credit Brett Clancy SBR

Bolted on clutch fork assembly with riser plate between deck and assembly, Credit Michael Nietz, SBR

Welded clutch fork assembly and dust cover, Credit Mic David, SBR

sections.title: Difference between Solid and Twin Rails
sections.text:
29 Ver. 25 The cease in production of the Model 45 may have been attributed to competition from cheap imported Star brand Chinese copies. With the copies selling for about $990 compared to the Rover Model 45 for about $1850, the days of the genuine Model 45 were numbered. IP Australia records reveal Scott Bonnar had various Patents registered from 1937 - 1975. A couple appear to be for the Diplomat and the later Queen (Model 30), but none identified relating to the likes of models 33, 40 or 45. In any event the Patents have well and truly ceased, and the specifications are open to the public. Rover Mowers have various Patents, one appears to be for the Thoroughbred, but none for the Model 45. “Scott Bonnar” is still a registered trademark. Both the words “Scott Bonnar” and the Scott Bonnar pentagon are trademarks registered to MTD Products Inc who own Rover.

sections.title: Difference between Models 33; 45 and Rover Thoroughbred
sections.text:
32 Ver. 25 Difference between Models 33; 45 and Rover Thoroughbred Scott Bonnar Models 33 and 45 to the inexperienced appear similar, when in fact they are quite different. Similarly, some people have mistaken the primary clutch on the Rover Thoroughbred for Scott Bonnar Model 45’s. I jokingly describe the Thoroughbred as the “second cousin, twice removed from a different mother”; in other words, a Thoroughbred is NOT a Scott Bonnar, it is a Rover. Below are various pictures highlighting the visual differences between these three mowers.

Thoroughbred Model 33 Model 45 Height Adjuster

Primary Clutch

Credit Chris Kermond, SBR Credit Michael Hardman, SBR Centrifugal clutch Credit Brendan Noolan, SBR Credit Chris Channell, SBR Credit Ollie Morris, SBR Note the longer cone and rear facing lever. Credit Kym Schrapel, SBR

33 Ver. 25 Thoroughbred Model 33 Model 45 PTO Bearing Holder

Handlebar plate

Model 33’s do not have a handlebar plate. They have the “pram” style handlebars. The Handlebar plate is the same shape, size and position on all Mod 45’s except those with the “pram” style handlebars that do not have a plate. It is just the decals that vary. There are other differences such as the grass deflector mounts and the reel bearing holders. If you are looking at a mower then look at the ID Plate, that will tell you the model number.

Credit Rodney Unicomb Credit Andrew Suter, SBR Credit Ashlee Panting Credit Kym Schrapel, SBR Credit Troy Burton, SBR Rover Thoroughbred Credit Chris Pedersen, SBR Mod 33 Credit Kym Schrapel, SBR Mod 45 Credit Paul Ryan, SBR

sections.title: What is a Unicorn?
sections.text:
34 Ver. 25

What is a Unicorn? Those who are new to the Scott Bonnar scene will hear the term “Unicorn”; mythical creatures which are rarely seen, to describe a mower. The Unicorn is just a name given by somebody with a passion for Model 45's. So, what is it? Model 45, 20” solid decks (Unicorns) were originally part of the Scott Bonnar “Professional” range. The 20” axle is bigger (1” shaft diameter) than a 17” (7/8” shaft diameter) to obviously increase the overall strength in the shaft length. Therefore the 20” has larger rear roller bushes (which are different to the 14 and 17” versions) and the drum shaft collars have a larger inside diameter hole. The rear shaft has a step down on the shaft at the bearings to allow a universal bearing size The Unicorn also has ratchet boxes which have a slightly larger opening. Rear rollers of 17” versions therefore will not fit the Unicorn. Early Unicorns came with an 8 blade reel.

The Unicorn is a Scott Bonnar Model 45, 20” solid deck with triple rear roller. The Unicorn. Note the engine position and the 6 reel blade supports identifying it as a 20”. Credit Glenn Russell, SBR Ratchet boxes - Left - Unicorn, Right 14 and 17". Credit Glenn Russell, SBR

35 Ver. 25

Rear roller shafts - Left - 14 and 17" Right - Unicorn. Credit Glenn Russell, SBR During the transition from solid decks to twin rails (circa 1973), some Model 45, 20” twin rails came out with the triple rear roller. They are NOT Unicorns despite being far less common than the solid deck “Unicorn”. There are Scott Bonnar models (e.g., Model 43 or 12 volt battery Model 40’s) which are harder to find than the 20” solid, but the 20” solid is harder to find than other sizes and is the most sought after of the Model 45’s. BEWARE – there exist “ ” builds i.e., twin rail 20” Model 45’s converted to appear like a solid deck purporting to be Unicorns.

sections.title: What Size is it?
sections.text:
36 Ver. 25

What Size is it? The Model 45 saw Scott Bonnar introduce scalability across the one domestic mower model with 14”, 17” and 20” versions. Prior to that different sizes were only available via their three different domestic mower models: • Model 40 - 14” • Model 33 - 16” • Model 43 - 20” Determining the size of the Model 45 is a simple matter of counting the reel blade supports i.e., 4, 5 or 6 equals 14”, 17” or 20” and the positioning of engine and the PTO shaft length. 14's have a shorter (160 mm) engine shaft, 20's have a gap between the right-hand side of the motor and side rail, 17's the motor is generally flush between the right-hand side and the side of the mower, although they can vary with different engines fitted.

The measurement of the size (14’, 17’ etc.) is the distance between the outer reel blade supports

Scott Bonnar Catalogue circa 1978 Credit Outdoorking.com Motor flush with the side 5 reel blade supports = 17” 17” Model 45 - Size indicators. Credit Troy Carter, SBR

sections.title: How old is it?
sections.text:
37 Ver. 25 How old is it? Trying to date your mower by the ID Plate serial number is not possible. ID Plates The ID Plates provide an indication in general year terms as to the age of the mower as follows: • Brass plates Brass plates came with several subtle variations. SBR member Will Be points out they had square corners, then beveled or 45 degree corners and finally with rounded corners. There were also variations in the spacing of the letters.

There are no known records available from the former Scott Bonnar/Rover that can date mowers based on serial numbers off the ID Plates. Early Mod 33 Brass ID Square corners with condensed lettering Credit Will Be, SBR Later Model 33 Brass ID Square corners with less condensed lettering Credit Will Be, SBR

38 Ver. 25

Brass ID with square corners (off an early Mod 45 14' solid) Credit Karl Shepherd, SBR Brass ID with beveled or 45 degree corners Credit Will Be, SBR Brass ID with rounded corners. Note the "condensed" or close letter spacing. Credit Will Be, SBR

39 Ver. 25

• Brass single plates 1968 – 71. These mainly had rounded corners although some early models had square corners.

• Brass double plate 1972 – 73 (Plates for model number and mower number) As with the single brass plates these had rounded corners.

• Mixed Twin plates (brass on alloy) 1973 (Brass mower number and alloy model number). Appears to have been during the change from double brass to double alloy plates, being affixed on early gen twin rails with straight handlebars & Kirby engine.

Combination Brass/Alloy plates with “square” corners Credit Tim Kay, SBR Single Brass Plate Credit Andrew Doyle, SBR Double brass ID Plate Credit Tony Bitonatm

40 Ver. 25 • Double Alloy plates 1973 – 1975 (alloy plates introduced as a cost saving measure in late 1973)

• Alloy mower no. over brass model no. This ID plate combination has been rarely seen. It is thought to be circa 1973 when the alloy top ID plate was introduced. It may have simply been a carryover using old plates, or simply because it’s a Scott Bonnar and things like that happened.

• Single Alloy plates 1975 – 77

Alloy Plate Credit Shawn Adams, SBR Twin Alloy Plates. Credit Tim Kay, SBR Alloy over brass. Credit Jayden Spencer, SBR

41 Ver. 25

• Sticker ID 1978 – 80 May be found on models other than Model 45 e.g., 430 Diplomats and 38 Edgers

• Green alloy plate 1980 Believed to have been used before the plates went to the Rover/Scott Bonnar version. Not commonly seen.

• Green Rover Scott Bonnar plate 1980-83 This green version of the Rover Scott Bonnar plate is also not commonly seen. It is probably a transition plate between the first green alloy plates and the later black Rover Scott Bonnar plates.

Sticker ID, Credit Shayne Symes, SBR Green colored Allor Scott Bonnar plate. Credit Luke Hammer, SBR Green Rover Scott Bonnar plate Credit Brenton Hetherington, SBR

42 Ver. 25 • Rover-Scott Bonnar plate 1980 – 83 (3 versions – with/without Brisbane address)

• ROVER Mowers alloy plate 1983 onwards. This was seen on “Kermit’s” from about 1984 to around 1994 and even appeared on the Red Rovers.

Rover Scott Bonnar plate Credit Shannon Barber, SBR Rover Mowers (Aust) ID. Credit Scott Beinke, SBR Alloy Rover ID Plate from mid-late 80’s possibly mid 90’s Credit Warrick Fazakerley, SBR Rover Scott Bonnar Note the Brisbane address. .Credit John Kane, SBR Red "Rover-Scott Bonnar alloy plate found on a Rover "Rapid 4" rotary mower. Rarely reported. Possibly a "transition” badge Credit Christopher Courtney, SBR.

sections.title: Decal dating
sections.text:
43 Ver. 25

• Rover sticker Mid 1990’s – 2000’s

SBR member Jamie Cleveland, a former employee at Rover Scott Bonnar advises that for Rover ID Plates the first three digits of the serial number are the week and year of production. Model 38 Edger’s similarly saw ID plates change from metal plates to the sticker form. The ID plates from the sticker (1978) onwards are of a screen-printed type and do not have raised lettering like the earlier plates. This means restoration options are limited. Be careful that you do not remove the paint as you will only have a metal plate with stamped numbers visible. Whatever form of ID plate/sticker you have, treat them with care – some are NOT available after market. It has been reported (unconfirmed) that the first ten ID Plates/mowers went to the Bonnar family and high profile people in the business. Decal dating Below are a range of decals which appeared on Scott Bonnars. As with ID Plates, they provide a general indication as to the age of a mower. This section may also be of assistance when purchasing decals if you are concerned with it being period correct.

Chain cover of the Scott Bonnar Model 45 of the 1960s and 1970s. It goes with the winged catcher decal with the "saw blade" pattern in the medallion.

Later model Rover Sticker Credit Joe Rogers, SBR Credit eBay seller silensmessor

44 Ver. 25

The picture opposite from an early Scott Bonnar brochure (circa 1968) depicts a Series 1 Model 45, 17” solid with a Kirby engine, there were decals on the handlebar plate, fuel tank and the front of the catcher but not on the chain cover. The handlebar plate has the 'saw blades' pattern around the 'SB'. The catcher decal is the “yin and yang decal” without the saw blade pattern.

In the picture opposite the “saw blade” decals are on the handlebar plate and catcher with the “Scott Bonnar” and “45” chain case decals on a 17” twin rail circa 1976.

The pentagon decal and the small handlebar medallion were used on the Scott Bonnar Model 45 from mid-late 70's to the 1980s.

Credit eBay seller silensmessor Credit Outdoorking.com Credit Outdoorking.com

45 Ver. 25

The original pentagon shaped decals of mid-late 70's machines were white and black on a clear background. The originals were clear so that you can see the original hammertone paint work through the decal.

The red pentagon decal and the small handlebar medallion were used on some Scott Bonnar Model 45 mowers in the 1980s

Grass catcher and medallion for the handlebar cover plate for early Model 45s (series 1) produced in the 1960s and 1970s The small version of the winged decal with a 'blades' pattern around 'SB' was used on the cowling or petrol tank of various rotary mowers and edgers.

Credit eBay seller silensmessor Credit eBay seller silensmessor Original pentagon decal Credit Outdoorking.com

46 Ver. 25

Rover/Scott Bonnar Model 45, 17- inch mower, catcher and handlebar cover plate.

Chain cover of the Rover/Scott Bonnar Model

45. Black band and red Scott Bonnar shield go with the similar catcher decal as above.

Decal with a black and silver band goes on the catcher of the Rover Model 45 mower. The decal suits a 17-inch “Red Rover” mower.

Credit eBay seller silensmessor Credit eBay seller silensmessor Credit eBay seller silensmessor

47 Ver. 25

Chain cover decal of some red Rover - Scott Bonnar Model 45 reel mowers.

Export Award decal. Found on various machines in the late 1960’s after Scott Bonnar received an Export Award in 1966.

Decals which appeared on Model 45 mowers produced in the 1960s and 1970s with a Kirby Tecumseh engine.

Depicted are engine cowling Indian Head, Air Filter Instructions and the orange Petrol Tank decals depending on the engine and mower size.

Credit eBay seller silensmessor Credit eBay seller silensmessor Export Award decal Credit Kym Schrapel, SBR

48 Ver. 25 Petrol tank decals: ‘Export Award' sticker from the mid-1960s (e.g., as seen on the front of the Kirby petrol tank of Model 33’s)

Update: The 14” decal also appeared on the front facing side of the grey coloured Briggs and Stratton engine on the 14” Model 45’s.

This decal was used on the grass catcher of the early Scott Bonnar 'Bonmow' 16" Model 33 mower introduced in 1960 with a Villiers 98cc Midget Two Stroke engine. It also appeared on some later examples of the Scott Bonnar 'Bonmow' 14" Model 19 fitted with a Villiers 70cc Mark 3G Two Stroke engine.

Credit eBay seller silensmessor Credit eBay seller silensmessor

sections.title: Engine number dating
sections.text:
49 Ver. 25

Engine number dating The Model 45 released in 1968 came with the Kirby- Tecumseh engine and then with the 2.5HP Briggs Model 450520 17" in late 1973/74 which later became the Model 450589 17" with the larger Briggs 3HP engine; prior to them being badged Rover-Scott Bonnar after the takeover in 1980 and finally as Rover in 1983. See Annexure 1 and 2 for Model Numbers to Engine information. Those fortunate enough to have a mower with an original Briggs and Stratton engine, it is possible to date the mower via the engine number. You can determine the age of your engine if it was made after 1965. The engine's code number indicates the exact date it was manufactured. This system provides an easy way to determine the age of your small engine. The following is an example of interpreting the date for the code number 9901155613: • The first two digits (99) denote the year of manufacture. In this case it is 1999. • The next two digits (01) are the month of the year, which is January. • The following two digits (15) are the day of the month. In this case the 15th. • The final two digits designate the plant and assembly line. Therefore, this engine’s manufacture date was January 15, 1999. Unfortunately, the process for determining the manufacture date for older engines is not the same since the tracking system with the Code Number was not implemented until 1965. A source for older and antique Briggs & Stratton engines can be found through the following link - B&S Antique Engine Dating Chart Kirby engines were a popular choice for lawnmower manufacturers in Australia in the 1960s and 1970s. Made under licence from the North American Tecumseh company, the HK25 and HK30 engines powered the early Victa Imperials, Rover Thoroughbreds, and Scott Bonnar Model 45. Since Kirby’s are basically Tecumseh engines and the ones on SBs were made before 2004 from what I have found (courtesy of Google research), there should be 4 numbers and a letter on the engine plate. First number indicates the year, but you must estimate the decade so e.g., 2 would be either 1972 or 1982 etc. Being on a SB the estimated year would be 1972. The next 3 numbers are day of the year starting from 001 for January 1st. Last letter denotes where made. For those with Villiers engines, from Internet research conducted, dating via the engine number appears to be a can of worms. It appears the best way is to locate numbers stamped on the flywheel. If it is one of

13 https://www.briggsandstratton.com/na/en_us/support/faqs/browse/engine-manufacture-date.html accessed 22 August 2018.

Briggs & Stratton engine number. Manufacture date 15/02/1969. Credit Aaron Smillie, SBR

sections.title: Which engine was fitted to which model?
sections.text:
50 Ver. 25 the older engines you will need to remove the cowl. On the outer edge of the flywheel there is a string of numbers, the last 2 denote the year IE 62 = 1962 etc. Which engine was fitted to which model? Over the years Scott Bonnar used a range of engines. From Villiers, BSA, Kirby Lausen or Kirby Tecumseh, Briggs and Stratton, Hondas and even their own engine on the Model 43. Model 40’s had the 75cc Villiers 4 stroke then the 2 HP Briggs and Stratton which appeared on the early Model 45’s. Earliest Model 45, twin rail 14” had a 2.5 hp Kirby engine, later by 1975 they had the orange Briggs and Stratton. Solid deck 14’s we’re still distributed until 1975 alongside the twin rail. Solid 14” were discontinued in late 1975. They had the silver 2 and 2.5 HP engine. Model 45’s in 14” were discontinued in 1980. For a more detailed engine listings please refer to Annex 314 and 415, obtained from Outdoorking.com (with some minor additions to Annex 2).

Other age indicators (UPDATED) In addition to the ID plates, decals and engines, there are several other indictors as to the probable age of your mower. • If you have a Model 45 twin deck which has no holes on the rear rail where the ID plate would normally be (adjacent to the clutch fork); you probably have a model that has lost its sticker ID. o That is, a 1978 -1980 vintage or a later model Rover from mid 1990s onwards if it is a red colored mower. o Mowers with sticker ID’s often appear on the rail with no holes where the metal ID’s would have been secured. However, this is not always the case, some Scott Bonnar Model 45’s and later Rover Model 45’s have been found with holes where the metal ID’s would be secured whilst having sticker ID’s. • Catcher rubber mounts o Scott Bonnar Model 45’s - welded on o Rover made Model 45’s - bolted on • The height adjuster. o Early models came with brass “clover” shaped height adjusters. o These changed to cast alloy adjusters around 1970 – 71. • Mod 45 solids appeared initially with straight handlebars o In the mid 1970’s they changed to bars with a bend (kick out at the bottom) in them. • Series 1 solids had metal deflector tab screws. • No oil hole in the PTO bearing holder indicates a later model 45 or a Rover Model 45.

14 https://www.outdoorking.com/forum/ubbthreads.php?ubb=showflat&Number=55416 Accessed 29/11/2018 15 Ibid

51 Ver. 25

• In 1966 Scott Bonnar won an Export Award. After this time various machines including Models 33 and 46, had the “Export Award” sticker applied to them.

• Later Rover Model 45’s such as the Selects came with several subtle improvements such as a “U” bracket holding the bars together; the thicker, deeper steel rails and the front engine mounting bolts drop through the front rails and a washer and are fixed by a nylex nut rather than a nut welded to the underside of the rail. “Red Rover” Model 45’s do not have holes for the counterweights in the rear roller; and each roller is about 2kg heavier than a Scott Bonnar Model 45 17” roller.

Brass clover shaped height adjuster circa 1968-70. Credit Kym Schrapel, SBR Handlebars with the kick out. Credit Brendan Keir, SBR Export Award sticker as found on a Model 33. Credit Kym Schrapel, SBR Left - Scott Bonnar bars Right - Rover bars with the "U-shaped" bracket. Credit Nev Gordon, SBR

52 Ver. 25 • Occasionally seen are red clutch knobs instead of the usual black knobs. The exact reason for these is not known. It is noted that the red knobs seem to only appear on mowers with the pentagon decals and sticker ID’s dating the mowers as late 1970’s to early 1980’s (1983). Perhaps Scott Bonnar ran out of black ones during the transition of the move to Brisbane or simply because they could. • If you go to the trouble to completely tear down the rear rollers you should find PVC dirt covers inside the rear rollers. You may be lucky to find the PVC tubes with a PVC tube manufacture date stamped on them. Then again they may be blank, if so, don’t feel too bad.

Red clutch knob Credit Dave Cox, SBR Rear roller dirt sleeve with date stamp. Credit Shane Kosteszyn, SBR

53 Ver. 25 The latest and perhaps the last version of the Red Rover Model 45 had twin levers. These were unique having two levers, the drive clutch lever being on the right (rather than the left). The left lever being an engine cut-off, i.e. you need to have this engaged for the engine to start and remain running. This version is known to have been sold overseas having been seen on “Marketplace” in the U.K. SBR member Shane Kosteszyn advises having seen a parts diagram which depicted the additional lever with an “Export” notation in brackets They have been seen, albeit rarely in Australia.

Late model Rover 45 with twin levers (circled) Credit Danial Tan, SBR

For a more detailed engine listings please refer to Annex 314 and 415, obtained from Outdoorking.com (with some minor additions to Annex 2).

Other age indicators (UPDATED) In addition to the ID plates, decals and engines, there are several other indictors as to the probable age of your mower. • If you have a Model 45 twin deck which has no holes on the rear rail where the ID plate would normally be (adjacent to the clutch fork); you probably have a model that has lost its sticker ID. o That is, a 1978 -1980 vintage or a later model Rover from mid 1990s onwards if it is a red colored mower. o Mowers with sticker ID’s often appear on the rail with no holes where the metal ID’s would have been secured. However, this is not always the case, some Scott Bonnar Model 45’s and later Rover Model 45’s have been found with holes where the metal ID’s would be secured whilst having sticker ID’s. • Catcher rubber mounts o Scott Bonnar Model 45’s - welded on o Rover made Model 45’s - bolted on • The height adjuster. o Early models came with brass “clover” shaped height adjusters. o These changed to cast alloy adjusters around 1970 – 71. • Mod 45 solids appeared initially with straight handlebars o In the mid 1970’s they changed to bars with a bend (kick out at the bottom) in them. • Series 1 solids had metal deflector tab screws. • No oil hole in the PTO bearing holder indicates a later model 45 or a Rover Model 45.

14 https://www.outdoorking.com/forum/ubbthreads.php?ubb=showflat&Number=55416 Accessed 29/11/2018 15 Ibid

51 Ver. 25

• In 1966 Scott Bonnar won an Export Award. After this time various machines including Models 33 and 46, had the “Export Award” sticker applied to them.

• Later Rover Model 45’s such as the Selects came with several subtle improvements such as a “U” bracket holding the bars together; the thicker, deeper steel rails and the front engine mounting bolts drop through the front rails and a washer and are fixed by a nylex nut rather than a nut welded to the underside of the rail. “Red Rover” Model 45’s do not have holes for the counterweights in the rear roller; and each roller is about 2kg heavier than a Scott Bonnar Model 45 17” roller.

Brass clover shaped height adjuster circa 1968-70. Credit Kym Schrapel, SBR Handlebars with the kick out. Credit Brendan Keir, SBR Export Award sticker as found on a Model 33. Credit Kym Schrapel, SBR Left - Scott Bonnar bars Right - Rover bars with the "U-shaped" bracket. Credit Nev Gordon, SBR

52 Ver. 25 • Occasionally seen are red clutch knobs instead of the usual black knobs. The exact reason for these is not known. It is noted that the red knobs seem to only appear on mowers with the pentagon decals and sticker ID’s dating the mowers as late 1970’s to early 1980’s (1983). Perhaps Scott Bonnar ran out of black ones during the transition of the move to Brisbane or simply because they could. • If you go to the trouble to completely tear down the rear rollers you should find PVC dirt covers inside the rear rollers. You may be lucky to find the PVC tubes with a PVC tube manufacture date stamped on them. Then again they may be blank, if so, don’t feel too bad.

Red clutch knob Credit Dave Cox, SBR Rear roller dirt sleeve with date stamp. Credit Shane Kosteszyn, SBR

53 Ver. 25 The latest and perhaps the last version of the Red Rover Model 45 had twin levers. These were unique having two levers, the drive clutch lever being on the right (rather than the left). The left lever being an engine cut-off, i.e. you need to have this engaged for the engine to start and remain running. This version is known to have been sold overseas having been seen on “Marketplace” in the U.K. SBR member Shane Kosteszyn advises having seen a parts diagram which depicted the additional lever with an “Export” notation in brackets They have been seen, albeit rarely in Australia.

Late model Rover 45 with twin levers (circled) Credit Danial Tan, SBR

sections.title: CHAPTER 3 – BUILD OPTIONS
sections.text:
54 Ver. 25

CHAPTER 3 – BUILD OPTIONS

sections.title: Build 1 – The freshen up
sections.text:
55 Ver. 25 There are two standards for a rebuild: • Restoration i.e., back to the original and as close as possible to maintaining all historical aspects of the mower; and • Renovation or a custom build, e.g., custom or non-original colors and nonstandard engines. Therefore, strictly speaking adding a new motor or a nonstandard e.g., blue hammertone finish is a renovation. But really, who cares? I am not losing sleep over restoration versus renovation, more importantly a piece of the past is being kept alive. Let us just say it is a rebuilt mower and move on to the next mower project. The following has been compiled by Glenn Russell and Travis Hines as a guide to what you may care to do to your mower. Obviously if parts are damaged/worn and need replacing you will have to replace them. Parts mentioned like the motor are your choice. Original or new replacement Honda, Briggs and Stratton, Robin or the variously branded “Chonda” cheaper Chinese copies, it is up to you. Build 1 – The freshen up Basic parts replacement. You have a good solid mower but just want to touch it up a bit. Oil what you need to Give your engine its regular service Lube chains Adjust chains New clutch cork and drive cork – contact adhesive for corks. New thrust pad Sharpen reel/bed knife New reel bearings Adjust cutting settings Replace other parts as required.

Build 2 – The new look You have a good solid mower that you want to give a refresh. Sharpen reel/bed knife new service parts, motor still good just want it looking new New paint Strip whole mower down Paint strip/sand blast all painted parts Brace frame if its twin rail Ranex/ citric acid to treat rust Steel Primer Paint for frame Paint for reel

Paint for motor Paint for handlebars Paint for rear roller Give your engine its regular service Adjust chains Lube chains New thrust pad New clutch cork and drive cork Kwik grip for corks New front roller caps New grips New reel bearings New nuts and bolts (refer Chapter 9 for listings) New decals New red caps

sections.title: Build 3 – The almost new
sections.text:
56 Ver. 25 Build 3 – The almost new You have a good solid mower that you want to give a thorough refresh. Strip whole mower down Paint strip/sand blast all painted parts New paint Check for cracks – repair/brace as required Ranex/ citric acid to treat rust Steel Primer Paint for frame Paint for reel (if required) Paint for handlebars (if not re-chrome) Paint for rear roller New 3.5hp Briggs & Stratton (or whatever) motor New chains Lube chains New thrust pad and washer New clutch cork and drive cork Kwik grip for corks New front roller caps or a new front roller

New grips New reel bearings New rear roller bearings New clutch bearing New drive bearing New clutch shaft bearing New drive cable New decals New cotter pin New red caps New nuts and bolts New reel New bed knife Reel and bedknife sharpened Full adjustment/tune of Clutch, chains, reel to bedknife

Build 4 – The all new Basically, the same list as above but buy everything you can brand new e.g., Sprockets, deflector, etc. Not many people go this extent due to the costs. However, if you are at this point you should know a fair amount about the mower. Build costs It is not possible to advise on the exact cost of any of the above build options. There is no such thing as a "Restore Kit" per se, it depends on the extent that you want to go, from just a freshen up to a total replacement of parts for the “all new”. Costing also depends on what you intend to replace, outsource or do yourself. Experienced restorers from the SBR page indicate costs in the mid-$2000 range are easily spent on a restoration16. Their figures if anything, are conservative. If you were to totally rebuild a model 45, (irrespective of size) with available new parts the cost could be as high as between $4500 - $5000. To add perspective, a model 45 in 1982 cost about $500 new or in current terms about $1700. Comparable mowers nowadays, such as the Superswift cost from $1600; Proteas range from $2400 (14") to $3200 (20"). A Masport will set you back from $2000 and the Swardman is around $3000. See Annex 4 for an indicative price list of parts and services.

16 https://www.facebook.com/groups/1207116562722291/announcements/ accessed 8 April 2020

sections.title: CHAPTER 4 – TOOL LIST
sections.text:
57 Ver. 25

CHAPTER 4 – TOOL LIST

sections.title: Basic Tools
sections.text:
58 Ver. 25 Before you start on a tear down and rebuild there are certain tools and consumables that you will need. There are also tools that are “nice to have” which will make life easier. Basic Tools • Degreaser • Multi use penetrating/water dispersant e.g., WD40, Inox, Loctite Freeze and Release, RP7, CRC or whatever • Oil can (general maintenance lubrication) • Lithium based grease or chain lubricant • Imperial and metric socket and spanner (open end and ring) sets o whilst many Scott Bonnars were made pre-metric, you may find nuts/bolts have been replaced over the years • Multi grips or vice grips • Adjustable spanner • 3 jaw gear puller o 3” will normally suffice o 4” is more suited for clutch half removal o A gear puller is a “MUST HAVE” for the novice. • Hammer • Rubber mallet • Side cutters (remove stuck wellington keys) • Screwdrivers: o 2x large flat blade o 1 x Phillips head o 1 x small flat blade (remove wellington keys) Nice to have tools • A bearing puller (different to a gear puller) o More suited to removal of the Mod 40 rubber friction drive, Mod 45 rear bearings and intermediate sprocket bearing. • Dead blow hammer • Manual impact driver (removal of blade) • Oxy/acetylene or propane gas (removal of blade) • Nut spanner (for cutter adjustment) • Circlip pliers (chain joining link) • Bench vice • Panel beating kit with dolly and hammer (catcher and chain cover repair). • Spin spanners o Specifically for Model 40, 430 amd 590 Nut Spanner Gear puller Credit Reece Abbott, SBR Bearing puller Credit Stewart Wallace, SBR

59 Ver. 25 • MIG welder for rail repairs. A small machine will do your basic repairs, thinner wire is better for thinner material. 0.6mm will be good for up to 5mm thick steel.

You now should have some idea of what mower you have, its age and hopefully some idea of the extent of the build you want to embark on. Before the tear down starts there is one final thing to remember: Reel mowers are machines, they are logical in the way they work. The most important tool to use is your brain. The below picture of a sticker an SBR member has on his mower is appropriate. If all else fails – read the instructions.

Funny but true sticker, Credit Danial John Falcocchio, SBR

sections.title: CHAPTER 5 – BEFORE YOU START
sections.text:
60 Ver. 25

CHAPTER 5 – BEFORE YOU START

sections.title: Before you start
sections.text:
61 Ver. 25 Before you start Before you start the tear down there are several things to do: • Take the time to study the parts diagram. These are invaluable and will inform you how the mower is assembled. Parts diagrams are at Annex 1. • Get an assortment of containers or clip seal bags to put nuts, bolts, bearings and any other small part that may get misplaced. Unless you are extremely neat, tidy and organized, invariably that one little piece (e.g., circlip or bushing etc.) will disappear into an alternate dimension, not to be found until you have obtained a replacement, delaying the rebuild. • Irrespective of whether you intend to replace nuts, bolts or bearings with new ones, it is advisable to keep the old ones. You never know when you may need a nut, bolt or washer. If you have not got one then now is the time to start a collection of nuts, bolts, washers and assorted bits and pieces. Do not throw out old bearings, they come in handy e.g., loosely put reel bearings in the bearing holder then you can spray the holder without the need to mask up. • Beware, certain parts (especially those made of cast iron) need to be treated with care during disassembly as they are not currently available either new or OEM parts. If you break/damage them you will have to source secondhand parts. The parts you should be careful with are detailed below. Parts not available either new or OEM. Model No Parts only available second hand (effective 21/09/2021) 33 • Reel bearing holders • Pawls • All pulleys and sprockets • Complete drive clutch assembly • Rear bearing holders. • Clutch operating handle • Drum shaft collars • Inter. Sprocket bracket assembly • Sole plate • Chain cover • Height adjuster brackets 38 • All parts except o Side cover, Bearings, blade holder and blades 40 • Reel bearing holders • Complete primary clutch i.e. Rubber friction drive and drum assembly • All sprockets • Rear bearing holders. • Sole plate • Rear rollers including the shaft. • Chain cover • Drive clutch thrust bearing • Height adjuster brackets • Clutch cable

62 Ver. 25 Model No Parts only available second hand (effective 21/09/2021) 45 • Rear rollers • Ratchet boxes • Pawls • Pawl holder • Drum shaft collar • Inter. Sprocket bracket assembly • Sole plate • Unicorn Reel bearing holders 430 and 590 • Height adjuster assembly • PTO shaft • All pulleys • All bearing holders • Sole plate • Rear roller assembly including shaft and ratchet boxes. • Clutch assembly including clutch cable (Mod 430) • Drive pinion Parts in red are those that particular care should be taken with as they are prone to being found broken, badly worn or are susceptible to being broken during disassembly. Secondhand parts for Models 33, 38, 40, 430 and 590 are not as readily available as those for Model 45. You have been warned.

sections.title: CHAPTER 6 – THE TEAR DOWN
sections.text:
63 Ver. 25

CHAPTER 6 – THE TEAR DOWN

sections.title: How do I tear down a mower?
sections.text:
64 Ver. 25 How do I tear down a mower? The following is a basic guide on how to tear down a Model 45. It is not intended to be detailed, rather it is a “101” list of steps. This guide may be applied to other models in general terms. If you are about to attempt your first tear down, it is recommended that you take the time to view the videos prepared by Glenn Russell, Travis Hines or Love Larn for more detailed explanations. The saying “a picture is worth a thousand words” applies to the tear down, especially for the novice. The videos may be found in the Videos section at Annex 3 which contains a compilation of useful video links or do a search for “videos Travis” or “videos Glenn”. Searching on You Tube is also advisable for the videos by Travis, “Love Larn” and “junkyarddog87”; all have some particularly good Scott Bonnar related how to videos. Please also refer to the parts diagrams available in the Files section of the SBR Facebook page. • Remove chain cover and CRACK THE NUTS (the 3 sprocket nuts) • Remove the clutch cable where it connects at the side plate, disconnect throttle cables from the engine and remove the handlebars from the side plates. The clutch and throttle cables will be attached to the handlebars and can be fully stripped down later. • Remove chains by removing the circlips on the joining links. • Check that the clutch runs true i.e. does not wobble. o Make a mark on the cone and rotate the engine half slowly and check that it rotates truly. Alternatively remove the spark plug, pull the starter repeatedly and while the engine is turning over look at the clutch housing spinning to see if the edge of the housing is spinning straight. If it is not, you will have to replace the clutch housing as it will be warped. • Remove the engine and clutch. May have to undo the three bolts securing the outer clutch half to the inner clutch half. Check the condition of keyways and keys o remove the clutch, take the key out of the clutch body and slide the clutch body back on the engine shaft. If that clutch body is “rocking” it is worn and probably will need replacing. • Remove grass deflector securing screws • Remove the sprockets. Check the condition of keyways and keys. • Cutter and PTO sprockets. May need a bearing puller. Do not lose the wellington keys (they may be stuck in the keyway – refer later in this document). • Remove the large inter sprocket which comes off by removing the two bolts holding the inter adjustment bracket. • The inner sprocket assembly with the clutch lining comes off. The cork clutch lining may be scraped off and replaced later. • Remove the drum sprocket again a gear puller will probably be required. • Remove the clutch fork assembly. • Remove PTO/engine shaft bearing housing from the side plate and remove the PTO/engine shaft. Check the condition of keyways and keys. • Remove either the twin rails or solid deck. o If solid deck, then the bracket holding the clutch fork assembly can then be removed. • Remove the reel by removing the bolts on the cutter bearing housings (2 bolts per housing). This will allow the top halves of the bearing housing to be lifted off and the reel to be lifted out. Note

65 Ver. 25 the drive or chain side bearing holder has a distinctive piece where the bearing sits. Check the condition of the keyways and key. • Remove the bottom halves of both cutter bearing holders o Will probably require you to loosen off the cutter bearing adjustment screws on both sides. • Remove bed knife (2 bolts each side) • Remove height adjuster (described later in this document) • Remove front roller and front roller bracket assembly. • Remove rear roller. • Remove bearing holder and bearings and separate. Note the sprocket side holder has a spacer in the holder and the non-drive side holder has two slotted bolt holes and one round bolt hole • Remove drum shaft collars • Separate the rear roller halves and remove the drum shaft • Do not lose the pawls which may come out of the pawl holder. o Note the dimple on the pawls goes inwards and they are not to be greased when reassembled. • Remove the ratchet boxes from the rollers by removing the three bolts. Be careful they do not break. How to remove these is described later in this document. • You may now fully clean all parts, replace worn parts etc. and reassemble. If you are new to Scott Bonnar reel mowers it is advisable that during the tear down process you take numerous pictures. The pictures should include both wide angle views of e.g., the chain set up through to close ups. Pictures of throttle springs and linkages are advisable. The pictures will prove invaluable come time to reassemble the mower. Below are some examples of pictures I have taken as a reference tool. Reassembly is basically the reverse order of the tear down Left - Model 45, solid Top Right Model 33 Right bottom Briggs & Stratton throttle spring Credit Kym Schrapel, SBR

66 Ver. 25

There have been posts on the Buy/Sell page, Marketplace and Gumtree where mowers are offered for sale because the owner “does not have time to do a restoration”. Please, persist with the tear down, restoration and the rebuild, it will be worth it. SBR member Glen Richardson posted the following: “A few years ago, I stripped down a motor bike (Honda CB750) stripped the engine down too. Had parts all over the garage and never thought I'd get it back together. A guy from work suggested I put two parts on per day... which I did and within a month it was done.”

Kirby springs/linkages Credit Kym Schrapel, SBR Solid Deck Clutch Cable Credit Kym Schrapel, SBR

sections.title: Occupational Health & Safety (OH&S) issues
sections.text:
67 Ver. 25 Occupational Health & Safety (OH&S) issues Just because you are working from home does not excuse you from considering OH&S issues. Before you start to tear down and move to the restoration of your mower consider the following. Lead Paint Around 1980 lead in paints started to be phased out in Australia due to government legislation. It is therefore likely the paint on the Scott Bonnars contained lead. For those not sandblasting but stripping themselves, steps to minimise the ingestion of lead should be taken. If not for you, think of your loved ones. Asbestos Asbestos has not been used in domestic building materials since the 1980’s but it was not until December 2003 that asbestos and all products containing asbestos were banned throughout Australia. Like lead paint, asbestos may be encountered, particularly in the Supercut and Queen clutches. Older engine gaskets have also been found to contain asbestos. Please refer to the Department of Health (www.health.gov.au) for information on handling asbestos and see their Fact Sheet17. OH&S In whatever you do, use the appropriate Personal Protective Equipment (PPE). The following is a guide as what you should consider for your safety. • Eye protection o Especially if you use a wire wheel or flap disc to remove paint. • Hand protection – gloves o If you use paint stripper, heavy rubber gloves to protect against caustic paint stripper o General work gloves to protect against oil and grease and the inevitable nick/cuts • Hearing protection o Wear hearing protection if you are using e.g., angle grinder with flap disc to strip rust. • Masks – filters or respirators o To be worn whilst painting and sanding o To be worn if the presence of asbestos is likely ▪ Supercut clutches ▪ Engine gaskets o To be worn if the presence of lead is likely ▪ Paint removal o If you are sand blasting. • Clothing o When using power equipment ensure you have no loose clothing which may get entangled in e.g., flap discs

17 https://www.health.gov.au/internet/publications/publishing.nsf/Content/CA2578620005D57ACA2579FB0008A15F /$File/asbestos-feb13.pdf accessed 11 July 2019

68 Ver. 25

• Spray painting o Work in a ventilated area. o Wear a mask/respirator • Flammable substances o Keep all combustible items away from spark-producing activities. All flammable articles must be away from any welding. Paint can be damaged by sparks from a grinder so keep these away from spark-producing equipment as well. o If welding or grinding - have a fire extinguisher accessible and close by. • Lifting heavy weights – watch your back o Remember that at a minimum the mower you are to work on weighs at least 65 kgs (17” Mod 45). If you are to put it on a bench to work on, either: ▪ Get help ▪ Remember to bend the knees not the back • Waste disposal o Unused paint, old oils etc. should be disposed of properly. Refer to waste disposal guides from your local Council. o Consider where any run-off from degreasing/hosing down your mower goes. o As mentioned above – take care when handling and disposing of asbestos and lead containing materials.

Be safe in whatever you are doing. Be aware of the likelihood of old paint containing lead and that Supercuts/Queens and old engine gaskets may contain asbestos. Read and follow directions, material data sheets and use/wear the appropriate PPE.

sections.title: Crack The Nuts
sections.text:
69 Ver. 25 Crack The Nuts The first thing to do in a tear down is “CRACK THE NUTS”. Before attempting to crack nuts it is advisable to give them a good spray of Inox, WD40 (or whatever you choose to use) and even leave overnight. Then, with the chains on, crack the top nut before anything else. With the reel and chains on, jam the reel with a piece of wood and crack the nut. You may need an extension bar for extra leverage on your socket; or a rattle gun also works if you have one handy. Crack all the nuts under the chain cover, these are a MUST. It is advisable to “crack” ALL the nuts on the mower. I forgot to “crack the nuts” “Yep I forgot to crack the nut, is there a recommended way to remove the nut on the engine sprocket, PTO shaft?” Yes, it does happen, but all is not lost. The following are three options: • Longest way: re-install the soleplate, Reel and cutter sprocket and fit the chain and crack the nut as described above. • Shorter way is going to probably mark the PTO shaft - tightly grip the PTO shaft with vice grips then try to undo the nut on the sprocket. Placing a rag around the shaft may save too much of a gouge mark from the vice grips. This option may see the shaft slip if the nut is on tight. • Another option is put the chain over the PTO sprocket then a rag and use vice grips to hold/jam it.

• Since you have taken the chain off, place it around the sprocket and thread both ends of the chain through a large ring spanner to fashion a chain wrench in a similar manner as shown in the opposite picture.

"I forgot to crack the nut". Credit (hidden) to save embarrassment, SBR "Chain wrench" Credit https://www.facebook.com/groups/373701903005705/permalink/908965389479351/

sections.title: How do I remove …?
sections.text:
70 Ver. 25 How do I remove …? The reel sprocket Ensure you have “cracked all nuts”. On Model 45’s, use a gear puller to remove the reel or cutter sprocket. If it is hard to remove you can leave the puller on, under tension , overnight .The next morning hopefully it as released itself with that constant overnight pressure. You can also try giving the puller bolt a slight tap once you have the tension on it Alternatively, carefully lever it off if you do not have a gear puller. One suggested method is using a large screwdriver and a hammer and metal files. Slide one file straight down between the sprocket and the frame. Then slide the screwdriver between the sprocket and the file. .Hitting the large screwdriver downwards you should be able to hear when the sprocket lets go. Remember to inspect the keyway. The cutter sprocket from a “Unicorn”, 20 “ model 45 is about 5 mm shorter than the 14 or 17” version as shown in the above picture. Note: Model 40 reel sprockets are screwed on – do not attempt to remove with a gear puller.

Model 45 Reel Sprocket, Credit Unknown, SBR Difference between sprockets - 20” sprocket on the left 17/14” on the right. Credit Nic Williams, SBR
