sections.title: The top sprocket?
sections.text:
71 Ver. 25

The top sprocket?

With nut and chains off use a gear puller to remove the sprocket. Remember not to lose the Woodroffe key and to inspect the keyway.

The Clutch bearing out of my clutch half? There have been several methods provided: • the gravity bash or “Bearsby method” is a simple method using inertia and a good “gravity bash”. Open a vice and firmly tap the clutch half down on the vice and the bearing will drop out. If you do not have a vice, then two blocks of wood placed apart can be used. o If this fails, it is advisable not to keep bashing harder and harder, you will probably end up with a broken clutch half. There are alternatives. • For alloy halves - heat the outer circle - not the bearing, it will fall out. Don’t heat it till its glowing, just get it hot to get it back in, put the bearing in ice for about 1 hour it will contract, heat the inner circle it will expand, take the bearing out of the ice it will drop straight in let it cool, and you are good. o Note – it is not advisable to heat cast clutch halves but use the next suggested method. • working from the engine shaft side, use a long punch (or similar) and lightly tap working around the bearing until it is out, some are much more stubborn than others but The Brock ”Bearsby method”

sections.title: The Clutch bearing out of my clutch half?
sections.text:
71 Ver. 25

The top sprocket?

With nut and chains off use a gear puller to remove the sprocket. Remember not to lose the Woodroffe key and to inspect the keyway.

The Clutch bearing out of my clutch half? There have been several methods provided: • the gravity bash or “Bearsby method” is a simple method using inertia and a good “gravity bash”. Open a vice and firmly tap the clutch half down on the vice and the bearing will drop out. If you do not have a vice, then two blocks of wood placed apart can be used. o If this fails, it is advisable not to keep bashing harder and harder, you will probably end up with a broken clutch half. There are alternatives. • For alloy halves - heat the outer circle - not the bearing, it will fall out. Don’t heat it till its glowing, just get it hot to get it back in, put the bearing in ice for about 1 hour it will contract, heat the inner circle it will expand, take the bearing out of the ice it will drop straight in let it cool, and you are good. o Note – it is not advisable to heat cast clutch halves but use the next suggested method. • working from the engine shaft side, use a long punch (or similar) and lightly tap working around the bearing until it is out, some are much more stubborn than others but The Brock ”Bearsby method”

sections.title: The outer bearing race from the old self aligning bearing from the housing?
sections.text:
72 Ver. 25 light taps and rotating works. See the video produced by Glenn Russell18. • A variation to the above Glenn Russell method has been provided by SBR member John Beadsy. If you don’t have a bearing puller/extractor you can manually tap it out. Lay the clutch housing with the bearing down on a vice. Find a cup head bolt with a head that will fit the centre hole of bearing. Once the head is past the bearing move it across so the head of the bolt grips the edge of the bearing. Using a punch tap the bolt head from above moving it from side to side. May need a big tap but it will come out. When it is re-installed/replaced (use a bearing press or a vice and a socket); the rounded edge sits on the inside of the cup, and they are supposed to go down past flush by about 4mm. The Clutch bearing is also known as the thrust race is an “underground bearing” and is not available from bearing suppliers – it is available from the usual SBR parts suppliers. Remember to inspect the keyway.

The outer bearing race from the old self aligning bearing from the housing?

Occasionally the self-aligning bearing in the PTO housing will fall apart and the outer race will become stuck. The following are suggested removal options: • with a welder, run a bead inside the steel race at around - as it cools it will shrink the cage and it will come out. • Die grinder • Tap around the edge of the bearings outer race with a hammer and punch until it loosens and comes out • Use gravity as in the “Bearsby method”. Tap it a few times on a piece of wood and it should come out . A little bit of spray will not hurt either. • Heat the bearing housing (e.g., butane or oxy), then it should fall out or give it a tap to help.

18https://www.facebook.com/glenn.russell.127/videos/pcb.2333370170252225/10157305016239647/?type=3&th eater&ifg=1) Accessed 25 August 2019 Stuck outer race of self-aligning bearing. Credit Matt Townsend, SBR Tapping the thrust race from the engine side Credit Glenn Russell, SBR

sections.title: The clutch off the shaft?
sections.text:
73 Ver. 25

The clutch off the shaft? Before you attempt to remove the clutch it is recommended that you take the time to examine it whilst still connected to the engine and PTO shaft. Take time to check that the engine side clutch half is not warped, and it does not “wobble”. Make a mark on the cone and rotate the engine half slowly and check that it rotates truly. Alternatively remove the spark plug, pull the starter repeatedly and while the engine is turning over look at the clutch housing spinning to see if the edge of the housing is spinning straight. If it is not , replace the clutch housing as it will be warped. Please see the short video by Jason Mihatsch at: https://www.facebook.com/jason.mihatsch/videos/10156969478551225/?t=0. Whilst you are the tear down stage also check: • the cone for wear in the keyway • check the engine shaft condition • condition of the clutch thrust bearing If you do have a warped or wobble with the clutch you may be up for a new engine clutch half. Please also refer to “My clutch does not run true, it wobbles” in Chapter 13. If you have a clutch half with a cotter pin, it is worthwhile understanding how the cotter pin is fitted and holds the clutch on the shaft refer to the below diagram and pictures.

Clutch half with cotter pin and motor Credit SBR How the cotter pin holds the clutch onto the output shaft. Credit Michael Hanes, SBR

74 Ver. 25

You are now ready to remove the clutch from the engine. In the example below with a cotter pin, the order of operation is to remove the engine from the machine, then remove the clutch off the motor and then remove the cotter pin. Referring to the below collage of pictures:

1. Loosen the cotter pin - do not take the cotter pin nut off the thread (otherwise you may damage the thread and be up for a new cotter pin)

2. Tap the cotter pin (nut) down to break the seal so to speak. Wriggle it to make sure it is free. It will not fall out or through the hole (because of the shape of the cotter pin) until the clutch is off the shaft.

3. The clutch should now pry off using a jimmy bar, tyre lever or large screwdriver. It will come off, just be patient.

4. If the cotter pin is loose in the hole the clutch should pry off. If it is a struggle, undo the 3 screws and split the clutch. Then apply Inox, WD40 or whatever, then use a gear puller to remove the clutch half. If you have the clutch with the 2 bolts, simply loosen them and remove as per the above. For those unsure as to how the cotter does its magic inside the clutch housing, hopefully this gives a better understanding on how to remove the clutch. The collage pic shows a worn and new cotter as examples. Credit Georgebonmods, SBR

75 Ver. 25

TIP: Thanks to SBR member Luke Duke for this tip19. Should you find that when using the gear puller, you run out of thread simply insert a small socket (e.g., ¼’) or a 3/8” small extension from a socket set into the centre of the clutch. (The square hole helps align the puller bolt and helps keep it all straight). Then insert the centre screw into the centre of the clutch, screw it out and remove the clutch. Remember to inspect the keyway.

19 https://www.facebook.com/groups/1801521490103765/permalink/2556261997963040/?hc_location=ufi Take care not to damage the crank case. Put the hammer away, you will just cause damage to the clutch and shaft. The shaft may be damaged, or the key is broken, and/or it is jammed. Clutch removal. Credit Luke Duke, SBR Fitting a gear puller to remove the clutch. Credit Nev Gordon, SBR Clutch removal steps Credit VNR Mechanical, SBR

sections.title: Grub Screw versus Cotter Pin
sections.text:
76 Ver. 25

Once the clutch has been removed it is advisable to take the cotter pin and key out of the clutch body and slide the clutch body back on the engine output shaft. If that clutch body is “rocking” it is no good and will need replacing. Also check the hole for the cotter pin is clean, and that the cotter pin is snug and not loose in the hole.

Grub Screw versus Cotter Pin “Which is better, a cotter pin or grub screws?” Grub screws tend to damage the shaft to lock the clutch half in place. The cotter pin offers greater clamping force with less shaft damage, and you can keep cotter pins tight with a spring washer compared to grub screws. George SB-FabandParts speculates that Scott Bonnar or Rover changed to grubs to cut costs, both in the manufacturing of the clutch bodies and to delete parts like the cotter pin. Grub screws are an off the shelf item and are cheap compared to the cotter pin. The cotter pin is the best option and making sure it is always tight will save in the long run. Corks “What is the best way to take off or remove the old cork from the clutches?” Simply run a long Stanley knife blade under the cork to get most of the cork off. Then with a paint scraper or even a chisel, scrape the rest. Using a fine wire wheel on a drill helps to clean up the metal surface. If you do not have a fine wire wheel, try sandpaper. Finish off with solvent (e.g., methylated spirits) to clean the surface prior to gluing the new cork

Twin grub screws on a clutch half Credit Ash Woj, SBR.

sections.title: Corks
sections.text:
76 Ver. 25

Twin grub screws on a clutch half Credit Ash Woj, SBR.

sections.title: A stuck Woodruff Key?
sections.text:
77 Ver. 25 A stuck Woodruff Key? This is one of the most encountered problems. The following are suggested removal methods: • A pair of side cutters or wire pincers down low and lever out. • With a cold chisel and hammer on a slight angle. • Spray some Inox or similar and put a set of vice grips on it and wiggle. • Use a little precision-sized flat head screwdriver. Angle it in lengthways & give a couple of taps with a hammer to wedge it under the keyway. • Like the above, use a centre punch or pin punch on the top of the key at one end and firmly tap down. The key will “rock”. Once the opposite end tilts up use a chisel or similar and tap it out the rest of the way. • Drilling a few small holes along the middle of the key being careful not to drill too deep, basically just material removal to collapse the sides in. • Grab the keyway, in a good vice. Simply turn the shaft so the keyway is on the bottom with the shaft laying horizontal. The keyway should be at the end of the vice, with the shaft over hanging the end of the vice, make sure the vice is done up VERY tight on the keyway, grab the shaft with you hand and jiggle gently up and down. The bed knife? Of all the questions, this is the one that seems to cause people the most problems. Removing the screws can be difficult to say the least., It is highly recommended that you soak the blade with your preferred product e.g., WD40, Inox, Breakaway or even 50:50 combination of Mineral Turpentine with Auto Transmission Fluid for several days. • Manual impact driver (shown opposite) 2-3 hits on each screw and they hopefully are cracked. • Weld a nut (5/16 has been suggested) on the screw, undo with a ring spanner. Most quality bed knifes come with new screws. • Application of heat – blow torch (propane torches are not hot enough) followed by the impact driver. • Use a sharp punch to spin it - hit the corner of the screw on an angle with the pin punch. • Drill the screw out and if necessary, re-tap the thread. Drill out to maximum of 3/16” and 5/16 UNC tap. • Cheat - take it with the reel and get a professional to replace and grind the blade and reel at the same time.

Seized woodruff key Bedknife and impact driver

sections.title: The bed knife?
sections.text:
77 Ver. 25 A stuck Woodruff Key? This is one of the most encountered problems. The following are suggested removal methods: • A pair of side cutters or wire pincers down low and lever out. • With a cold chisel and hammer on a slight angle. • Spray some Inox or similar and put a set of vice grips on it and wiggle. • Use a little precision-sized flat head screwdriver. Angle it in lengthways & give a couple of taps with a hammer to wedge it under the keyway. • Like the above, use a centre punch or pin punch on the top of the key at one end and firmly tap down. The key will “rock”. Once the opposite end tilts up use a chisel or similar and tap it out the rest of the way. • Drilling a few small holes along the middle of the key being careful not to drill too deep, basically just material removal to collapse the sides in. • Grab the keyway, in a good vice. Simply turn the shaft so the keyway is on the bottom with the shaft laying horizontal. The keyway should be at the end of the vice, with the shaft over hanging the end of the vice, make sure the vice is done up VERY tight on the keyway, grab the shaft with you hand and jiggle gently up and down. The bed knife? Of all the questions, this is the one that seems to cause people the most problems. Removing the screws can be difficult to say the least., It is highly recommended that you soak the blade with your preferred product e.g., WD40, Inox, Breakaway or even 50:50 combination of Mineral Turpentine with Auto Transmission Fluid for several days. • Manual impact driver (shown opposite) 2-3 hits on each screw and they hopefully are cracked. • Weld a nut (5/16 has been suggested) on the screw, undo with a ring spanner. Most quality bed knifes come with new screws. • Application of heat – blow torch (propane torches are not hot enough) followed by the impact driver. • Use a sharp punch to spin it - hit the corner of the screw on an angle with the pin punch. • Drill the screw out and if necessary, re-tap the thread. Drill out to maximum of 3/16” and 5/16 UNC tap. • Cheat - take it with the reel and get a professional to replace and grind the blade and reel at the same time.

Seized woodruff key Bedknife and impact driver

sections.title: The “Medallion” off the handlebar cover plate?
sections.text:
78 Ver. 25

The “Medallion” off the handlebar cover plate? The label or medallion on the cover plate was originally a screen printed brass plate glued to the cover plate. It must be removed before cleanup of the cover plate. Removal methods are: • Lift from the edge with a knife blade, paint scraper or putty knife and work around until it comes off. • Use dental floss or fine fishing line. And just pull through. If it does not go through, heat it with a heat gun or hairdryer. Note: the medallion is only screen printed so any polishing may cause irreparable damage. Clean up with soapy water is about the simplest method available. The front roller shaft out of the bracket assembly? The front roller shaft simply slides out of the bracket assembly. Some shafts will be corroded with the rust hindering the removal. Clean up the shaft by sanding, wire brush or flap disk and it should come off.

Bedknife removal - Nut welded onto screw Credit Shane Kosteszyn SBR Cover plate medallion Credit Gregory Smith, SBR Front roller shaft Credit Kym Ess, SBR Bedknife screws after removal by a punch Credit Kym Schrapel SBR

sections.title: Catcher hanger pin?
sections.text:
79 Ver. 25 Catcher hanger pin? With either a grinding disc or flap wheel grind the weld (on the outside of the side plate) and then knock the worn pin out.

Worn Catcher hanger pin Credit Michael Nietz, SBR Pulley for bolt on edger attachment? Not commonly found these days but the question has been asked “Does anybody know how to remove the pulley attachment from the actual mower? The hole between the chain case and the pulley needs to be undone and replaced with a nut on in its place. If it is on tight, place a piece of wood between the reel and bed knife and then with a large screwdriver or rod placed into the hole undo it. The thread is normal (i.e., anticlockwise)

Pulley for edger attachment, Credit Jordan Wall, SBR. Insert a rod or large screwdriver in this hole Ground weld on a Catcher hanger pin. Credit Adrian Greener, SBR

sections.title: Intermediate bracket assembly bearing
sections.text:
80 Ver. 25 Intermediate bracket assembly bearing Removal of the small intermediate sprocket assembly and bearing from the intermediate bracket can be as simple as pulling the sprocket assembly off the bearing and shaft with little effort required. Unfortunately, some will put up a fight and will be difficult to disassemble. In most instances the sprocket will easily come off leaving the bearing on the shaft. Be careful using a gear puller on it as you can warp it. Just give it light taps and it will come loose.

The options are: • With it still on the mower use several large screwdrivers (or pry bars) to gently pry it off • If the sprocket is still on the bearing, try a gear puller. However be warned the gear puller jaws may be too thick to fit underneath the bearing. • Alternative to a gear puller is to use a bearing puller. This is the more appropriate tool to use. • Remove it from the mower turn over and tap on the outside ring(outside to the cork) it will come off the bearing with a couple of hits. • Remove from the mower and place it in a vice so the shaft faces upwards. Slip two pieces of 3mm angle iron (as it doesn't bend) under the bearing. Just open the vice enough for the piece of 2" flat steel to sit in Intermediate bracket assembly and sprocket. Credit Dion Jefford, SBR. Intermediate sprocket assembly taken apart. Credit Harry Hoff, SBR.

sections.title: Handlebar bolt
sections.text:
81 Ver. 25 there loosely so then the angle iron sits across the vice jaws . A few taps on a piece of wood over the end of shaft and hopefully it comes off easily . • Use a spanner as in the below picture to pry the bearing. A few taps with a hammer will help. • Worst case scenario is to use a bearing splitter or carefully cut the bearing case in two places with an angle grinder, then squash the bearing in a vice to break the bearing case. Installation is simply getting the correct size socket or tube spanner for the INNER race of the bearing and tapping it back on.

Handlebar bolt Normally separating the top/bottom sections of the handlebar is a simple task, however at times the bolt will be seized and hard to remove. Suggested removal methods include: • Liberal application of e.g., WD40 etc. and let soak. • Application of heat • Tap it out with a nut on (so the thread is not damaged). • With a smaller diameter bolt or punch tap it through • If it is a bolt simply turn the head whilst tapping it should let it break free • If it is threaded both ends, simply keep tightening one side and it may lever itself out.

Seized Handlebar bolt Credit Mario Cavar, SBR Intermediate sprocket bearing removal off the bracket using a spanner Credit Christian Baird, SBR

sections.title: Handle grips
sections.text:
82 Ver. 25 Handle grips Because the old, genuine grips are often hard they can be difficult to remove. The new, aftermarket grips which have a snug fit can also be problematic. Irrespective of whether the grips are old or new the same applies. Again, there are several alternatives: • Place in hot soapy water for 30 seconds to soften the grips. o Wrap the grip in a towel to avoid burning your hand and slide the grip off. • Use dishwashing liquid. • Get in with a small flat head screwdriver and spray some WD40. • Air compressor. Lift one side with screwdriver and insert air nozzle. ID Plates Removal of the metal (brass or alloy) ID Plates is simply a matter of drilling out the pop rivets with a 1/8’ drill bit. The clamp (pivot) bolt from the Cutter Bearing Housing

During a tear down you may find the clamp (pivot) bolt is seized in the cutter bearing housing. It is advisable NOT to try and force the bolt, the risk is you will break the housing. Soak the bolt with penetrating fluid (e.g. Inox or whatever you use) overnight. After a good soaking: • put a nut back on the thread and put the housing in a vice. Place the housing on the vice with the bolt able to come out below and punch the bolt back down with the housing fully supported so the bolt can come free. Have the vice jaw just wider than the head of the bolt and drive out. • If you don't have a vice you could placer a larger socket over the head of the bolt place it on a hard surface and the same drive it backwards into the socket. Handlebar grips Credit Tim Hugo, SBR Above left and right 0 seized clamp (pivot) bolt in the cutter bearing housing. Credit Yusuf Osman, SBR.

sections.title: How do I take the height adjuster apart?
sections.text:
83 Ver. 25 • Alternatively, use a small i.e. 3” gear puller, just use 2 jaws. Gently ease it out - like the vice method.

How do I take the height adjuster apart? Or as was asked “has anyone got a diagram of how the height adjustment goes together, I went to adjust it and the spring and circlip comes off?” The height adjuster does not simply screw apart. If the plastic dust cover (24) is missing the threads are likely to be full of dirt etc., a good dose of Inox, WD40 or whatever you prefer is advisable. As per the parts diagram below there is a circlip (26) below the spring (25). You need to get that off. Then the spring and washer (34) will fall down the shaft. Then you can unscrew the adjuster.

If it is really stuck you can gently heat the lower section of the adjuster with a butane torch and the aluminum will grow and let go of the steel shaft. Once you have the height adjuster apart sooner or later you will want to put it back together, refer to Chapter 9. Decals or stickers If you particularly have the sticker ID or Rover ID stickers which are not available aftermarket, you may want to attempt to remove them. The following tips have been provided for the removal of any decal/sticker. • Clean the decal or sticker and the area surrounding it with soapy water and a cleaning cloth • Use a hair dryer to warm the decal and gently remove/lift it then save it onto a sheet of hard plastic. It may then be reapplied after painting. o Initially you may have to use a sharp knife under an edge to lift the decal, so you do not crease or bend it. • If you want to re-use the decal and it does not stick – try double sided tape in lieu of the original adhesive. Left - Height adjuster Right - Height adjuster parts diagram

sections.title: Bearing is stuck on the rear roller shaft
sections.text:
84 Ver. 25

Bearing is stuck on the rear roller shaft The problem with these bearings is the thin metal of the bearing holders making the use of a gear puller impractical. There are several options for the removal of the bearing. • Use a bearing puller (not a gear puller) • If you don’t have a bearing puller the simplest method is to use 2 pry bars or large screwdrivers and lever it off using the drum to lever against (see page 20 for the Like Duke method) • Alternatively, if you can remove the bearing on one side, then you can remove the drums leaving just the shaft with the bearing on the other end. You can then use a gear puller or rest the bearing on a vise and drive the shaft down/out with a soft metal drift. • Another option is to slide the bearing housing out as much as you can and secure the bearing in the vice jaws and then drive the shaft out with the soft metal drift. You need somebody to support all that heavy construction while doing that. The SBR member in this instance reported having a rear drum shaft oversized/improperly machined on the gear side end so after damaging one bearing, he had to remove it. Because of the thin, pressed metal of the bearing holder, they can easily bend out of shape. Another alternative to removing them is to fashion a backing plate to add strength to the bearing holder. Two examples are shown below which are used in conjunction with a three-jaw bearing puller.

Remember to inspect the keyway. Rear roller bearing holder

sections.title: How do I pull the rear drum apart?
sections.text:
85 Ver. 25

How do I pull the rear drum apart? Pulling the rear drum apart is often exacerbated by stuck Woodruff keys, bearings and parts generally stuck on due to corrosion. Liberal use of Inox etc. is recommended and some soak time. • Remove bearings and holders from both sides. • Loosen one of the drum shaft collars and remove the collar. • Wedge a large flat blade screwdriver between the rollers and lever them apart. Remove one roller. • Undo the other shaft collar and use gravity and if necessary a rubber mallet to remove the other roller. • Be careful not to lose the pawls which will come out from the ratchet box and pawl holder. You will now have the rear rollers and shaft apart. There are several ways to completely pull apart the rear rollers: Rear drum shaft collar Credit Blake Richards, SBR Drum shaft collar Backing plates for rear roller bearing holder Left Credit Steve Berghofer, SBR. Right Credit Tim Fraser, SBR

86 Ver. 25 • Put a bar through the other side and tap it out. You can use the front roller shaft. But put the nut on the end so you do not damage the thread. • WD40 and use a flat blade knife along the edges and scrapped then tap it between the two meeting faces. The blade eventually opens it up pushing it off the face. Does the job without a single tap. • Heat and quench • Apply Loctite freeze & release and gear puller. Flat bar across the drum with spacers between bar and the drum. The negates the need to tap metal on metal. Refer to the below picture.

Rear drum to be pulled apart. Credit Kym Schrapel SBR Gear puller method. Credit Kym Schrapel SBR Be careful with whatever method you use. If you are overly enthusiastic i.e. heavy handed, you are likely to crack the spokes near where they join the outer ring … or worse. Be careful - worst case scenario. Credit Simon Anderson, SBR

sections.title: Rear roller Counterweight
sections.text:
87 Ver. 25

When you have removed the rear rollers off the shaft you will be left with the pawl holder on the shaft. Removal of the pawl holder off the shaft can be done, it has a locating point on the shaft, but it is not necessary to remove it. You can replace the grub screw if you want but the key underneath does not wear so there is no need to take it off. To err on the side of caution in this regard is not a bad idea, if you damage the pawl holder they are not available aftermarket. Rear roller Counterweight Model 45 rear rollers for 14” and 20” models have a counterweight inside the rear roller on the non- drive side. It is attached by two threaded bolts on the side of roller. The counterweight: • helps balance the machine whilst turning. • helps the differential action of the rear rollers. • counters the effect of the off-set engine placement. • The 17” does not have the counterweight.

Rear roller and counterweight Credit Jason Mihatsch SBR Rear shaft, pawl holder and pawls removed and cleaned. Credit Brisbane Cylinder Mower Specialist, Andrew Mortimer SBR

sections.title: Rear drum shaft dirt sleeve
sections.text:
88 Ver. 25

Piece of trivia: Rover Model 45’s (Red) do not have holes for the counterweights. But each roller is about 2kg heavier than a Scott Bonnar Model 45, 17” roller. Rear drum shaft dirt sleeve Within the Model 45, 430 and 590 rear rollers you will (should) find two pieces of white plastic or PVC tube. These are the shaft dirt sleeve. They are meant to be there. Should you damage the PVC dirt sleeve you will have trouble finding the exact size PVC . Try either plumbing PVC pipe or electrical conduit; 32 mm OD. To fit it you may have to apply some gentle heat on the ends to flange it, so it fits on the roller and ratchet box. On a 17” model they are about 125mm long. Removal of rear roller shaft nut at the drum sprocket. Trick to stop the rear roller shaft from spinning so it can be undone The simplest and safest method is to place a long screwdriver into the rear roller hole which then will catch against the frame and stop the rear roller from moving.

Insert screwdriver here Nut on drum sprocket Credit Ian Barklem, SBR Rear drum showing hole to insert screwdriver in Credit Kym Schrapel, SBR Rear drum shaft dirt sleeve. Credit Ni Ck I SBR

sections.title: How do I remove the clutch cable?
sections.text:
89 Ver. 25 How do I remove the clutch cable? The clutch cable, is attached to the chassis as below. The end fits into the operating lever to work the clutch to operate the rear roller. The metal mounting bracket is slotted to allow you to slip the cable through. The bracket also has a threaded hole that the end of the cable screws into and can be adjusted. At times the threaded end of the clutch cable breaks off. Undo the nuts either side of the support and it will slide straight out of the slot.

On the Rover model 45’s there isn’t a thread in the hole in the bracket, both nuts lock it on place. Removal is simply a matter of unscrewing the broken piece under the spring then slide cable out the slot. Rear Roller – drum bush If, when taking apart the rear roller you see the drum shaft hole appears off centre, don’t be too worried. The rear roller is a cast iron part, the centre hole has been machined to be in the centre. One side may be slightly thicker on one side compared to the other as in the opposite picture. This is of no concern, what should be checked is the condition of the brass drum bush. Conduct a visual inspection to see if it and the rear shaft is not worn. How do I clean up the parts? Now that you have completed the tear down, you will want to clean up the parts before you progress to the next stage. If you do not have access to a parts washer, the following are various suggestions which have been made: • Toothbrush and kerosene • Petrol in a tray and brush • Mineral Turpentine and brush Broken clutch cable end. Credit Tom McAliden, SBR Rover Clutch cable bracket Credit Paul Pitt, SBR Rear roller and drum bush. Credit Mike Donovan, SBR

90 Ver. 25 • Degrease and hose it off. • Degrease, high pressure wash, put it in the dishwasher when the wife is not looking and then grease and reassemble. (not sure how advisable this one is) • Brake cleaner. Cleans everything well and any excess just evaporates. • Even energy drinks are the go (apparently)20

20 https://www.facebook.com/viralhog/videos/2074182692851058/

sections.title: CHAPTER 7 – RESTORATION
sections.text:
91 Ver. 25

CHAPTER 7 – RESTORATION

sections.title: Parts Suppliers
sections.text:
92 Ver. 25 Parts Suppliers To avoid any appearance of endorsing one supplier in preference to another, I have chosen not to include a list of parts suppliers. So stated, the following website21 has been identified which provides a useful parts supplier guide: www.scottbonnar.com There are a few parts suppliers who are frequently mentioned on the SBR page, particularly when members showcase their restoration projects and give credit to the suppliers they used. It is recommended that you take the time to search the SBR page or look through the “Recommendations” part of the page. Finally, find a good, local mower shop with experience in reel mowers. They will have an account with GA Spares and will be able to source most parts, often cheaper and quicker than the online options. Plus, they can be an invaluable source for advice. Secondhand Vs New Parts Having gone through the tear down phase there may be parts that require replacing. Unless you are intending to rebuild to the “all new” level you will invariably be faced with the decision, “do I replace with new or second-hand parts?”

Frequently people seek or sell various secondhand mower parts. So, what about second-hand parts, are they good, bad or just ugly?

• There are some secondhand parts like soleplates, rear rollers, catchers, chain covers and rear bearing holders, because they are either not available new or there is little risk using them, they are “good”. • The “bad”, the likes of the clutch half and front roller (including shaft) which in the ideal world should be confined to the bin and replaced with new parts. Will you be replacing worn parts with worn parts? • Finally, the ugly, bearings and chains. Just buy new. o unless it’s one of the unavailable bearings.

There are reasons for and against. For example, by installing a new cone onto a new PTO shaft, it will be a perfect fit with no movement. If one was to use a second hand clutch cone on a new PTO shaft there may still be some sort of sideways movement. Point being, there is a risk of unwanted issues and problems. Conversely the second-hand option will probably be cheaper, and some parts (e.g. reels with worn keyways) can be refurbished (new keyways can be machined) for a fraction of the cost of the new item.

Not everyone can afford new; and there's nothing wrong with a good secondhand part. Just beware, it's a gamble which only you can decide if it’s worth it. If in doubt – seek advice.

21 www.scottbonnar.com. Accessed 13 July 2020

sections.title: Secondhand Vs New Parts
sections.text:
92 Ver. 25 Parts Suppliers To avoid any appearance of endorsing one supplier in preference to another, I have chosen not to include a list of parts suppliers. So stated, the following website21 has been identified which provides a useful parts supplier guide: www.scottbonnar.com There are a few parts suppliers who are frequently mentioned on the SBR page, particularly when members showcase their restoration projects and give credit to the suppliers they used. It is recommended that you take the time to search the SBR page or look through the “Recommendations” part of the page. Finally, find a good, local mower shop with experience in reel mowers. They will have an account with GA Spares and will be able to source most parts, often cheaper and quicker than the online options. Plus, they can be an invaluable source for advice. sections.title: Flattened Bearing Shield
sections.text:
93 Ver. 25 Parts sellers also have a responsibility – that is to have a good understanding of what is serviceable and what goes into the bin.

In conclusion, be mindful that some parts come with inherent risks. Simply put, buyer beware.

Flattened Bearing Shield When the reel is removed it is advisable to check the condition of the bearing shields. Some have been found to have flattened. They can be replaced with new ones, or there is a simple method to reshape the bearing shield.

To return the shield into the beveled shape simply get two suitably sized sockets and place it in a vice as shown opposite. Slowly close the vice to bend the shield back to the right shape.

Above - Bearing shields - Left - good, right - needs refurbishing Credit Evan Sakkas, SBR Re-shaping a bearing shield using two sockets. Credit Luke Duke, SBR

sections.title: Worn sprocket
sections.text:
94 Ver. 25 Worn sprocket An integral part in the working of a cylinder mower is the sprocket. Over time, and especially if the chains are not tensioned correctly and lubricated, they will wear and cause problems for you. Examine how well the chain meshes with the sprocket teeth. With a healthy chain and sprocket, the individual chain rollers align perfectly with the spaces between the sprocket teeth and fit down into those U-shaped grooves evenly. They will start to either really point out, thin and roll like a wave or really shorten. If the teeth are sharp, the sprocket is worn. Look for indented wear around the circumference of the sprocket at and below the teeth that resembles the shape of the chain. This “shadow” wear indicates a well-worn sprocket that should be replaced. If the sprocket is worn, it is an indication that the chain also needs replacing. Below are examples of sprockets in good and bad condition.

Badly worn sprocket Credit Steven Wenzel, SBR Remember to inspect the keyway. Worn Clutch Cone

Should the end of your clutch cone look like the one opposite, then it needs replacing. The wear has been caused by the clutch fork. In this instance the clutch fork is also likely to be worn and needs replacing. Remember to inspect the keyway.

A sprocket in particularly good condition Credit Michael McGerr, SBR Worn clutch cone Credit Kearney Royle, SBR

sections.title: Steel or Alloy Clutch half
sections.text:
95 Ver. 25

Steel or Alloy Clutch half If you are doing up multiple Model 45’s and have forgotten which clutch goes on which machine this is for you. If not, it is a piece of trivia. Rule of thumb: • Steel on solids (top image) • Alloy on twin deck (bottom image)

Next piece of trivia - the steel is cast iron and was only in the 5/8” internal diameter (believe there have been 3/4” ones which were aftermarket machined to that size).

How do I fix a bent or sagging rail? Bending or sagging of the rails, particularly where the motor sits may be found on twin rail models. With the use of solid pieces of wood and clamps as depicted opposite the rails can be straightened. With the wood and the clamp, it simply pulls the rails back up to level . You will have to wind a bit past level and let it spring back until they are level (check using a straight edge). The sole plate should be installed to maintain integrity/strength of the chassis during this procedure.

This process should be completed before any rail strengthening is attempted. Once the rails are straight – then you should repair cracks and/or strengthen the rails.

Straightening a bent rail. Credit Nev Gordon, SBR Solid top , twin bottom. Credit Adam Motbey, SBR

sections.title: How do I repair a cracked rail? (UPDATED)
sections.text:
96 Ver. 25

How do I repair a cracked rail? (UPDATED) Twin rail models are susceptible to cracks appearing near the engine mounts (solid decks are less susceptible but still have been known to develop cracks). The cracks form due to vibration from the likes of loose engine mountings, the cotter pin, clutch mounts; basically, poor maintenance. At times, the cracks will not be visible under the paint/powder coat as they will be hair-line fractures emanating from the bolt holes through to actual and visible cracks across the rail as shown below. If you see paint cracks around the engine mounts, they are probably the rail, not just old paint. Therefore, you should remove the paint (strip/sand blast) early in the rebuild process to identify cracked rails.

Checking the bent rail during straightening. Credit Nev Gordon, SBR Cracks on a Mod 45 Solid deck. Credit Bluey Cullen, SBR Cracks

97 Ver. 25

Visible cracked rail Credit Rob Forsythe, SBR Hair line cracks clearly visible after removal of paint. Credit Michael Hanes, SBR

98 Ver. 25 SBR member Ben Parry advises model 45 rails get a standard high cycle fatigue crack, i.e., where many vibration cycles (often in the millions) are applied to a small area of local stress. In the twin rail mowers, there are two nuts welded underneath each rail to hold the engine down. Each nut has two spots of weld to attach, and this is the propagation site for the cracking that happens. The stress concentration at these welds at the nuts is high; add in the millions of engine firing cycles and eventually a tiny crack may appear. Once there, the crack will keep running. In the tip of a crack there is always a higher stress point, and the crack will try to find a neutral stress point and stop, or the part will weaken, and stresses rise, then a catastrophic failure occurs.

To fix an already cracked rail, it is important to connect the engine mounting nuts to whatever bracing you use to spread the load out over a wider area. Spreading out load reduces stress which will in turn reduce cracking.

To fix the cracks: • Drill out each end of the cracks to stop them growing and grind in a channel for a weld to hold. • MIG or TIG weld the cracks and weld 3mm strengthening plates or 25mm x 25 mm angle inside/underneath the rails. Grind flat the welds on top of the rails/deck. Angle is better as it provides additional strength, both horizontally and vertically. It is advisable to prime the rails/deck and strengthening steel before. • It is suggested that the strengthening be placed along the length of the rails thereby adding extra support the entire rail length from side plate to side plate. This helps with the distribution of weight (even the solids have a strengthening rail that runs under them the whole way across). This will necessitate drilling holes where the bolt holes/nuts are and for the deflector mounts. • When welding, care needs to be taken so that excessive heat is not generated. Rather than one long weld, run several small stitches and do it in an order to distribute the heat evenly and avoid warping. When welding underneath it is suggested that you also weld around the nuts. Drill a hole at end of the crack Grind groove along the crack to be welded Crack repair. Credit Brendan McCubben SBR

99 Ver. 25 o Shrinkage from excessive heat, too much or over welding may cause bowing or warping of the rails. It has been suggested to use a thick heavy steel plate clamped on the outside, this will help to disperse heat and let it cool down with it on. Also to avoid warping of the chassis -solidly clamp down the frame during the process, keep the sole plate in-place, even blocks of wood between the plates. • You may also seal underneath between the weld stitches with a paintable silicon sealant to prevent the ingress of moisture and the possibility for corrosion to develop between the rail and strengthening pieces. Should you be tempted to try and fabricate a solid deck to replace the twin rails, the advice is that you do not, go and buy a genuine solid deck mower instead. Why? Simply, it has about staying true to the original, not changing what already is there. There are too many “Frankenstein” builds and/or fakes already circulating. Below are several examples of rail strengthening fixes showing the use of angle or 3mm flat strap as posted by members. None of the examples as depicted are recommended in preference to others, they are merely provided as examples. Left - Example of rail strengthening Credit Trent Deaves, SBR Particularly good examples of strengthened Twin Rails and a Solid Deck. Note the short, evenly spaced welds and the strengthening goes the full length of the rails and deck. Credit, Nev Gordon, SBR

100 Ver. 25

SBR member Nev Gordon suggests instead of cutting pieces out of the angle iron for the nuts it is much stronger to grind the nuts off and put a full piece of angle iron in with just the holes for the bolts to go through and then weld the nuts onto the angle iron. An alternative method has been offered by SBR member Andrew Lengkeek. Repairs occur with the paint still on the underside of the rails. Instead of leaving the engine mount nuts on, this method involves removing the engine mounting nuts and welding in new ones (5/16 UNF) so that the new reinforcement is taking most of the load/vibrations and it is not just on the welds around the nuts. Rail strengthening example Credit Chris Levi, SBR Rail strengthening example pre-weld Credit Simon Chin, SBR

101 Ver. 25 • For the front rail, cut the angle short so there is no need to modify the deflector brackets. For the rear rail run the angle the full length with a 5-10mm gap each end. Clamp the angle pieces in place and stitch weld them in. • Then turn the chassis over and drill though the engine mount holes into the new angle piece, using the holes in the rail as a guide. • Then mount the engine, bolting it down. o Then weld the nuts on so you know they are perfectly positioned. • You then strip the chassis of paint/rust or send it to be sand blasted. o When painted seal all the edges of the new angle with silicone to reduce the ingress of moisture between the rail and the angle.

Rail reinforcing kits are now available from Core Earth Designs (https://www.coreearthdesigns.com.au/). Alternatively, replacement rails are available from Moteck Machining and Cylinder Mowers (https://moteck.com.au/). Both these businesses are SBR page members. Whatever method you choose, repairing or replacing cracked rails is highly recommended as a “preventative maintenance” measure - maintenance to lessen the likelihood of it failing. It is performed while the equipment is still working so that it does not break down unexpectedly.

Alternative rail strengthening method Credit Andrew Lengkeek, SBR

sections.title: Grass Deflector issues when strengthening the rails
sections.text:
102 Ver. 25

Grass Deflector issues when strengthening the rails Whether you choose to use flat steel or angle for strengthening, remember to make appropriate cut outs to allow the deflector knob brackets to work.

Some members have reported having their rails repaired without making allowances for the deflector tabs (as shown above). The thickness of the flat bar or angle used with the position of the slot on the deflector brackets won’t work. There are two options; either modify the repair or modify the deflector brackets such that the slot is higher up and lengthen the deflector screws (i.e., remove and replace).

Example of cut-out for grass deflector, Credit Darrall Suter, SBR Left ; Rail repair without allowing for the deflector Credit Tom Verryt, SBR Right - modified deflector bracket Credit David DQ de Closey, SBR

sections.title: How do I fix a worn keyway on my PTO shaft?
sections.text:
103 Ver. 25 How do I fix a worn keyway on my PTO shaft? Several options have been posted: • Make a brass key clamp in place and run a weld either side remove brass key and machine back or use flap disk if you do not have a lathe. • Get a new slot machined in. The keys do not need to be clocked, so they do not have to line up with each other. • And the final and easier option, buy a new shaft.

If the keyway is worn, check the condition of the clutch half as it may be worn and need replacing.

Movement in front roller frame During the tear down phase, you may notice excessive movement in the front roller22. It is better to fix during the restoration phase. The probable cause is worn chassis bushes of the front roller bracket. Before painting remove the old bushes and weld in new bushes. Meanwhile, source a new front roller bracket assembly.

22 https://www.facebook.com/chris.francis.77377/videos/10156444251612225/?t=0 accessed 18 July 2019 PTO shaft with worn keyway A worn PTO shaft can cause wear to the clutch, sprockets and more importantly, cause vibrations – a cause for rails to crack. It is therefore advisable to replace rather than repair a worn PTO shaft. Left - Movement in front roller assembly Credit Chris Francis, SBR Right - New chassis bush welded in Credit Tim Delios SBR

sections.text:

sections.title: Worn Front Roller Bushes
sections.text:
104 Ver. 25 Worn Front Roller Bushes Wear in the front roller bushes will cause movement in the front roller and therefore an uneven cut. To quickly assess if the bushes are worn and in need of replacing place the shank of a 3/8" bolt in the bush. That will identify if it is round or not and how much wear you have. Or simply clean the paint off the inside face of the side plate and bush and see if it is elongated.

If you have vernier calipers measure the inside diameter (I.D.), new bushes measure 9.5mm ID (3/8”). If they are worn, source new ones, grind the old ones off and weld the new bushes in place.

Whilst at it check the front roller bracket assembly as it will probably be worn and need replacing

Refurbishment of Original “Anthony” Brand Bearings The original Anthony Brand bearings as used on the rear rollers are no longer available. There are replacement sleeved bearings available, however if you have the time and inclination the original bearings can be refreshed. SBR member Pete Attard with tips from Darrall Sutter used the following procedure23: • Remove all the excess grease and build up off the bearings, • Carefully remove the plastic cap off the bearing using a fine jeweller’s flat blade screw driver • After the plastic cap is removed the bearings move freely within the race, so once again with a small screw driver clean all the excess and old grease from the race • Place the bearings and small plastic bearing caps in a small plastic container and apply degreaser and let sit for approximately 30minutes • Give the plastic cap, internal ball bearings and race a clean with an old tooth brush and the outside with stainless steel wool and rinse • Thoroughly dry the bearing and remove any remaining grease with the fine flat screw driver again • Mark the ball locations on the outer race of the bearing using the plastic cap • Place grease in the bearing methodically and place the ball bearings adjacent the texta mark (doing this allows the cap to go straight on when installing at the end) one at a time using the small screw driver • Once all bearings are in position with grease, additional grease is added, there is no need to pack it excessively with grease • Plastic bearing cap is replaced. • The bearing is rolled continuously to expel the excessive grease.

23 https://www.facebook.com/groups/1801521490103765/permalink/3197286960527204/ accessed 6/1/2023 Worn front roller bush Credit Peter Morton, SBR

105 Ver. 25

Degreasing the bearings Credit Pete Attard, SBR Marking the ball bearing position Credit Pete Attard, SBR Packing with grease Credit Pete Attard, SBR

sections.title: How to remove rust
sections.text:
106 Ver. 25

How to remove rust As part of the restoration process you may wish to totally strip your machine back to bare metal. There are several options, from doing it yourself to outsourcing. Some options are as follows, remember if you are doing it yourself, wear appropriate personal protective clothing etc. Sand blasting Unless you own or have access to a sand blasting cabinet, this will mean getting the job done for you at a cost. Sand blasting is probably the easiest and best method for removing all rust, paint and especially the powder coats of later Brisbane made models (e.g. the “Kermit greens”). Remember to ensure that machined surfaces are protected. Professional sand blasters should return your parts primed to prevent further corrosion. When sand blasting the following areas should not be blasted: • where a bearing goes onto a shaft or a shaft through a bush e.g. inside bearing holders • where the cork attaches • on a surface that touches cork e.g. clutch cone Wire brush/Paint Stripper Various wire brushes can be used, from ones on a bench grinder to others that attach to an angle grinder and finally those which fit an electric/cordless drill. The choice is yours. Like sand blasting, wire brushes can also be used to strip paint, however, to make the job easier the use of a chemical paint stripper is suggested. When using paint stripper, it is suggested to first go over the paint with the wire brush to etch or scratch the surface, thereby making it easier for the paint stripper to work on the paint. When using paint stripper once applied to the surface it has been recommended to wrap the item in clear plastic e.g. “Glad Wrap” after applying. It tends to help with the process. “Diggers Industrial Strength Paint Stripper” reportedly works on powder coat. Using any paint stripper – remember to wear good gloves. Wire brushes on drills tend to lay over when used in the forward direction constantly and polish rather than remove paint/or rust. It is therefore recommended that they are periodically run in reverse to “stand” the wire up. Stripping disk An alternative to the wire brush is the stripping disk (right) fitted to an angle grinder. These are less severe than the “flap disk” but just as effective. Acid baths (Vinegar, Citric and Oxalic acids) The use of mild acids such as vinegar (acetic acid) or citric acid is a simple and cost-effective method to remove rust. Citric acid is known to remove stains and Phosphoric acid is also commonly used for rust removal. Such acids do not remove paint unlike stronger acids such as hydrochloric acid. Simply clean oil, grease and dirt from the parts and submerge in the mild acid solution. Neat vinegar may be used. Adding salt to the vinegar Stripping disk Credit https://www.bunnings.com.au/flexovit-125mm- multi-purpose-flexi-strip-stripping-disc_p6310281

It is therefore recommended that they are periodically run in reverse to “stand” the wire up. Stripping disk An alternative to the wire brush is the stripping disk (right) fitted to an angle grinder. These are less severe than the “flap disk” but just as effective. Acid baths (Vinegar, Citric and Oxalic acids) The use of mild acids such as vinegar (acetic acid) or citric acid is a simple and cost-effective method to remove rust. Citric acid is known to remove stains and Phosphoric acid is also commonly used for rust removal. Such acids do not remove paint unlike stronger acids such as hydrochloric acid. Simply clean oil, grease and dirt from the parts and submerge in the mild acid solution. Neat vinegar may be used. Adding salt to the vinegar Stripping disk Credit https://www.bunnings.com.au/flexovit-125mm- multi-purpose-flexi-strip-stripping-disc_p6310281

sections.title: Stripping disk
sections.text:
106 Ver. 25

How to remove rust As part of the restoration process you may wish to totally strip your machine back to bare metal. There are several options, from doing it yourself to outsourcing. Some options are as follows, remember if you are doing it yourself, wear appropriate personal protective clothing etc. Sand blasting Unless you own or have access to a sand blasting cabinet, this will mean getting the job done for you at a cost. Sand blasting is probably the easiest and best method for removing all rust, paint and especially the powder coats of later Brisbane made models (e.g. the “Kermit greens”). Remember to ensure that machined surfaces are protected. Professional sand blasters should return your parts primed to prevent further corrosion. When sand blasting the following areas should not be blasted: • where a bearing goes onto a shaft or a shaft through a bush e.g. inside bearing holders • where the cork attaches • on a surface that touches cork e.g. clutch cone Wire brush/Paint Stripper Various wire brushes can be used, from ones on a bench grinder to others that attach to an angle grinder and finally those which fit an electric/cordless drill. The choice is yours. Like sand blasting, wire brushes can also be used to strip paint, however, to make the job easier the use of a chemical paint stripper is suggested. When using paint stripper, it is suggested to first go over the paint with the wire brush to etch or scratch the surface, thereby making it easier for the paint stripper to work on the paint. When using paint stripper once applied to the surface it has been recommended to wrap the item in clear plastic e.g. “Glad Wrap” after applying. It tends to help with the process. “Diggers Industrial Strength Paint Stripper” reportedly works on powder coat. Using any paint stripper – remember to wear good gloves. Wire brushes on drills tend to lay over when used in the forward direction constantly and polish rather than remove paint/or rust. It is therefore recommended that they are periodically run in reverse to “stand” the wire up. Stripping disk An alternative to the wire brush is the stripping disk (right) fitted to an angle grinder. These are less severe than the “flap disk” but just as effective. Acid baths (Vinegar, Citric and Oxalic acids) The use of mild acids such as vinegar (acetic acid) or citric acid is a simple and cost-effective method to remove rust. Citric acid is known to remove stains and Phosphoric acid is also commonly used for rust removal. Such acids do not remove paint unlike stronger acids such as hydrochloric acid. Simply clean oil, grease and dirt from the parts and submerge in the mild acid solution. Neat vinegar may be used. Adding salt to the vinegar Stripping disk Credit https://www.bunnings.com.au/flexovit-125mm- multi-purpose-flexi-strip-stripping-disc_p6310281

sections.title: Molasses
sections.text:
107 Ver. 25 is also an option - add 1 cup salt to 4 litres of vinegar. Citric acid can be mixed at the rate of approx. 15 grams per 400 ml water. Mixing vinegar with citric acid solutions is not recommended, you will dilute whichever is the stronger solution (vinegar is generally around 5% acetic acid). You should see the parts with bubbles appearing, indicating the solution is working. Depending on the strength of the solution and the amount of rust the soak time may only be a matter of hours or need an overnight or days soaking. Another acid which is readily available is oxalic acid. This is found as concrete rust stain remover available from hardware stores or in powder form from cleaning product suppliers. Simply mix with water, make a solution of 1 part Oxalic to 10 with water and apply24 and then wash clean with copious amount of water. Avoid placing alloy parts in acid solutions as it will probably end badly for you e.g. the throttle cable lever as you will likely find the threads will be “chemically” stripped. Even cast iron parts should not be left in acid for extended periods for the same reasons. In other words, keep an eye on them. After an acid bath it is advisable to rinse parts in water to neutralize the parts. I also give the parts a wipe over with methylated spirits (a personal preference prior to priming). Cleanup with “Ranex” is another option. Molasses To remove rust without using toxic chemicals, mix 9 parts water with 1 part molasses in a container (ratio has also been reported as 5:1), then soak the parts between several days and two weeks. The solution breaks down the rust and holds it in suspension. Coke and Foil A popular method for cleaning up chrome handlebars is to use coke and foil. Simply crumple some foil and dip in coke and rub away. To reduce the scrubbing, you may soak items in coke (as with other mild acids). Coke works in several ways as a rust remover. Carbonation combined with the acid can dissolve metal oxides. Electrolysis Some members have set up an electrolysis bath to remove rust. I suggest you search YouTube and Google for such setups. The following has been found on a Whirlpool forum.25 For the "electrolysis" method all you need is a 12v battery or a 12v battery charger, a couple of spoonsful of washing soda, and a water tub large enough to fit your item into. Left overnight it will remove all your rust completely without any nasty chemicals and it is quite safe to do. Here is how. 1. Fill tub with water. 2. Mix in a couple of tablespoons of Washing Soda (sold cheap at supermarkets) 3. Place your rusty item into tub so it is completely underwater. 4. Place a sizeable piece of scrap steel (NOT stainless steel) into tub. Note: This scrap piece must NOT be completely underwater and make sure your rusty item and the scrap steel are not touching each other.

24 https://www.westchem.com.au/products/oxalic-acid-powder accessed 2/1/2019 25 https://forums.whirlpool.net.au/archive/2607628 accessed 2/1/2019

sections.title: How do I clean up the handlebars?
sections.text:
108 Ver. 25 5. Connect the NEGATIVE (-) lead of battery/charger to your rusty item, ensuring it has good contact with the metal. 6. Connect POSITIVE (+) lead of battery/charger to that part of the scrap steel that is NOT underwater, this connection should not get wet. 7. Turn battery charger on and leave on overnight. How do I clean up the handlebars? Not only handlebars, but people have also asked about cleaning or polishing reel bearing holders and height adjusters. A simple and cheap method for the chrome is elbow grease, aluminum foil or fine steel wool and coke. Other suggestions have included: • Purple Polish, Autosol or Bowdens Own metal polish • Vinegar with a pinch of table salt • Brasso • Mix vinegar with flour and a small pinch of salt, you want a paste, a good bit of elbow grease and a rag and then finish with Brasso. • Get a calico polishing disk for the bench grinder and polishing compound. • Use a Dremel to sand imperfections out. Then a hand sand. Then onto a polishing wheel on a bench grinder • See the Rust Removal section.

SBR member, Nev Gordon has provided the following method to clean up handlebars. • In a large plastic container add 4 litres of vinegar , 1 cup salt , left soak overnight. • Mix up a bi carb soda paste and rub in with fine steel wool. • Rinse off, dry and spray with wd40 and leave overnight . Above - Before and after handlebars Credit SBR Left - Cotton buffing wheel with ultra-fine compound on the bench grinder Credit Brendon Mccubben, SBR

109 Ver. 25 • Wipe off and onto the buffing wheel and then finish off with Autosol.

Above and below, before and after of handlebar clean up. Credit Nev Gordon, SBR

sections.title: What is the paint colour of Scott Bonnar mowers?
sections.text:
110 Ver. 25 What is the paint colour of Scott Bonnar mowers? Unfortunately, there are no remaining official records from Scott Bonnar as to the colour/s used on their machines. This applies across all models and the various colours, be they “Apple Green” hammertone or the later powder coated “Kermit Green” mowers or the blue Model 19 or whatever. The short answer is no one knows. The codes and mixtures were lost over the years, they changed slightly with each batch made and as such there was nothing set in stone. What with slight variations from the factory and the effects of time causing mowers to fade differently depending on their exposure to the elements etc. who knows what any of the “original” colours were. So, which "original" do you want. There is no right or wrong “original” colour. The following background information on the various colours has been copied from OutDoorKing.com26 (ODK): There were a few other colours used within the (Scott Bonnar) range. They were Hammertone paints in the following colours, "Machine Pacific Blue" and "Machine Grey" which were used on Model 45 "Lawn Kings", also "Machine Gold" was used on earlier Model 33 "Lawn Kings" in limited numbers. Machine Pacific Blue:

26 https://www.outdoorking.com/forum/ubbthreads.php/ubb/showflat/topic/11922/gonew/1.html accessed 5/8/2018. The best advice is to remove and clean inside the chain case cover (or other piece which has not been exposed to the sun, take it to an automotive paint supplier to be colour matched. (some suppliers may charge for the matching). Machine Pacific Blue (note the Lawn King Decal) Credit Bonnar Bloke, ODK

111 Ver. 25

Pacific Blue “Lawn King” Model 45, Credit Bed Godridge, SBR Machine Grey:

Machine Grey, Credit Bonnar Bloke, ODK

112 Ver. 25

Machine Grey, Credit Bonnar Bloke, ODK The 4 mainstream colours are featured below. 1st. Alpine Green: Used from Pre-1968 built machines, in slight variants of colour shade up until late 1976.

Alpine Green, Credit Bonnar Bloke, ODK

113 Ver. 25 The above catcher is from a late 1976 Electric 14-inch mower which sports the Red and White Scott Bonnar Decal. 2nd. Apple Green: Used from late 1976 until 1984 in both the Adelaide and the replacement Brisbane Plant.

Apple Green, Credit Bonnar Bloke, ODK This Model 45 is an original factory-made survivor and NOT a repainted or Restored Machine. 3rd. Kermit Green: This colour was instigated by the introduction of the "NEW" Rover Powder Coating line in Brisbane.

Kermit Green, Credit Bonnar Bloke, ODK

114 Ver. 25 Note the change of Engine Colour to Black. This combination sold poorly. All these Kermit Green units were Powder Coated and NOT painted. 4th. Rover Red: Used on the last two variants of the Model 45, Mk I forward facing clutch lever and Mk II Rear facing lever. The opposite depicted mower is the later Mk II and last issued "Model 45 Reel Mower" prior to cease of production. Note the rearward facing Primary Clutch Lever. Rover Red was Powder Coated only and NOT painted.

Black chain cover variation During 1974 – 75 there was a change to the Model 45 colour scheme with some mowers appearing with black hammertone chain and handlebar covers. The example below shows it with the “Scott Bonnar” and “45” decals on the chain cover. The single alloy ID plate on this example dates the mower 1975 – 1977. The below brochure (circa 1976) confirms the colour scheme to be genuine from the factory. The brochure shows the pentagon decal on the chain cover.

Mod 45, 14" twin rail with black hammertone chain and handlebar covers. Credit Shane Kosteszyn, SBR Model 45 Red Rover. Credit Rik Wickett, SBR

115 Ver. 25

Brochure (Circa 1976) showing black and green colour scheme. Credit Outdoorking.com

Black chain cover with “Scott Bonnar 45” decals on Model 45 electric Credit Mark Turci, SBR

sections.title: Scott Bonnar Green
sections.text:
116 Ver. 25 Scott Bonnar Green Scott Bonnar Restoration members have variously attempted to replicate the original colours. Some members prefer to keep the colour codes their secret to maintain their originality, some have been willing to share the codes. The following are shared examples:

Scott Bonnar Green Credit Scott Dunning, SBR Credit Ni Ck Ian, SBR

117 Ver. 25 Shane Kosteszyn has advised Wattyl Hammer finish in olive Green matches closely to one of the green colours. The Wattyl color chart is shown below.

There was another alternative avaiable in aerosol cans, the GALMET brand Hammertone Jade Green, a very close match to the Apple Green colour. Sadly it appears to no longer be available.

Wattyl Hammer Finish colour chart. Credit Wattylindustrial.com.au

118 Ver. 25

Protec Hammertone colour chart Credit Kylie Cee, SBR Note: The colours off the colour card will not match the paint anymore as Protec/ PPG has changed the way it is made and the formula. They are all now a slightly different colour.

119 Ver. 25 For those who may have trouble visualizing the difference between the Protec Apple and Olive Green hammertone colours the below picture is provided.

The Protec or PPG branded 311 Hammertone enamel is now sold as “HFE” – “Hammer Finish Enamel”. It is an enamel paint based on a copolymer alkyd resin which has been pigmented with aluminium flake. Protec or PPG paint suppliers can do their whole colour range in a hammered finish. For suppliers of Wattyl or Protec paints let Google be your friend, both brands have store locators on their web pages.

Left Apple green. Right Olive Green. Credit Glenn Russell, SBR PPG - Hammer Finish Enamel Credit PPR.com.au

120 Ver. 25 Andrew Lengkeek advises for those Brisbane residents that Queensland Paint Supplies, Brisbane, have on file “Scott Bonnar Mower Green”. He further advises an Apple Green colour mix is hammerdry silver using Wattyl Ultra Tint. 360-45, 370-71.5 and 550-95.75. Jordan Wall also advises for those located on the Northside of Brisbane; Peninsula Paint Supplies in Redcliffe have colour matched his original colour. They have it on file called “Scott Bonner Green”. Remember, the colour of the Scott Bonnars varied, there were various shades of the various colours.

Variety Paints in Western Australia offer 300gm spray cans of customs hammered green finish27. Feedback indicates this colour “is a work in progress”. For those Perth based members, SBR member Andrew Wright obtained a match from Protect Pty Ltd, 87 Welshpool Road, Welshpool, WA of Apple Green which has been saved and named “Bonnar Green” Powder Coat (NEW) Apart from the “Kermit Green”, other Scott Bonnar green variants were paint finishes. Finding a suitable “Scott Bonnar Green” match in powder coat has proven difficult. That said, SBR member Brent FOSTER has sourced a powder from Oxytech in Sydney which appears to be a good close match as shown opposite.

"

27 https://varietypaints.com.au/products/variety-paints-custom-hammer-finish-scott-Bonnar-green-spray-paint- 300-grams accessed 15 August 2018. Protect Olive green, hammertone Credit Kylie Cee SBR Scott Bonner Green" Credit Jordan Wall, SBR Right: Powder coat finish - close Bonnar Green match from Oxytech, Sydney. Credit Brent FOSTER, SBR

sections.title: Kermit Green
sections.text:
121 Ver. 25

Kermit Green The original “Kermit Green” mowers were powder coated out of the Brisbane factory. Two aerosol can paint colour options have been identified as being close matches: • Dulux “Mistletoe Green” • Rustoleum gloss “Meadow Green” A colour match has been done, the paint formula appears opposite.

Rover Red The colour “Signal Red” is reported as a good, close match. Protec reportedly has “Scott Bonnar red” in their formulation computer. DULUX 9613268 has been reported to be Rover Red. It also has been colour matched, ref number crj4(81165) code: KT16. The red in the Wattyl Kill Rust range is also a close match. Lawn King models Lawn King models came in several colours. Color options identified for the Machine Pacific Blue which has also been referred to as “Bonmow Blue” are: • Septone Hammertone Blue (reported as close match in Outdoorking.com) Model 33 painted in Hichen Hammertone Gold, Credit Reece Jennings, SBR Rover Red OEM Code, Credit Darren Tooth, SBR Kermit Green colour matched paint formula Credit Shane Kosteszyn, SBR

sections.title: Briggs and Stratton engine
sections.text:
122 Ver. 25 • The colour “Dolphin Blue” (SBR member Trent Matthew Hassum advises that tints can be added in if it needs to be lighter) Specific to the Model 33’s Lawn Kings, some came out in a gold finish, known as “Machine Gold”. SBR member Reece Jennings has found Hichem hammertone gold to be a close match.

Briggs and Stratton engine "International Orange" is the colour of older Briggs and Stratton engines. VHT brand paint in the “Aluminium” colour is reported to be close to the grey of the 2 HP Briggs and Stratton engines found on the early Model 45, 14” solids.

sections.title: Kirby engine
sections.text:
123 Ver. 25 Kirby engine Australian standards colour chart AS2700 R11 International Orange is almost identical to the original Kirby’s. Opposite, Chevrolet Orange is another option, reported to be a very, good match. Advice has been received from a long-time mower repairer that early Kirby engines were a red colour, the author has found red paint on a Model 33 Kirby powered mower. That red is like the Dupli – Colour engine enamel Ford Red colour.

Good paint shops can scan a part, match the colour and mix paint to match; and even do it for you in a spray can.. You can also get two pack colour matched paint in spray cans, but once you activate it you must use it all as it will harden after a few hours. The custom aerosol spray-option can cost more than simply getting a can of paint matched and mixed.

What NOT to paint. The following areas should not be painted, or powder coated: • where a bearing goes onto a shaft or a shaft through a bush o e.g. inside bearing holders (includes the lip) o The brass bushings inside the ratchet box • where the cork attaches • on a surface that touches cork o clutch cone Inside o friction surface of the large intermediate sprocket • PTO shaft into the cone and the other end where the bearing fits

Kirby engine paint match Credit Glenn Russell SBR Large intermediate sprocket DO NOT paint the friction surface Credit Glenn Russell, SBR DO NOT PAINT this surface

sections.title: Paint or Powder coat?
sections.text:
124 Ver. 25

The only area that is optional is the inside of the ratchet box within the real roller. If you do paint the ratchet box remember, do not grease the area or the pawls. Personally, I prefer not to paint this area, it has lasted 50 years from new without paint. Clean it up with a wire wheel to remove any rust and built up grime to ensure the pawls move freely.

Paint or Powder coat? Powder coat is hygroscopic and draws water between the finished surface and the metal. Compared to paint as a coating it is a harder finish, however like paint, if it is damaged, rust will get in. Even when surfaces are properly powder coated with a zinc primer it is still porous. Similarly, Hammertone enamel is also porous and will let moisture in. A good etch prime and two pack paint will have superior adhesion and will give much better corrosion resistance. Ask yourself this – “are car bodies power-coated? Or sprayed with wet paint?” Answer is - wet dipped in electro-phosphate (etch) primer and spray painted. Powder coating is used in industry because it is fast and on an industrial scale it is low cost and easy to control the process and avoids the use of solvents. However, cost wise for the one off jobs (as in mowers), powder coating is more expensive than painting, most people cannot powder coat whereas most can use a spray can at a minimum. Another way to answer the question is powder coat for a strong finish that isn't easily repaired. Paint for a weaker finish that is easy to touch up and repair dents and scratches.

Unpainted ratchet box Credit Ryan McClure, SBR Painted ratchet holder and shaft Credit Glenn Russell, SBR Example of where to paint the PTO shaft. Credit Nev Gordon, SBR

sections.title: How much paint is required?
sections.text: 125 Ver. 25 Should you choose to powder coat: • Normal body filler (e.g. for repairing the chain cover) is not suitable to be used when powder coating. • When masking areas not to be coated (e.g. clutch friction surfaces) a heat resistant masking tape should be used. Normal masking tape when powder coated can be difficult to remove. If you are considering powder coating the reel, consider this; some professional reel sharpeners prefer not to grind freshly powder coated reels as it can affect the ultimate grind quality. Also, the powder coating is prone to chipping. It is therefore considered better to paint the reel rather than powder coating. For minor repairs, paint is much easier to repair and blend in. It is all in the preparation, application of the paint/powder coating and how you treat the finished product. Ultimately it is a personal choice. How much paint is required? The following are approximate amounts of topcoat paint required when spray painting (not spray can): Model 45: 750 ml Model 38: 300 ml Model 430/590: 500 ml Painting tips General The following is a collection of various painting tips. They are by no means exhaustive nor intended to be a comprehensive set of instructions. • Talk to the paint shop that is supplying your topcoat and ask them what primer is needed for their topcoat .That way if there are any problems you have a little bit of come back. o If you want quality advice and products go to a trade or automotive supplier, not your local hardware supermarket e.g. Bunnings, Mitre 10 etc. • In the absence of professional advice from a reputable supplier - Do NOT mix brands between primer and topcoats to avoid compatibility issues. • Work in a Ventilated Area. o Wear PPE • Protect surrounding surfaces with drop cloths. • Preparation is the key, make sure the surface to be painted is clean of rust, oil and other contaminants and the paint is thoroughly mixed. • If using a spray gun, a gravity fed gun is recommended. The consensus is a 1.8 mm nozzle is ideal for hammertone paint. • Follow manufacturer’s instructions/Materials Data Sheets o Check re application & flash off.
