sections.title: Clear coat
sections.text:
126 Ver. 25 o Avoid painting in direct sunlight, hot or high humidity conditions (between 10 – 30 deg C and less than 85% RH) • Apply a Test Coat o Practice if you are a novice. • Hold the spray gun/can approximately six inches from the work piece and spray in a side-to-side sweeping motion. Only apply the trigger/nozzle when you are moving the spray gun/can. If you continuously hold the trigger down, you will get a continuous spray of paint. This will lead to the paint being thicker in some spots which could cause the paint to run. • Tip: If you intend to use “Chrome” paint it is suggested that you apply the chrome paint over gloss black. It helps with the “chrome” effect. • Tip: Use ear plugs to protect threaded holes. • You will struggle to keep paint on the exhaust, but to keep it from rusting keep an oil soaked rag in a container and wipe the exhaust after use. • Want to paint the reel? A couple of pieces of wood can be a simple stand as in the below picture.

Clear coat When it comes to painting issues, the application of clear coats can cause issues. It is also a topic where there are mixed views, some say do it and others say do not. The best advice I can offer is do your homework, seek advice from a reputable professional supplier and even then do a test piece to ensure it is compatible with the base coat. Clear coating Hammertone finishes is a different matter and is discussed further below. • If you have used the “Chrome in a can” spray paint, I strongly suggest you do NOT apply a clear coat to this “chrome” paint. Personal experience resulted in the chrome finish turn grey with the clear coat applied. This will occur especially if it is not a UV rated clearcoat. Chrome and silvers will go greyish and some other colours will yellow. Reel painting tip. Credit Mike Donovan, SBR

sections.title: Hammertone
sections.text:
127 Ver. 25 • SBR member Scott Macleod has been advised by a Dupli Colour rep NOT to use High Heat Engine Enamel Clear on the paints. It will only dry once put in an oven over 200°C. Just use normal Clear Coat. Mower engines do not get hot enough to warrant this type of paint. • You can spray an alkyd-based clearcoat over an acrylic base paint, but NOT vice-versa. Acrylic paints dry quick (<2h), whereas alkyd-based paints need a day or 2 to fully harden and dry. Acrylic clearcoats on an acrylic paint is no problem though. • Enamel paint with a clear acrylic is not a good mix as the solvent in the acrylic will react with the enamel. Hammertone Spray painting hammertone paint is by far the best option. However, if you do not have access to a spray gun then the alternative is to apply it with a foam roller, or it has been suggested a 4mm nap 100m roller– thick coats are the way to get the hammered finish. If it is your first time – do a practice piece. Spraying: • Do not have the air pressure too high. (20 to 25psi max or 1 to 1.5 bar) • Move slowly but in even motions. • First light tack coat then second coat is a full wet coat. • No thinners! Brush or roller?: • Roller is better. Clear coating Hammertone Consensus and some members negative experience28 is NOT to clear coat hammertone finishes. Hammertone paints are already a gloss finish and putting a clear coat is likely to diminish the hammered effect. Further, I have read29 that hammertone paints have silicon in it which may affect adherence of the clear coat. Patina effect Strictly speaking a “patina” is a thin layer that forms on the surface of carbon steel with age and use. It can also refer to any fading, darkening or other signs of age, which are natural or even unavoidable. In the case of mowers some people are attracted to the aged, faded and slightly rusted finish found on 50 year old mowers. It is the distressed or aged look. To preserve this natural, or aged effect, treat all paint work for rust and clear coat. If it’s brown, its rust; rust is not patina but metal cancer. Rust treatment can include a light wire brushing and treatment with products such as Penetrol to stop and prevent further rust and to improve adhesion of the clear coat.

28 https://www.facebook.com/groups/1801521490103765/permalink/2117159708539940/?comment_tracking=%7B %22tn%22%3A%22O%22%7D accessed 10 July 2019 29 https://www.outdoorking.com/forum/ubbthreads.php?ubb=showflat&Number=35518 accessed 10 July 2019 Patina effect restoration. Credit Tim Fraser, SBR

128 Ver. 25 Instead of clear coat, a 50/50 mix of boiled linseed oil and “turps” has been suggested to get a more natural look. It’s what some “Rat Rod” car builders use on their cars. SBR member Angus West has provided the following guide for those wishing to keep an aged appearance. STEPS: 1. Strip your mower completely. 2. Degrease all patina finish parts. (I use Lanotec Citra force because I can buy it cheap for my businesses and it smells good). 3. Light scrub with steel wool and methylated spirits (to clear any loose material). 4. Dry it with clean cloths (I use microfibre). 5. For excessive rusting, apply some Ranex Rustbuster over the parts using a small cloth. Ranex is a fast-working rust treatment formulated to convert rust from a detrimental Iron Oxide to a beneficial Phosphate of Iron. Chemically destroying any rust present and leaving the surface in good condition. 6. Once it's clean and dry, apply Penetrol from a tin. Use a paint brush, lightly brush it on, throw the brush away once you're finished. You throw the brush away to avoid contamination (dirt etc). Penetrol from a tin is better than a can (from trial and error) as its sticky and overspray from the can is nasty. The liquid form will run into hard to reach places (watch out for drips) so brushing it is better for consistent coverage with this stuff. Buy cheap brushes, they do the job. Value packs are handy. If you plan properly, 2 brushes are all you'll need (2 coats). 7. It takes about 24 hours to dry (until it’s not sticky). Once it's dry, apply a small amount of methylated spirits to a cloth (I use microfibre) and give it a wipe down to clear any fresh dust etc, make sure your parts are dry, then apply a second coat of Penetrol the same way as step 6. 8. Once your second coat is dry (until its not sticky) apply your methylated spirits again for a small clean and dry it (I use microfibre). 9. Add any fresh decals now if you need. 10. Clear coat enamel gloss in a spray can as a topcoat, it's sticky so be careful of overspray. Spray all your parts, leave it to dry. 11. Once the clear coat on all your parts has dried (until its not sticky) you're all done and ready to reassemble your machine. COMMON QUESTIONS: What about twin rail reinforcement (e.g.kits from Core Earth Design)? Epoxy resin, stick this to the bottom of the frame. This is for the sole purpose of easy welding with no clamping or guess work (if you're not doing it) stitch weld is the best weld for the job. Intermittent stop start keeps the heat at bay and will cause the least amount of stress to the frame (warping etc) done properly there will be no evidence of a reinforcing kit. Done properly there will be no damage to your sealed patina finish. How clean does it need to be? What isn't cleaned off will be sealed. Clean it properly. If you can't clean something in a hard to reach place with methylated spirits, use the degreaser (Lanotec Citra Force has a fine nozzle). How long does the finish last?

sections.title: How do I repair the catcher?
sections.text:
129 Ver. 25 Anything will dull over time. However I'm yet to see any further signs of mower deterioration. I clean the mowers after each use with the blower to get the bulk of grass off. Then do a quick wipe over with natural baby wipes. I'm yet to have a reduction in shine. Why do you brush Penetrol but not the clear coat enamel gloss? Reasons to brush Penetrol are to get consistent coverage and controlled application. The overspray is very sticky. The clear coat enamel gloss is nowhere near as sticky as Penetrol. Do you apply Penetrol to the engine, roller, handle bars and cutting reel? Yes, you can Penetrol it all. Parts like the rear roller and cutting reel will still suffer wear and tear though far quicker than the painted parts you have sealed. It is your decision as to whether this is worth doing or not. Angus advises this has been his method through trial and error on other equipment prior to tackling the Scott Bonnar Model 45. This is the best method he has found through his personal experience. As always, use your brain and observe safety data sheets with the products and use correct PPE. How do I repair the catcher? Common for old reel mowers are dents and even holes in the catcher that will need to be repaired. The following suggestions should be read in the context of where the holes(usually caused by rust) are located. It will be up to you to decide the most cost effective option. • Take it to a professional panel beater. • Knock the dents out yourself with a “dolly, finishing off with body filler, sanding and priming. • A good welder may be able to spot weld the holes (depending on the condition of the metal). Then you will need to grind and sand. • A sheet metal business may be able to replace a damaged side. • Affix a plate and pop rivet it on the inside then apply body filler over the holes, give it a sand and a spray • Use a fibreglass repair kit to patch the holes then apply body filler, sand and prime • Raptor bed liner. Masking tape on the outside. Spray inside with zinc rich paint then hit it with a couple of coats of thinned Raptor. Let it dry. Remove the tape. Paint outside normally. You can paint the Raptor bed liner after its dry, or even get it tinted if you like. • Buy a new catcher.

Catcher with holes and dents Source, SBR

130 Ver. 25 The following has been offered by SBR member Steve Fembock, a panel beater who advises: • Lay the catcher on flat concrete and get in from the inside with a brickies bolster or a block of wood and gently beat out the catcher to its original shape first. • Then use the dolly on the inside and dress from the outside. • Go gently or you will stretch the metal and you will need an oxy to shrink it back. You will need to use filler to finish off. (Remember, panel beating is a skill that can take years to master).

Whether you first apply primer then filler or filler to bare metal and then prime; there is no right or wrong answer to this. It all comes down to the products you are using. Consideration should also be given to how you intend to coat the catcher, i.e., paint or powder coat.

Beware - some types of body-filler products are not suitable for use with powder coating. “JB Weld” (Steel reinforced cold-weld epoxy) has been identified as a product suitable for withstanding the heat involved in powder coating.

On the underside of the catcher there is a strengthening piece of angle running the width of the catcher. At the ends they may appear bent as in the below picture. This is a common occurrence; they get knocked and fold over with use. Simply use e.g., vice grips to gently straighten them.

.Panel repair -hammer and dolly set. Credit SCA Bent strengthening on the underside of a catcher. Credit Mason Peter SBR.

sections.title: Alternative to Red Plugs
sections.text:
131 Ver. 25

Alternative to Red Plugs Want an alternative to the red plugs for the chain cover and height adjuster? Simply get rubber grommets available from e.g. Clark Rubber. They come in black, black or black. Chain cover • 28 mm blind grommet Height adjuster (Brass) • 10 mm blind grommet • 19/9.5 mm pull through grommet. (better fit than the blind grommet) Height adjuster (alloy) • 13 mm blind grommet Remember this, the height adjusters came with different size plugs, hence one parts supplier supplies two different sized red plugs.

Grey rear roller On some of the Scott Bonnar catalogues the rear roller is often shown in a grey colour. As with other Scott Bonnar colours, the actual grey rear roller colour code or name is not known. To replicate the grey color the following colours have been suggested: • Paint the roller with a grey primer then a shale grey topcoat. • Supercheap mid grey has been used and looks a close match • Killrust (Wattyl) pewter. Vulcanised rear rollers If you are considering having the rear rollers vulcanised and you have a later model 45 (e.g. series 2, Red Rover) beware there is an issue with the gap between the rear rail and the roller, due to the rear rail having a larger drop at the rear. They are different in the front of the rear rail which doesn’t allow for much levelling adjustment once the thicker rubber is fitted. The alternative is to have thinner rubber applied, then there should be no problem. It is recommended that you consult with your supplier. Rear Roller restoration QUESTION: “What are people using to paint or finish the rear rollers? I will be rolling over concrete unfortunately.” ANSWER: Inevitably unless you are going to roll out the red carpet, the rear roller will suffer wear and tear. You can try different coatings to minimise the wear but ultimately it is going to happen. Black height adjuster and chain cover plugs Credit Kym Schrapel, SBR

132 Ver. 25 Originally out of the Thebarton factory they were painted grey on the outside as shown on the brochure in the “brief Model History” section in Chapter 2. Suggestions offered include: • Prime and paint them with: o Dupli Color Bed Armor polyurethane coating o Enamel paint and let nature take its course with wear and tear. o 2 pack Ute liner such as Raptor o To minimise the visual impact, if you intend to have a black topcoat then prime with a black primer rather than a grey primer. o If you paint the rear roller you can minimize the wear and tear by moving the mower over concrete/paved surfaces on a cheap moving dolly • The ultimate and perhaps foolproof method is to have the rear roller vulcanised with rubber by SB Fabrication and Parts.

SB Fabrication and Parts have developed a rear “wheelie bar” set up as shown opposite for the Model 45’s.

Due to the drive clutch setup on Models 33 and 40, a “Wheelie Bar” set up as shown opposite for the Model 45 is not easily done. One alternative for non-model 45’s is to make up a cradle (to sit the rear roller on) and roller/wheels. In the example below I used an old front roller.

Vulcanised rubber rear roller Credit SB Fabrication and Parts. Rear roller "Wheelie Bar". Credit George Bonmods, SBR

133 Ver. 25

To paint or not to paint the inside of the rear roller is a matter of personal choice. The rear rollers are normally cast iron although some are cast alloy (e.g. Mod 45, 240 volt electric rear rollers). Being cast iron they are relatively corrosion resistant (because of the carbon content) and why they have lasted 50 plus years without being painted. So stated, I prefer to treat the rust (with e.g. Ranex, wire brush, rust converter) and then a prime and paint.

If you are going to the trouble of painting the mower, then why not for completeness paint the inside of the rear rollers.

Above Left and right - Home-made cradle/roller to transport mover over rough surfaces Credit Kym Schrapel, SBR Inside the rear roller Credit Matt Farley, SBR

sections.title: Grey rear roller
sections.text:
131 Ver. 25

Alternative to Red Plugs Want an alternative to the red plugs for the chain cover and height adjuster? Simply get rubber grommets available from e.g. Clark Rubber. They come in black, black or black. Chain cover • 28 mm blind grommet Height adjuster (Brass) • 10 mm blind grommet • 19/9.5 mm pull through grommet. (better fit than the blind grommet) Height adjuster (alloy) • 13 mm blind grommet Remember this, the height adjusters came with different size plugs, hence one parts supplier supplies two different sized red plugs.

Grey rear roller On some of the Scott Bonnar catalogues the rear roller is often shown in a grey colour. As with other Scott Bonnar colours, the actual grey rear roller colour code or name is not known. To replicate the grey color the following colours have been suggested: • Paint the roller with a grey primer then a shale grey topcoat. • Supercheap mid grey has been used and looks a close match • Killrust (Wattyl) pewter. Vulcanised rear rollers If you are considering having the rear rollers vulcanised and you have a later model 45 (e.g. series 2, Red Rover) beware there is an issue with the gap between the rear rail and the roller, due to the rear rail having a larger drop at the rear. They are different in the front of the rear rail which doesn’t allow for much levelling adjustment once the thicker rubber is fitted. The alternative is to have thinner rubber applied, then there should be no problem. It is recommended that you consult with your supplier. Rear Roller restoration QUESTION: “What are people using to paint or finish the rear rollers? I will be rolling over concrete unfortunately.” ANSWER: Inevitably unless you are going to roll out the red carpet, the rear roller will suffer wear and tear. You can try different coatings to minimise the wear but ultimately it is going to happen. Black height adjuster and chain cover plugs Credit Kym Schrapel, SBR

132 Ver. 25 Originally out of the Thebarton factory they were painted grey on the outside as shown on the brochure in the “brief Model History” section in Chapter 2. Suggestions offered include: • Prime and paint them with: o Dupli Color Bed Armor polyurethane coating o Enamel paint and let nature take its course with wear and tear. o 2 pack Ute liner such as Raptor o To minimise the visual impact, if you intend to have a black topcoat then prime with a black primer rather than a grey primer. o If you paint the rear roller you can minimize the wear and tear by moving the mower over concrete/paved surfaces on a cheap moving dolly • The ultimate and perhaps foolproof method is to have the rear roller vulcanised with rubber by SB Fabrication and Parts.

SB Fabrication and Parts have developed a rear “wheelie bar” set up as shown opposite for the Model 45’s.

Due to the drive clutch setup on Models 33 and 40, a “Wheelie Bar” set up as shown opposite for the Model 45 is not easily done. One alternative for non-model 45’s is to make up a cradle (to sit the rear roller on) and roller/wheels. In the example below I used an old front roller.

Vulcanised rubber rear roller Credit SB Fabrication and Parts. Rear roller "Wheelie Bar". Credit George Bonmods, SBR

133 Ver. 25

To paint or not to paint the inside of the rear roller is a matter of personal choice. The rear rollers are normally cast iron although some are cast alloy (e.g. Mod 45, 240 volt electric rear rollers). Being cast iron they are relatively corrosion resistant (because of the carbon content) and why they have lasted 50 plus years without being painted. So stated, I prefer to treat the rust (with e.g. Ranex, wire brush, rust converter) and then a prime and paint.

If you are going to the trouble of painting the mower, then why not for completeness paint the inside of the rear rollers.

Above Left and right - Home-made cradle/roller to transport mover over rough surfaces Credit Kym Schrapel, SBR Inside the rear roller Credit Matt Farley, SBR

sections.title: Damaged Chain Cover
sections.text:
134 Ver. 25 Damaged Chain Cover Chain covers, like the catcher typically are found with dents and even holes or splits, particularly on the underside.

There are two options: • Buy a new one (only Model 45 chain covers are available after market). • Strip it back, knock the dents out then bog it up and sand it smooth before you paint it. If it is split then weld up the split, being mindful that the metal is thin. Using “JB Weld” is also an option to fill holes (especially if the cover is to be powder coated).

Positioning of Decals The below information is based on original decal positioning. It is noted that for example on the Model 38 Series 2 Edger a variance of up to 10 mm has been found with the placement of the original decals. Allowing for the human factor in putting them on, plus or minus 10 mm is not a big deal. When placing reproduction decals, you may have to factor into the positioning the possible different size of the reproduction decal compared to the original.

Chain cover being repaired Credit Tony Bear, SBR

sections.title: Catcher
sections.text:
135 Ver. 25 Catcher Scott Bonnar Decal The “Scott Bonnar” catcher decal distance from the top edge of the catcher to the top of the original decal is 200 mm. It is positioned centrally. The side measurements will vary with catcher size and with the width of the reproduction decal.

The Pentagon shaped decal is 130 mm from the top of the catcher to the top of the decal and is positioned centrally. For a 14” catcher it is 115 mm from the catcher side to the point of the decal as below. Positioning of catcher decal Credit Nev Gordon, SBR Positioning Pentagon catcher decal on a 14”catcher Credit Scott Macleod, SBR

sections.title: Rover Decal
sections.text:
136 Ver. 25 Rover Decal The Rover catcher decal goes diagonally across the catcher. Positioning is as per the below pictures.

Chain Cover Model 33 - Scott Bonnar From the bottom edge of the catcher to the red shadow outline of the decal measures 125mm. Again, position it centrally, lining up the middle line if the “SB” with the apex of the chain cover. The original decal was 73 mm from the right-hand point to the very edge of the cover when placed against a straight edge (e.g. a wall). Allowing for the lip on the cover the measurement will be about 70 mm, however this may vary depending on the reproduction decal. 175mm down for Rover Select Decal Credit Nev Gordon, SBR 105mm down from front lip of catcher for Rover Credit Nev Gordon, SBR Chain cover decal Credit Warren Hill, SBR

sections.title: Model 40 – Scott Bonnar
sections.text:
137 Ver. 25 Note: these measurements are from a decal which does not have the “saw blades” around the middle “SB”.

Model 40 – Scott Bonnar Not all Model 40 chain covers came with a decal. For those that did the decal is approximately 160mm from the top of the cover to the decal. Model 43 - Scott Bonnar Centrally position the decal as best as possible (note the cover is not symmetrically shaped). From the top apex of the cover the top flat edge of the decal is 180 mm.

Model 45 - Rover Decal The decal is centrally positioned, use the middle of the “V” and the apex of the chain case. A hard one to measure but approx. 17mm from centre of the hole , across at a 45 deg angle to top part of decal above the “V.” Chain cover decal Credit Warren Hill, SBR Mod 33 Chain cover original decal Credit Andy Hill, SBR Mod 40 Chain cover original decal Credit Ollie Morris, SBR

sections.title: Model 45 - Scott Bonnar
sections.text:
138 Ver. 25

Model 45 - Scott Bonnar As of the release of Version 6.0, no specific measurements have been received as to the decal placement on the chain cover. For twin rail mowers, one indicator has however been identified as to the decal placement – that is the top of the decal appears to be in line with the left hand/front apex of the cover as indicated in the below picture. For solid deck, the mounting holes appear to be a guide i.e. bottom of left-hand hole for the top of the decal and top of the right-hand hole for the bottom of the decal as indicated in the below right picture. It is noted that not all Model 45’s had chain cover decals out of the factory.

Rover Chain cover decal Credit Nev Gordon, SBR Solid deck chain cover Credit Boyd Townsend, SBR Model 45 - original factory-made survivor Credit Outdoorking.com

sections.title: Model 38 Edger
sections.text:
139 Ver. 25 Model 38 Edger Warning decal The warning decal on the Model 38 came in two styles, either the red on white “DANGER” or the black on white “CAUTION” decal. Viewing several Model 38’s indicates there are several options for the placing of these decals. The “DANGER” decal can be affixed on the blade cover: • On the top or horizontal surface, centrally positioned so that it faces the operator. • On the rear more vertical surface, so it faces the operator as shown in the below picture.

Above and below - “DANGER” decal Credit George Bonmods, SBR DANGER decal positioning Credit Alec Holmes, SBR

140 Ver. 25 The “Caution” decal similarly appears in several locations. • On the upper section of the engine cowling facing the operator • Sideways facing the engine on the rear more vertical surface • On the middle of the inner horizontal guard facing forward.

There is another “CAUTION” decal which currently I do not have a clear image of. It appears below. CAUTION decal positioning Credit Josh Smith, SBR Above - CAUTION decal on engine cowling Credit Tim Hugo, SBR CAUTION decal Credit Kym Schrapel, SBR Right – incomplete CAUTION decal positioning Credit SBR

sections.title: How to affix decals
sections.text:
143 Ver. 25

How to affix decals SBR member Cameron McNamara has provided the following basic step by step method (and the pictures) for applying stickers/decals. These steps, together with the above measurements may be used for positioning any of the decals so they are level and centrally located. Above - Mod 38 Series 2 positioning. Note slight variance compared to above versions. Credit Chris Pethick, SBR

144 Ver. 25

1. Tape the decal in place.

2. Tape the middle of the decal.

3. Tear or carefully cut the tape on one end - gives you your line up.

4. Pull back half the decal.

5. Either tuck under backing paper (preferred way - works on larger prints so you can just keep it tapped and keep pulling the paper) or on something little cut the paper.

6. Line up your cut tape and apply that half.

7. Tear or cut the other end. Then remove rest of backing paper.

Applying decals Steps 1 & 2 Credit Cameron McNamara, SBR Applying decals Step 3 Credit Cameron McNamara, SBR Applying decals Step 4 Credit Cameron McNamara e, SBR Applying decals Step 5 Credit Cameron McNamara e, SBR

145 Ver. 25

8. Line tape registration mark back up and apply.

9. Remove application tape.

10. Job done.

Applying decals Step 6 Credit Cameron McNamara e, SBR Applying decals Step7 Credit Cameron McNamara e, SBR Applying decals Step 8 Credit Cameron McNamara, SBR Applying decals Step 9 Credit Cameron McNamara e, SBR Applying decals Step 10 Credit Cameron McNamara, SBR

sections.title: How do I clean a carburetor?
sections.text:
146 Ver. 25

How do I clean a carburetor? “Rookie question but how do you clean your carburetor if it's blocked?” One piece of advice with two options as to the products to be used: Take it apart and clean with: • Petrol • Carby/throttle body cleaner from an automotive parts supplier Note: keep carburetor cleaner type products away from plastics or rubber, it is not good for them. Which way does the needle go? If you have disassembled the carburetor and removed the needle you may ask “which way does the needle go?” The needle or pointed end goes in first remembering to ensure the seat is still in there.

How do I restore ID Plates? For those wishing to restore their brass plates there are basically two options.

• Clean off (paint stripper) all the paint and simply polish with the likes of Brasso.

• Alternatively, (and the next step) is to spray with light coats of red paint (even heard of nail polish being used); let it dry and cure and then use 2000 - 2500 grit sandpaper on a block and sand off the raised parts. Finish off by polishing with Brasso and optionally a clear coat. If you choose to use a clear coat; ensure it is compatible with the base coat.

Before and after - brass ID Plate Credit Kym Schrapel SBR. Carburetor needle Credit Damien Cooke, SBR

sections.title: Engine Issues
sections.text:
147 Ver. 25 Fixing the plate to the mower – use a 1/8” or 3.2 mm pop rivets (stainless steel are ideal).

Unfortunately, if you have one of the screen-printed alloy plates, sorry, they are not recoverable using the above method. The only known restoration method is to get a sticker printed out of laminated vinyl. Basically, you clean up the plate and place the sticker over where the paint was. The drawback is the cost.

Engine Issues Cylinder head clean up A clean cylinder head is crucial to good ignition and to prolong engine life. Considering the Kirby and Briggs and Stratton motors on the old Scott Bonnars can be over 50 years old, carbon buildup is to be expected. You may find a buildup on the piston and the valves which will affect the engines compression. Clean up is simple and inexpensive.

• Remove the spark plug and engine cowling. • Unbolt and remove the cylinder head. o Be careful not to damage the head gasket otherwise you will need to get a new one. • Use degreaser first then clean with abrasive nylon brush or a fine wire brush in a drill (or even an old toothbrush will do) and then a final clean with carby cleaner. • Assembly is the reverse. • Don’t be too worried if you don’t have a torque wrench to tighten the head bolts, do them up tight you should be right. If you have a torque wrench see below. • Remember the cylinder heads are aluminum so they are easy to crack or warp if you over torque/tighten.

If your engine has an aluminum block, a nylon brush as shown opposite, may be used rather than a wire brush (suitable for steal block).

Nylon cup brush suitable for cylinder head clean up. Credit Phil Wood, SBR Before and after head cleanup Credit Ashley Rabbett, SBR Replica sticker ID Plate Credit Joseph Marano, SBR

sections.text:

sections.title: Cylinder head fitting
sections.text:
148 Ver. 25

Cylinder head fitting After cleaning the block and head of your Briggs and Stratton engine, ideally you should properly torque the head bolts. That is tightening the bolts in the correct sequence to the correct torque. Below picture shows the sequence for the series 8 and 9 motors. The Briggs and Stratton website has information for other motors. Torque should be 15.8 Nm.

Tip: use a piece of cardboard with holes punched in it to replicate the bolt pattern. That way they go back in the same hole they came out of.

Left – Briggs & Stratton Tightening sequence. The 3 arrows denote the longer bolts ( if you have them ) as they go around the exhaust port Credit Nev Gordon, SBR Right - Use piece of cardboard to replicate the original bolt placement Credit Nev Gordon, SBR Kirby engine - head bolt tightening sequence Credit Outdoorking.com

sections.title: Clean the inside of the fuel tank
sections.text:
149 Ver. 25

Clean the inside of the fuel tank Due to often years of sitting idle, the fuel tank may become corroded or just simply dirty inside. As part of the restoration you may need to clean it out, the following are several suggestions: • carby cleaner • you can also use petrol and a tip, add a handful of fish tank pebbles as well, put the tank cap on block other holes up and shake side to side. • a Citric Acid mix and then thoroughly rinse it out. • Put 1 scoop of Napisan Oxy Action in tank and half fill with boiling water, let stand for an hour and rinse out. Removal of the flywheel Several methods have been suggested to remove the flywheel off the engine: • Using a pinch bar, wedge between fins then use a punch or even a large bolt and hammer hitting anti clockwise on the indicated area (picture below left) and pry off from behind. Take care not to break the fins. • Another method is to use a large pair of stilsons on the flywheel and holding the crankshaft with a shifter or spanner. Then undo the cast housing, as if it is a “big nut” (picture below).

Flywheel removal, Credit Scott Macleod, SBR

sections.text:

sections.title: Engine flush
sections.text:
150 Ver. 25 Engine flush It is advisable to check the condition of the engine oil to determine whether a simple oil change will suffice, or a flush is required, particularly in poorly maintained engines or those that have been sitting unused for an extended period. . A good engine flush can help loosen deposits and dissolve sludge, it cleans up the engine components; keeps the new oil clear from dirt and other particles. Flushing the engine is simple: • Run the engine for a short period so that it is warm. • Drain the old oil. • Refill with oil (some people suggest a “flushing oil”)l. Run engine until hot. Let cool a bit then drain. Refill with correct grade engine oil. • If you change the oil often, the inside of the engine will clean up anyway. If the condition of the oil is such that it is severely contaminated and is the consistency of e.g. custard or treacle, you can try and thin it by adding diesel or kerosene so it flows out the drain plug; or worst case scenario a complete strip down i.e. removal of crankcase may be required. Removing the flywheel from a Kirby engine is slightly different. As in the picture opposite, put the nut back on so it sits flush with end of thread and hit it with a hammer, and it will pop off. Just don’t go silly, it’s more about giving it a shock, not beating the &%$ out of it!

Flywheel removal - Stillson method, Credit Joshua Forward, SBR. Kirby flywheel. Credit Tim Hugo, SBR

sections.title: Custom painting new Briggs and Stratton engines
sections.text:
151 Ver. 25 Custom painting new Briggs and Stratton engines New Briggs and Stratton engines are predominantly black with some red on the cowling. Some people may want to keep the feel of the older Briggs and Stratton engine keeping the “Chevy orange” colour, others may want to totally customize the engine paint scheme to match the rest of the mower. SBR member Luke Duke has set the bar with his custom engine and complete mower paint schemes. The following are the steps he takes to break down the main, basic parts of the new engine for custom painting: • Remove the air filter cowling and filter. • Remove the Exhaust cowling. • Remove the Fuel tank. • Remove the plastic throttle knob. • Remove (refer to How to remove Decals) the “throttle sticker” highlighted opposite, to get the plastic cowling off - there is a screw behind it. • Remove the plastic trim “behind/around” the throttle. • Remove the red pull start mechanism - 3 bolts (do NOT take out the recoil spring from behind this). • Remove the red metal cowling behind pull start mechanism. Reassembly is the reverse of the above steps. Remember to put the pull start on the right way - the pull cord faces the rear of the motor. Tips for re-painting the new engine: • Give the surfaces to be painted a light, fine sanding and then etch prime to give the new paint something to adhere to. • Thread strips of paper through the slots and between the recoil spring to keep paint off the recoil spring behind it.

Remove the sticker circled Credit Luke Duke, SBR Custom “Bulldogs” theme painted B&S engine Credit Luke Duke, SBR

sections.title: What is this? – Cutter bearing housing lubrication point.
sections.text:
152 Ver. 25

What is this? – Cutter bearing housing lubrication point. On the top half of the cutter bearing housing there appears a brass fitting with a small ball bearing in it. This is simply a lubrication point from the days when bearings were not of the sealed type and required lubrication. You may have one that has been damaged, and the ball bearing is missing. Later models or those with replacement housings may not have these. Either way with the use of sealed bearings this lubrication point has become redundant. If you have them and sealed bearings then leave then, they keep the original look. If the ball is missing, a dollop of silicone in the hole will prevent the ingress of dirt/debris to the bearing.

Cutter bearing housing lubrication point. Credit James McManus, SBR Custom “Chevy orange” painted new B&S. Credit Michael McGerr, SBR Above left and right. Masking of the pull start cover. Credit Luke Duke, SBR

sections.title: Clutch Operating Sleeve – Broken Roll Pin
sections.text:
153 Ver. 25

Clutch Operating Sleeve – Broken Roll Pin During the tear down the roll pin (part 30 opposite) in the clutch operating sleeve (part 29 opposite and pictured below) may be found to be broken or missing. You do not need to replace the whole sleeve, simply remove and replace the roll pin. Roll pins can be sourced from the likes of Repco and Supercheap Auto in blister pack of various diameter sizes. You will have to cut the pin to the right length. I believe it is 2.5 mm diameter (unconfirmed).

Operating sleeve clutch. Extract of parts diagram Credit, SBR files The Clutch Operating Sleeve (number 29 from above diagram) with a roll pin to be fitted. Credit Daniel James, SBR).

sections.title: Reel issues
sections.text:
154 Ver. 25 Reel issues Worn reel shaft. If, during the tear down process you find the reel shaft is worn where the reel bearing is located you have two options: • Purchase a new reel (budget permitting); or • Fill the worn area with weld and machine the shaft back to the same diameter as the rest of the shaft. Do not machine down the shaft and put a sleeve on, you will also have to recut a keyway. Then the keyway will be halfway through the sleeve and halfway through the reel shaft placing pressure on the key and the keyway to keep things where they should be once things are in drive.

When to Grind or Sharpen the reel Should I get the reel sharpened first then paint or paint then sharpen? This is a conundrum faced during the restoration process. The options are: Grind then paint: • Pros: o Heat to the blade during grinding does not affect the paint/powder coat finish. o Repairs can be made to the reel without affecting the finish of the reel. • Con: o Painting or powder coat will be applied to the freshly ground reel blades covering the edge meaning the paint/powder coat will have to be scrapped off the ground surfaces. Paint then grind: • Pros: o No need to remove paint or powder coat from the cutting edge as it will be ground off. • Con: o Sharpening causes heating of the reel blades which may affect the painted finish. o Means the sharpener prior to the grinding may have to scrape the paint off the blade face as it gets stuck on the grinding wheel. o imagine paint on your scissors. The answer is not simple, some experienced restorers have advised to paint then grind. However, some industry insiders who earn a living from sharpening prefer grinding on unpainted reels/blades. If you want to paint the blade holder without taking the blade off you can always tape over the blade. Worn reel shaft, Credit Jayden Pulford, SBR

sections.title: Can the reel be sharpened before installation?
sections.text: 155 Ver. 25 Ultimately the choice is yours. If your reel requires repairing (e.g. replacing a broken blade) or you are replacing the bottom blade, then it will be more convenient to have it repaired and ground at the same time and then painted. Can the reel be sharpened before installation? Alternatively, this question can also be asked as “does the reel and bedknife need to be on the mower for sharpening or can I get this done before re-assembly?” Answer: The reel and bedknife/blade can either be sharpened on the machine or off it. If they are off the mower they will be put into a machine to grind. If in doubt – ask the business who is to do the grind, it will depend on their equipment. Can I sharpen the reel myself? If you are tempted to make a jig for an angle grinder and try to do the grinding/sharpening yourself; the simple advice is DON’T. For the relatively cheap cost of sharpening take it to a professional and get it done properly without the risk of ruining the reel or bedknife. The only “sharpening” that can be done at home is the facing of the bedknife with a “Bedknife Buddy” which is described in Maintenance.

sections.title: CHAPTER 8 - THE REBUILD
sections.text:
156 Ver. 25

CHAPTER 8 - THE REBUILD

sections.title: Rebuild Steps
sections.text:
157 Ver. 25 Rebuild Steps The rebuild procedure is summarized as follows: • Build the frame (if solid deck including the front roller bracket) o fit height adjuster on left lower side. • Rear rollers • Bed knife • Front roller • Reel • Invert the mower so it is sitting upside down on the deck/rails and adjust the rear roller to the bedknife and front roller. • Sprockets • Primary Clutch assembly • Motor • Chains • Adjust o Reel to Bed knife o Chain tension o Cutting height o Both Clutches • Finally check all your nuts have been tightened before starting the mower. Rebuild tips. • To avoid paint/powder coat damage use flat washers under the nuts against those surfaces. o New flat washers tend to have a rounded face and a much sharper flat face. Put the rounded face to the paint to reduce the likelihood of the washer damaging the surface. • Apply an anti-seize compound (e.g. Loctite) or lightly grease the bolts prior to fixing – makes them easier to fit and at some time in the future easier to remove. o Blue Loctite 243 is a medium strength thread locker which is tolerant of oil and contaminants. Parts can be disassembled with hand tools. Suitable for use with stainless steel. o If using grease, It is advisable to use a moly based grease for stainless steel fixings. • Do not fully tighten bolts until you have all your bottom gear in place - makes it easier to slide things in when the bolts have not been tightened up.

During the rebuild there is a lot to do, and many a rookie has found some simple things get overlooked. To avoid the embarrassment of making a “rookie mistake”: • If buying a new engine, ensure you get the correct shaft size to match your engine side clutch half. • Ensure oil holes are facing upwards: o Thrust pad. o Bearing holders • Engine or PTO (self-aligning bearing) holder • Rear bearing holders

158 Ver. 25 • The cutter bearing shields are installed the correct way. • New engines come without oil – put the recommended oil in it. • Do not ever, ever pull the guts out of the recoil starter unless you absolutely must!! • Make sure you put decals the right way up, not upside down (it has happened). • Handlebars go on the inside of the side plates and if the bottom half is on the wrong way around it will lean back too much. • “Noticed a lot of the bolts were too tight to fit” - Remember you will have a mix of UNC and UNF nuts/bolts. • Rear roller bearing holders; the non-slotted one with the spacer goes on the “chain” side. • Once the engine is installed if you must lean the mower back - do it when you are changing the oil with a dry sump; or do not lean it past 45 degrees.

sections.title: Thread sizes (UPDATED)
sections.text:
162 Ver. 25 Note: the above fixing lists were compiled several years ago and was based on common fasteners that were easy to obtain. Keeping the right head, thread etc. is the hard part to find. For ease, convenience and to get the era correct heads it is suggested that the nut/bolt kits supplied by SB Cete Parts and others be considered rather than sourcing them yourself. Thread sizes (UPDATED) In addition to the above Fixings Lists, the following are some additional thread sizes. Description Thread Chain cover screws on Mod 45 solid deck 1/4 UNC X 1/2" (gutter bolts will do) Carburetor to engine on 3hp B&S 5/16 UNC Cotter pin 3/8 UNF (replacement nut) Height adjustment knob 3/8 UNF, thread pitch of 24 per inch or 1.058mm Engine side clutch half bolt

5/16 UNC Clutch lever knob/ball 3/8 UNC Rear roller shaft 1/2” UNF 20 TPI Clutch lever adjuster

Credit Chris Young, SBR 1/4 inch BSF thread M6 has been reported as fitting. Remember the Rover Model 45’s was not threaded Rover – catcher rubber bolt

Credit David Rees, SBR The bolt that retains the rubbers is a 3/8 x 1-1/4 UNC hex bolt and nut. The Rovers also have a inter spacer tube. Ratchet box retaining bolt (into the rear roller) 1/4" X 1/2" UNC Model 33 and 40 cutter adjustment screws

5/16" UNC. 1.1/2" and 1.1/4" lengths. Model 38 Cover plate mounting screws 3/16 x 12mm Clutch operating bolt 1/4 UNF X 31/4 inch long, or can replace it with M6 X 80mm long if you get the nylon lock nut Counterweight 5/16 x 1 UNC Ratchet Box 1/4 x 1/2 UNC Bolts that attach the carby to the engine ¼ UNC

Credit Ronald Lau, SBR

sections.title: What is this part called?
sections.text:
163 Ver. 25 What is this part called? Particularly for the novice, during a rebuild you may come across some small parts which you may not know the name of. Those parts may be broken, missing or have been “misplaced”. Name Example of where used Image Chain half link On any of the chains

Circlip or retaining ring May be used on the height adjuster assembly in lieu of the spring style retaining clip (26).

Model 33 - inter sprocket assembly.

Roll pin

As found in the large intermediate sprocket assembly (30)

Shaft ring

The clutch fork pivot pin which goes through the clutch fork assembly is secured by shaft rings (24)

164 Ver. 25 Name Example of where used Image Split pin Mod 33, clutch rod to clutch handle

Square shaft key

Clutch body key (31) 3/16 X 3/16 key steel

Woodruff key On the engine or PTO shaft (13 and 14)

V-Ring V-ring or V seal as found on the PTO shaft against the bearing housing. Size is “V20”

Should you require any of the above, you may use one of the popular suppliers who are supporters of the SBR page. Alternatively, and if you are in immediate need of the parts, they can be sourced quicker from a large hardware store (e.g. Bunnings or Mitre 10) or specialist suppliers (e.g. CBC Bearings or Blackwoods). If in doubt let Google be your friend. Remember, the above come in various sizes.

sections.title: Bearing Part Numbers
sections.text:
165 Ver. 25 Bearing Part Numbers

Bearing Numbers - Source Ryan McClure SBR Which Bearing? Rule of thumb – the cutter reel, PTO, rear roller and clutch thrust bearings are the same across Models 33, 40 and 45. BUT there are some issues to be aware of, especially when it comes to ordering new bearings. • Original rear roller bearings (Anthony brand) are NOT available from bearing suppliers. They are now a specialty part available from the usual SBR page suppliers as a sleeved bearing. o If you try and source them elsewhere you may get a bearing with the right internal diameter but the outside diameter will probably be slightly oversize and will not fit in the bearing holders. • Clutch thrust bearings are similarly not available from bearing suppliers, again they are Scott Bonnar specific. • Early Model 45 solids had a different intermediate sprocket bearing. o 42mm (early solid) OD or 47mm OD (later solid to rover 45) • Early Model 45 Unicorns and even some 20” Twin rails had a larger reel bearing, the RMS5 (still available) – treat the bearing holders like gold, they are NOT available. If you damage the bearing holder the only alternative is to replace the complete reel bearing holder AND bearing to take the standard RLS5 bearing. • Model 33 o The thrust race for the rear roller is not available as a genuine spare part, again treat it like gold. Advice recently received from SBR member Adam Blaylock, that a Timken T77- 904A1 will fit. Alternatively the primary clutch thrust bearing will fit with the internal sleeve turned down fractionally. o The belt tensioner takes two 6202 bearings.

Original "Anthony" brand rear roller bearing Credit Ash Power, SBR

sections.text:

sections.title: Large Intermediate Sprocket bearing
sections.text:
166 Ver. 25 • Model 430 (Diplomat) If you want to replace all the bearings you will need: o 5 x 6202 (reel, rear roller and PTO) o 1 x 6005 (drive It is advisable that you check which bearings you have (do not assume), especially on 20” Model 45’s. When ordering, check with and advise the supplier which model you have and if possible specify what bearings you have and want replaced. Large Intermediate Sprocket bearing During a tear down take notice of your intermediate sprocket assembly. They came in two versions as shown below. Note the differenced in the sleeve and three versus four welds..

Earlier models have a thinner sleeve and should have the 6004 bearing instead of the 6204. Andrew Lengkeek advises the 6004 (20x42x12)instead of the 6204(20x47x14) for the big sprocket, the 6003 go on the intermediate bracket and are all the same with 45s. Sprockets that take the 6004 have a fair bit of a lip on them so that’s probably where they make up the extra 2mm.

Difference between intermediate sprockets Credit Chris Trigger, SBR.

sections.title: Model 45 Chain sizes
sections.text:
167 Ver. 25 Model 45 Chain sizes The following details are as per GA Power Equipment Spares30. Engine to cutter 80P 3/8" X 7/32" . GA Spares part # CHM1863 (Includes joiner in pin count. Use offset link chain no #06B 3/8" X 7/32" Intermediate drive chain 59P 1/2" X 3/16". . GA Spares part # CHM1864 (Includes joiner in pin count. Joiner link only chain no #415 1/2" X 3/16". Offset / half link only chain no #415 1/2" X 3/16") Final drive chain 39P 1/2" X 3/16". GA Spares part # CHM1865 (Includes joiner in pin count. Chain offset link chain No #415H 1/2" X 3/16". Chain connecting link chain No #415H 1/2" X 3/16" Miscellaneous How do I put the handle grips on? Because the old, genuine grips are often hard they can be difficult to remove/fit. The new, aftermarket grips which have a snug fit can also be problematic in putting them on. Irrespective of whether the grips are old or new the same applies. Again, there are several alternatives: Installation • Hairspray, lubricates to help slip on, dries like a glue. • dishwashing liquid • slide a zip tie up the inside of the grip this gives you a small air pocket, so the grip does not seal tight. Slide it on and pull zip tie out. • Use an air compressor and a blower gun - blow inside the grip as u slide it on.

Handlebar extension plates When converting from the Kirby to Briggs and Stratton engines some of the Model 45 handlebars without the kick out will hit the engine. Short of replacing the handlebars (with the kick out) the solution is to fit a set of extension plates. The below diagram is provided for those handy enough to make a set. If you are not, several SBR members make them so search the page.

30 https://www.gaspares.com.au/search?q=&per_page=24&product_page=1&search%5Bcategory_url%5D=%2F_suit s%2Fscottbonnar accessed 29 July 2020 Handlebar grips Credit Tim Hugo, SBR

30 https://www.gaspares.com.au/search?q=&per_page=24&product_page=1&search%5Bcategory_url%5D=%2F_suit s%2Fscottbonnar accessed 29 July 2020 Handlebar grips Credit Tim Hugo, SBR

sections.title: How do I put the handle grips on?
sections.text:
167 Ver. 25 Model 45 Chain sizes The following details are as per GA Power Equipment Spares30. Engine to cutter 80P 3/8" X 7/32" . GA Spares part # CHM1863 (Includes joiner in pin count. Use offset link chain no #06B 3/8" X 7/32" Intermediate drive chain 59P 1/2" X 3/16". . GA Spares part # CHM1864 (Includes joiner in pin count. Joiner link only chain no #415 1/2" X 3/16". Offset / half link only chain no #415 1/2" X 3/16") Final drive chain 39P 1/2" X 3/16". GA Spares part # CHM1865 (Includes joiner in pin count. Chain offset link chain No #415H 1/2" X 3/16". Chain connecting link chain No #415H 1/2" X 3/16" Miscellaneous How do I put the handle grips on? Because the old, genuine grips are often hard they can be difficult to remove/fit. The new, aftermarket grips which have a snug fit can also be problematic in putting them on. Irrespective of whether the grips are old or new the same applies. Again, there are several alternatives: Installation • Hairspray, lubricates to help slip on, dries like a glue. • dishwashing liquid • slide a zip tie up the inside of the grip this gives you a small air pocket, so the grip does not seal tight. Slide it on and pull zip tie out. • Use an air compressor and a blower gun - blow inside the grip as u slide it on.

Handlebar extension plates When converting from the Kirby to Briggs and Stratton engines some of the Model 45 handlebars without the kick out will hit the engine. Short of replacing the handlebars (with the kick out) the solution is to fit a set of extension plates. The below diagram is provided for those handy enough to make a set. If you are not, several SBR members make them so search the page.

sections.title: How do I grease a bearing?
sections.text:
168 Ver. 25

Handlebar extension plates Credit Ryan McClure, SBR

How do I grease a bearing? If you have a sealed bearing you do not have to grease it, being sealed it is greased for life. However, if you have on the PTO shaft a self-aligning type as shown below then you should grease it. Also, the clutch thrust bearing requires greasing. Remember that overfilling the bearing cavity with grease will lead to increased temperatures and possible bearing failure. When greasing a bearing, be sure to leave enough space in the housing so that grease can be ejected from the bearing during start-up.

sections.title: Joining a chain
sections.text:
169 Ver. 25 Wearing rubber gloves, place a glob of grease in the palm of your hand and press the bearing into the grease working all the way around it. Clean up excess grease, leaving a smear on the outer and inner surfaces.

Joining a chain

When fitting any of the chains, the circlip of the joining link should be fitted so the closed end faces the direction of travel as shown opposite.

Fitting the front roller bracket. Or “how does the front roller bracket come out? Do you just pull the sides out i.e. stretch it open?”. Thanks to George Bonmods for the following tip: • To spread the chassis on a twin rail to replace the front roller bracket use a Quick Grip clamp, reverse the heads (to spread rather than clamp)and gently spread the sides. Self-aligning PTO bearing. Credit Jason Brindley, SBR Chain joining link fitted Credit Michael Hanes, SBR Quick Grip clamp Credit GeorgeBonMods, SBR

sections.title: Rubber PTO Bearing Shield
sections.text:
170 Ver. 25 Rubber PTO Bearing Shield QUESTION: Is this rubber gasket (shown opposite) required with a sealed bearing? ANSWER: Yes. Irrespective of whether a self-aligning or sealed bearing is used on the PTO shaft, the rubber bearing shield MUST be fitted between the bearing and the side plate or frame. The bearing when fitted should be fully inside the holder such that the rubber bearing shield takes up the gap as it acts as a spacer preventing the bearing moving back and forth in the housing. Handlebars Model 45’s came with two styles of handlebars, the “X-wing” and the Pram bars as on Craftsman models. The Pram style reportedly can feel too low, especially for those taller people. Within the “X-wing” handlebars there are two styles: • Straight • Bent(kick out at the bottom) to accommodate the larger fuel tanks. The bent version is slightly longer by about 100mm, as such they are often sought by taller persons who find the extra length more comfortable. • They are paired . Straight bars have a straight top connection at the plate, meaning the tabs extra lugs are there. i.e.. You cannot switch the straight bottoms with bent tops . And vice-versa. The bottom half of the X-wing bars are width specific i.e. 14” will not fit a 17”, in other words they are not interchangeable.

Rubber bearing shield. Credit Gavin Hackett, SBR X-wing handlebars. Bent (Left) and straight (right). Credit Peter Hayes, SBR Pram handlebars Credit Chris Thompson, SBR

171 Ver. 25 However, where the top half of the bars connect to the lower bars there are two sizes. Solid bars are wider than the twin version, having a “crush sleeve” or in layman’s terms a spacer. Twin rails don’t have the sleeve/spacer and the bottom bars tabs are closer as the sleeve/spacer isn’t there. The top half is therefore interchangeable so long as it is solid to solid and twin to twin. Bars are paired sets . Don’t mix them up.

Handlebars from a 45 Solid showing the crush sleeve or spacer. Credit Aaron Clayton, SBR

sections.title: Rubber clutch handle grips (UPDATED)
sections.text:
172 Ver. 25 Rubber clutch handle grips (UPDATED) Rubber grips on the left hand clutch handle/lever were fitted on Model 33’s, Model 40’s and 240 volt electric Model 45’s.They were not fitted to petrol Model 45’s. If you have one that is in poor condition unfortunately they are not available as new old stock or aftermarket. The only options are to remove them completely or attempt to refurbish them. They are likely to be perished, cracked and hard. Refurbishment options are: • Coat with black RTV Silicon31. Spread over thinly and build up to the required level. Some RTV silicon’s cure when exposed to moisture, in this case it is suggested to use mineral turpentine on the tool/finger you are using. It stops the silicone from sticking and helps to get a smooth finish. • Liquid electrical tape works well, brush on from a tin. You can also get tool grip dip, plastidip etc that you dip them into the tin and hang up to dry, repeat a few times for thickness. • Put some olive oil in hot water and put the perished part in for an hour. That makes it a bit flexible again, then sand the “crunchy bits” off and build up layers with a spray on RTV Silicon gasket • Heat shrink. • Replace with a handle cover off a pair of pliers. • Black sealant (not silicone) and liquid soap to smooth out. • Liquid electrical tape works well, brush on from a tin. You can also get tool grip dip, plastidip etc. that you dip them into the tin and hang up to dry, repeat a few times for thickness. Handlebar grips are available aftermarket, but the above options may be used to refurbish them as well.

31 Room-Temperature-Vulcanizing silicone is a type of silicone rubber which cures at room temperature. Top before - perished clutch handle grip, bottom after one coating of RTV silicon. Credit Kym Schrapel, SBR

sections.title: Rubber V-Seal
sections.text:
173 Ver. 25 Rubber V-Seal The rubber V-seal on the PTO shaft next to the bearing holder does not fit inside the bearing holder. It is pushed flush against the bearing holder so you can see the “v” groove. If it is fitted inside the holder, against the bearing you will find the PTO shaft will be hard to turn as the seal binds against the bearing.

Handlebar cable clips “It’s the little things that count,” well to some this applies even to the cable clips that fit on the handlebars as shown below. If you break one or lose them there is an aftermarket alternative. Either use cable ties or search eBay for:

• Cable / wiring 7/8’ Handlebar / tubing Clips; or • Vintage Cable Clip / Wiring Clip - 7/8" Handlebar Tubing

V-seal correctly fitted Credit Jason Mihatsch, Original Handlebar cable clip Credit Bayden Cable / wiring 7/8’ Handlebar / tubing Clips Credit eBay.com.au

sections.title: Tight Woodruff Key
sections.text:
174 Ver. 25

Tight Woodruff Key When installing new woodruff keys, you may encounter some that are tight to fit. Like bearings there are interference and clearance fits with the keys. The woodruff keys should be an interference fit on the shaft and a clearance fit on the sprocket i.e. very snug. So stated it may be necessary to rub with wet and dry on both sides, de-burr the key slot, add some CRC then support the shaft opposite the key then tap in with a brass hammer. If you don't support the shaft and just hammer the key you may bend it. Alternatively, with multigrips adjusted to allow the shaft to fit and few layers of electrical tape around the jaws to protect the shaft, locate the key and squeeze.

Stainless Steel nut/bolts Some people have expressed concern about the potential for stainless steel nuts and bolts to suffer “galling”. Galling is any transfer of material between metallic surfaces, resulting from friction when they slide past each other. Practically this means threads may seize and bolts can snap. Don’t panic about galling and stainless - it can even happen with lug nuts. The potential problem is avoidable if you use the moly grease supplied with the kits and don’t tighten up too fast (heat is one cause of galling). Vintage Cable Clip / Wiring Clip - 7/8" Handlebar Tubing Credit eBay.com.au Tight woodruff key. Credit Gary Kotzur, SBR

sections.title: Engine issues
sections.text: 175 Ver. 25 Engine issues How do I work out which replacement engine to buy? Many Scott Bonnar owners either restore the original engine or replace them with a Briggs and Stratton to maintain the “historical link” to the original in preference to other brands. If you want you may consider other engine brands such as Honda, Predator, Robin, Kawasaki or the “Chonda” (cheap Honda copy); ultimately the choice is yours. An “old chestnut” question often asked is “which is better, Honda, Briggs & Stratton or Predator”. There are fanboys for each engine brand, a bit like Ford versus Holden. That said, treat their comments as you like. So, what’s the “go”, be it Honda, Briggs or Predator, they all do the job. The engine will be on a lawnmower that is not going to “work” the engine. What you want is an engine that starts, is reliable and has longevity. They all do the job and are direct fits. Honda and Briggs & Stratton have reputations as being reliable and long lasting. The Predator is relatively new so reliability can’t really be compared as in over 20 plus years like the Honda or Briggs & Stratton. You may care to factor your budget; is the Predator being cheaper a factor or is the extra cost of a Honda worth it? There is also the “Chonda”, the cheaper Chinese clone engines. The likes of the Loncin are a good, cheaper alternative. Don’t be afraid of the “made in China” scaremongers, the Predator, Briggs and even the Honda are made in China. Ultimately it’s a personal choice. Whatever engine you choose: • Make sure it does not foul with the handlebars (e.g. some older Briggs & Stratton’s with the large fuel tanks do) • Make sure you get the correct size engine shaft. o Be aware that many “Chondas” only come with 19 mm shafts. • make sure it does not have a tapered shaft. • That you put oil in it before you start it. With regards to Supercut models, depending on the model you may have to fabricate new engine mounts for it and fit an engine with a reduction box. Normally a 5hp is retrofitted. Again, measure the shaft of the original and makes sure the new one is the same size. Engines off e.g. water pumps, generators People at times see cheap small engine powered water pumps and generators and think they could be a cheap engine option. Beware, they may have a ¾” shaft BUT the engines may have a threaded shaft on a water pump or a tapered shaft on a generator. These shafts are not compatible with a Scott Bonnar mower. Below are some other issues to consider when looking at a replacement engine.

sections.title: What size shaft is it?
sections.text:
176 Ver. 25 What size shaft is it? As per the opposite picture, measure the shaft between the clutch and motor. It will either be 5/8” (16 mm) or ¾” (19 mm). Rule of Thumb: • For a Mod 45, 17” and below it should be a 5/8” shaft and above should be a ¾” shaft. o However, the drive shaft may differ from engine shaft side if the clutch half has been replaced. • It is therefore strongly recommended that you measure the shaft. Do not assume the Rule of Thumb applies. o Measure with vernier calipers o Use for 5/8 and ¾ open ended spanners to fit over the shaft. o If you can’t fit vernier calipers between the engine and clutch then cut a slot in a piece of cardboard 16 or 19mm roughly for 5/8 and 3/4 respectively. Worst case scenario or you make a rookie mistake is you get the wrong size output shaft, all is not lost, the clutch halves come in the two sizes as well. Engine capacity The size or engine capacity is a personal choice; I shall not enter a discussion about “bigger is better”. However, these days a new Briggs and Stratton 3.5hp Model 550 I/C engine is the smallest available and commonly used as a replacement engine on Model 45’s. The Briggs and Stratton are a commonly used engine when replacing the former Kirby engines.

Measuring the crankshaft Credit Travis Hines, SBR

sections.title: Engine capacity
sections.text:

Rule of Thumb: • For a Mod 45, 17” and below it should be a 5/8” shaft and above should be a ¾” shaft. o However, the drive shaft may differ from engine shaft side if the clutch half has been replaced. • It is therefore strongly recommended that you measure the shaft. Do not assume the Rule of Thumb applies. o Measure with vernier calipers o Use for 5/8 and ¾ open ended spanners to fit over the shaft. o If you can’t fit vernier calipers between the engine and clutch then cut a slot in a piece of cardboard 16 or 19mm roughly for 5/8 and 3/4 respectively. Measuring the crankshaft Credit Travis Hines, SBR

sections.title: Output shaft height
sections.text:
177 Ver. 25 Output shaft height Whichever engine you choose, ensure the output shaft is at the right height: • Rato and Kawasaki FJ100D, the output shaft sits slightly lower in height. • There is a difference reported32 of 1/16" in the crank centre height between a Kirby and Briggs and Stratton. It has been advised to use a 1/16 shim under a Briggs and Stratton when it is replacing a Kirby. Furthermore33, there is a difference in the position of the PTO hole in the side plate which is 3/32" higher for the Kirby equipped chassis. You cannot raise the rails as on the non-drive side as they would be above the side plate. Therefore, to account for the PTO hole position, in the case of replacing a Kirby with a Briggs & Stratton you will have to shim it for correct alignment. • SBR member Steve Brown advises having stripped a 17” with dual ID plate which had two full width shims under the original Briggs engine. Maybe this was known in the factory back when the switch happened. To give context twin alloy ID plates were from 1973-75 and coincidentally the 2.5HP Briggs Model 450520 17" was released in late 1973/74. • Honda GP160 engines have the same crankshaft height as a Kirby but not the same as a Briggs & Stratton engine. Note – the use of a self-aligning bearing on the PTO shaft minimizes alignment issues caused by the height differences, whether it totally negates the difference is doubtful. As in the above situation it is getting close to 3mm difference which is probably too much for a bearing as big as a 50-cent piece to take. If you must raise the engine you may simply use washers as shims. However, it is advisable that an actual shim or plate be made to support the whole length of the engine on the rail, rather than just a washer at each engine mount.

32 Advice from Shane Kosteszyn https://www.facebook.com/groups/1801521490103765/permalink/2259893780933198/ 33 https://www.facebook.com/groups/1801521490103765/permalink/2313212348934674/ Accessed 22 July 2019 B&S shaft height difference in Kirby powered chassis Credit Shane Kosteszyn, SBR Briggs & Stratton (l) and Kirby (r) showing shaft height differences Credit Brendan Keir, SBR

sections.title: Kawasaki FJ100 engine – crankshaft issues
sections.text:
178 Ver. 25 Kawasaki FJ100 engine – crankshaft issues If you choose to replace an old engine with a Kawasaki FJ100 motor there are several issues with this engine: • Height of the crankshaft (mentioned above) • Length of the crankshaft. Horizontally the Kawasaki engine shaft is a fraction long. This results in the crankshaft and the PTO shaft being in contact with each other therefore preventing alignment of the engine to the chassis mounting holes. In this case there are several options • Machine the PTO shaft • Machine the engine shaft • Elongate the chassis mounting holes. • Elongate the motor mounting holes. Of these options the consensus amongst members discussing this issue is to machine the PTO shaft. Reasoning being if you cut too much off, the consequence is a $300 engine versus $40 PTO shaft. An alternative option which addresses the height differences is now available in the form of an adjustable bearing housing.

Kawasaki FJ100 engine shaft showing contact with PTO shaft Credit Andrew Reid, SBR. Adjustable bearing housing Credit George SB-FabandParts, SBR

sections.title: Replacement throttle cable to fit throttle lever
sections.text:
179 Ver. 25 Replacement throttle cable to fit throttle lever The older style throttle lever uses a flexible braided type of cable with a lead end (not the solid wire type of throttle cable with a “z” end) where the throttle body on the carburetor is pulled back by a spring as on the early model Kirby’s and Briggs. Tip: Need a new throttle cable? Can’t get one the right size? Try a reputable bicycle shop, they have brake or gear cables with different ends on the inner cable, outers and caps for a fraction of the price to get one made up.

Older style throttle lever replacement The older style chrome throttle lever (as shown above/previous page) is not available as an old original stock part. If you are missing or have a broken throttle lever, then all is not lost. You MAY be able to buy a used one, but they do not frequently appear for sale. That said, all is not lost, there is an alternative. Available online are levers variously described as: • “Magneto Choke Air Lever Right Hand Ball End Chrome Plated”; or • “Chrome choke lever (RH) for vintage bikes” These cost about $20.00 and are a good looking replacement.

Braided wire cable with lead end to fit the throttle control lever. Credit Bruce Banks, SBR Older style throttle lever Credit Ned Searle, SBR “Magneto Choke Air Lever Right Hand Ball End Chrome Plated “ - Alternative throttle lever option. Credit eBay.com.au

sections.title: Briggs & Stratton exhaust deflector
sections.text:
180 Ver. 25 Briggs & Stratton exhaust deflector For those that choose to purchase a new Briggs and Stratton engine, do not forget the exhaust deflector. Take time when unpacking it to look for the exhaust deflector. It will probably be in a plastic bag in a box or with the paperwork in the new box.. Fitting is advisable, it will avoid fowling the handlebar plate. Positioning it in an approximate 9 o’clock position will direct the exhaust away from the chassis and your legs.

How do I fit the throttle cable to a new engine? Briggs and Stratton You have replaced your old engine with a new Briggs and Stratton engine, and you want to use the original throttle cable, rather than the engines throttle lever (as shown opposite).There are two fixing points available, my preferred option is the one underneath the air filter. Simply remove the air filter and you will see it. (Refer to the below pictures.) With the throttle cable fitted to the lever on the handlebars, bring the cable between the air filter and the exhaust. Clamp the throttle cable and leave enough cable/wire exposed to thread through the hole and tighten the nut on top of the cable/wire. (you will probably have to adjust the cable length that you feed through the hole). Loosen the nut that holds the adjuster lever and enables the return spring to operate.

New Briggs & Stratton engine. Credit Matthew Thomas SBR. Engine throttle controls Exhaust deflector Credit Luciano Melito SBR Exhaust deflector

sections.title: Predator NH150
sections.text:
181 Ver. 25

Above and right. Throttle cable fixing Credit Kym Schrapel SBR.

Predator NH150 The below are several pictures to assist with fitting the throttle cable on the Predator NH150 engines.

Thread cable/wire, adjust and tighten nut. Loosen this nut Clamp throttle cable outer here. Air filter Exhaust Fitting throttle cable to NH150 Credit AJ Hughan, SBR.

sections.title: Honda
sections.text:
182 Ver. 25 Honda Fitting a throttle cable to a Honda is very similar to the Briggs & Stratton. However, it appears that new Honda engines do not come with the Throttle Cable Wire Lock. Thread the cable through the cable clamp, clamping the outer and then thread the cable through the wire lock and tighten the screw. Loosen the nut with the yellow paint as below to allow the return spring to work. Adjust the cable length as required.

An alternative bush fix, especially if you don’t have a cable wire lock is to use a split pin as shown opposite. Clamp the outer cable in the cable clamp. Put the ends of split pin through the hole (where the cable wire lock goes), spread the ends apart so that the cable end is clamped between the head of the split pin and the lever hole. As per the previous method, back off the yellow painted nut to enable the return spring.

Throttle Cable Wire Lock Stop Holder Swivel Credit eBay.com.au Throttle cable fitting Credit Angus West, SBR Cable clamp Fit Cable Wire Lock here Loosen this nut Alternative fitting of throttle cable Credit Andy Bell, SBR

sections.title: Older style engine stop switch.
sections.text:
183 Ver. 25 Older style engine stop switch. Before the newer engines with actual on/off switches, older engines often had manual engine stops. These came in a variety of styles but did the same thing in earthing the spark plug to shut off the engine. Call it the 1960’s and 70’s form of an engine immobilizer. Below is a collage of the various engine stops which may be encountered. Word of warning –Do not touch the mower with the opposite hand when turning off. Only have the hand touching the tab or you will get a shock. It works by shorting out the spark plug lead, earthing back to the body. The only way it can shock you is if you become a better conductive path than the metal of the engine

New Briggs & Stratton – what is this wire? Commonly asked when people have taken delivery of a new Briggs and Stratton engine is “what is this wire for?”(pictured opposite). Some older models have a similar wire. You will struggle to find the answer online or in the engine’s manual, but the answer is simple. It is there so you can fit a remote stop switch e.g. at the top of the handlebars. If you do not want to, then just leave it.

Engine mount holes wont line up. “Bolting on a new engine but can’t get all 4 holes to line up. The right-hand side on the rear is fractionally out”. Not a common problem but one that can be easily avoided in the rebuild process if you: • Disengage the clutch to take the pressure off • Ensure the clutch is fitted on the shaft correctly. Various engine stops. Credit Rodney Unicomb, Dan Gr, Tous Vlamenous and Martin Lang, SBR Wire on new B&S motor Credit Ric Brown, SBR

sections.title: Flywheel - Stripped Crankshaft thread
sections.text:
184 Ver. 25 • When fitting the mounting bolts, do not tighten them up until all bolts have taken in the nuts.

Flywheel - Stripped Crankshaft thread As an SBR member has said “this can’t be good”. The thread on the crankshaft is stripped so the flywheel nut cannot hold the flywheel and starter in place. The suggested fix is quite simple: • Grind it off flush, drill down the middle and tap a thread. • Use a bolt to hold on the starter cup rather than a nut. Easy fix, cheaper than a new engine.

Stripped thread Credit Brett Fitzroy, SBR Engine mounting bolts not lining up Credit Chris Williams, SBR

sections.title: Leaking fuel tap
sections.text:
185 Ver. 25 Leaking fuel tap Owners of the old Villiers or Kirby engines fitted with original brass fuel taps may experience fuel leaks from the tap. Inside the Villiers fuel tap is a washer that over time perishes/shrinks and allows fuel through. You should find a small screw which when you tighten it up it should (hopefully) make the washer expand. You can make the washer out of thick leather (e.g. from an old belt) or a wine cork. Alternatively, an easy fix is to get a generic or Victa fuel tap. GA Spares have a replacement washer (Part number PTC809).. They cost about $10.00. Kirby fuel taps have 'o' rings and a point on the end of the plunger to remove the screw ,replace the o rings and give the plunger a tap on the end of the tap without the screw out that gives it a good seal.

Kirby engine – crankcase removal For owners of Model 33 and early Model 45’s with a Kirby engine. If you are intending to remove the crankcase it is not a simple matter of removing the retaining bolts and sliding the crankcase cover off. The crankshaft has a circlip behind the oil seal, which is in front of a bearing, which will need to be taken off before the case can be removed. Drill a couple of small holes in the oil seal and then pick it out, the small circlip beneath can then be removed. You may need to clean up the crank with emery cloth (if there is a small lip on the edge of the circlip groove) and check the governor lever is free. This then frees the rear half of the crankcase for removal.

Left - Washer inside old style Villiers fuel tap Credit Sam Merema, SBR Brass Villiers fuel tap Credit Kym Schrapel, SBR Above - Petrol tap cork Credit Shane Herriman, SBR

sections.title: Engine swap (general)
sections.text:
186 Ver. 25

Kirby engine crankcase removal. Left - showing oil seal. Right - showing circlip after oil seal removal Credit Outdoorking.com The oil seal part number is OS1436, (9/16” x ¾” x 3/16”).

Engine swap (general) Replacing an engine is a simple mater, basically its bolt off – bolt on. Take the four engine mount bolts out from the old engine, undo any clutch bolts/cotter pin and slide out. Replace engine, mounting bolts and redo bolts up. Check the engine/clutch spacing and adjust as required. Video guide: https://www.youtube.com/watch?fbclid=IwAR3-uK4iIH-bhQwvnVOQKwXV- NC_m1i1g5I4QLPzasLAqtGaq6t51YTomVc&v=L0UG0BBK8UA&feature=youtu.be Engine swap (Predator Engines) Whilst a Honda or Briggs & Stratton engine swap is a bolt off – bolt on exercise, the Predator motors require a slight tweak. SBR member Steve Westerhuis provided the following guide: • The Predator is supplied with mounting bolts which are slightly shorter than the originals normally seen or come in other kits. The mounting base is slightly thinner. • These motors take half a litre of oil, Steve found it easier to pour in while the motor was off the mower. • Keep in mind all the fasteners on the Predator are metric. • Out of the box the throttle lever is set up to be used with a cable/remote lever. The cable needs to be run below and sheath clamped from the bottom. Oil seal to be removed Circlip in front of the bearing Predator throttle setup Credit Steve Westerhuis SBR
