sections.title: Primary Clutch issues
sections.text:
187 Ver. 25 • If not connecting a cable, you must tighten the 10mm nut on the throttle arm to add just enough resistance to hold it in place, otherwise it will spring back closed (this is opposite to how new Briggs & Stratton motors come out of the box). • To get the rear mount bolt in, removed one bolt from the throttle plate and loosen the other, slide the plate out the way and drop the mount bolt in. Then tighten it all back up again.

Primary Clutch issues Fitting the shaft key on the engine shaft The shaft key needs to be right down the end closest the engine. If not, it may contact the thrust race and will make a grinding noise when turned by hand (a common mistake). If the shaft key is damaged, replace it.

Shaft key on engine shaft. Tous Vlamenous, SBR Predator rear mount bolts Credit Steve Westerhuis SBR

sections.title: Fitting the shaft key on the engine shaft
sections.text:
187 Ver. 25 • If not connecting a cable, you must tighten the 10mm nut on the throttle arm to add just enough resistance to hold it in place, otherwise it will spring back closed (this is opposite to how new Briggs & Stratton motors come out of the box). • To get the rear mount bolt in, removed one bolt from the throttle plate and loosen the other, slide the plate out the way and drop the mount bolt in. Then tighten it all back up again.

Primary Clutch issues Fitting the shaft key on the engine shaft The shaft key needs to be right down the end closest the engine. If not, it may contact the thrust race and will make a grinding noise when turned by hand (a common mistake). If the shaft key is damaged, replace it.

Shaft key on engine shaft. Tous Vlamenous, SBR Predator rear mount bolts Credit Steve Westerhuis SBR

sections.title: Fitting the Clutch bearing or thrust race into the clutch half.
sections.text:
188 Ver. 25 Fitting the Clutch bearing or thrust race into the clutch half. Fitting the thrust race into the clutch half can be problematic because it is a tight fit. The key needs to be pushed all the way towards the motor and it needs to be shorter than the ones you buy. The key needs to be 35mm in length. Several tips have been provided: • Always grease the outer of the bearing and the housing it is going into. Use a socket (slightly smaller than the bearing so it does not jam in the housing) to tap the bearing in,. Never tap the inner ring or inner bearing cone during insertion. • Put the clutch half in the oven and warm it up. Then take it out and the bearing should slide straight in. Alternatively put the bearing in the freezer. o A word of caution: The heating of the housing and cooling of the bearing works well but can cause cracking of the alloy clutch half. • Press tool or vice set up is the ideal

Remember, the thrust race is fitted with the rounded edge on the inside of the cup. The race goes just past flush by about 4mm as in the above picture. Installing the clutch The easiest way to install the clutch back on the engine is with it together, as a whole unit. That is both halves joined. The Spring inside the clutch sometimes will not allow the clutch to go on the shaft so it is best to use an old PTO shaft, or the handle of a socket wrench; or just put your finger though the clutch cone to position the spring. Alternatively, some people prefer to fit the one clutch half onto the motor first, that way you can see if the key is not hitting the back of the thrust bearing. The other half is on the PTO shaft. Then slip the motor on and bolt the halves together. For the unwary, the cone should slide freely on the PTO shaft as it is engaged/disengaged. This may have been a “given” with original parts, but new OEM PTO shafts, new cones and new keys combined can result in a snug fit. Ensure the cone moves freely otherwise you may have issues with the operation of the clutch.

Fitted Thrust Race Credit Jayden Pulford, SBR

sections.title: Installing the clutch
sections.text:

Fitted Thrust Race Credit Jayden Pulford, SBR

sections.title: How close to the motor should the clutch be?
sections.text:
189 Ver. 25 How close to the motor should the clutch be? Correct placement of the clutch on the shaft is vital to ensure the proper operation of the clutch assembly.

Tighten the cotter pin nut and test the clutch engagement. If the clutch is not engaging/disengaging adjust the adjuster bolt on the clutch fork. It should do both, and the nylon thrust bearing should have clearance for the shaft to spin freely, otherwise you will either melt the plastic thrust pad or cause excessive wear to a brass one. Note: for those with a clutch half with two grub screws (not cotter pin) you must get the clutch square to the engine by adjusting both grub screws. If you can't do it, the clutch half or bearing is probably worn and will need replacing.

Clutch too close to the motor Credit Andrew Schultz SBR 5 mm gap between engine and clutch half. Credit Ryan Goodwin, SBR Gap should be 5 mm. Correct spacing The gap between the clutch and the crankcase should be about 5 mm

sections.title: How close to the cone should the thrust pad be?
sections.text:
190 Ver. 25 If you replace the engine with a new Briggs and Stratton engine the spacing between the clutch and engine is different. Simply push the clutch to the taper on the crankshaft, the taper gives the required gap as shown in the below picture.

How close to the cone should the thrust pad be? Normally you only need a 1 mm gap between thrust pad and the cone. Basically, adjust the fork with the reel engaged. Let the cone bite on the clutch and then wind the fork away (turning the bolt anticlockwise) to allow the thrust pad to be just off the end of the cone end. To set the 1 mm gap, a credit card is a suitable feeler gauge. Several persons experienced in setting up Model 45’s have identified difficulties in getting the clutch to function correctly when setting up the clutch with a 1 mm gap between the cone and thrust pad. Different tolerances between parts whether old or new OEM manufactured parts, the thickness of the cork and even the position of the engine relative to the PTO shaft (i.e. is it truly squarely positioned) appear to have a cumulative (if not confounding) effect such that the clutch may not function properly with a This gap should be 1 - 2 mm. Clutch on new Briggs & Stratton engine Credit Isaac Miller,

sections.title: Washer between cone and thrust pad?
sections.text:
191 Ver. 25 1 mm gap. They suggest that you start with the 1 mm “rule of thumb” gap and be prepared to increase the gap up to about 2 mm. Remember, keep an eye on the adjustment longer term to maintain effective clutch operation. As the clutch is operated the inner cork gradually wears and tends to be compressed over time moving the cone towards the pad changing the gap. Washer between cone and thrust pad? Question: Is it important to have a washer in between the cone and the thrust pad?

This is a valid question without a definitive answer. Before considering the merits of a washer, perhaps of more importance is lubrication of the thrust pad (whether plastic or metal). Even more important is correct setup as even brass pads will wear. I have seen a Model 33 parts diagram showing a washer in place yet in a later Rover Model 45 parts diagram no such washer is shown. Many original condition mowers with the plastic thrust pad do not have the washer. One of the SBR page suppliers selling metal (brass) thrust pads sells fibre washers to go between the thrust pad and cone. As for the washer, personally I would use a fibre washer even on plastic pads for the simple reason they are fibre and provide a degree of insulation to reduce heat transference and wear from the cone to the plastic pad. The use of a fibre washer also ensures the spacing between the cone and thrust pad. Call it insurance against poor setup/maintenance.

Cone and plastic thrust pad Credit George Souris, SBR

sections.title: How do I fit the cotter pin in the clutch half?
sections.text:
192 Ver. 25 How do I fit the cotter pin in the clutch half? Because of the shape of the cotter pin (below picture) you cannot simply insert the pin and tap it into position. You cannot put the cotter pin in whilst the clutch is attached to the engine shaft -. do not even try to hit it. The Cotter pin is inserted into the clutch before putting on the engine shaft. Either remove the engine or clutch halves, (clutch will be easier), place the pin in the hole with the notch lined up so it fits around the shaft and put back on the shaft. When doing this make sure the housing is a tight fit on the shaft before tightening the lock bolt because a loose housing will cause wear and damage to the shaft.

How do I replace the clutch corks There are two corks on the Model 45’s (primary and landroll/drive); one on the Model 40 (landroll). Model 33’s either have just the landroll clutch cork for the belt/chain models or both the landroll and primary clutch cork on the chain driven models. Irrespective of the model the principal remains the same. It is not mandatory to replace corks in a rebuild. If it is still intact and is fully adhered, a light sand to take the glaze off the surface may suffice. However, if it is contaminated with oil, replace it. Considering they are an inexpensive item and if you are doing a rebuild, then why not whilst the mower is in pieces. They do not have to be replaced annually. Please search the SBR page for a video by Glenn Russell on changing the corks. Firstly, remove the old cork with a paint scrapper (I use an old chisel) and clean the surfaces ready for gluing. Use a contact adhesive suitable for both hard and porous surfaces. Brands such as Selleys Kwik Grip or Parfix Fast Grip are ideal. Apply a thin, even coating of contact adhesive to both cork and metal surfaces and allow to dry to touch as per the product instructions. (Personally, when applying contact to porous surfaces such as cork, I apply two coats. One coat, allowed to dry; then a second coat.) After about 20 minutes bring both surfaces together, being sure they are correctly aligned. Clamp and allow to set/cure. When installing the clutch cone cork, ensure the cork is against the lip. That way, the ends should meet and form a cone shape without the need to trim the cork. Cotter Pin Cotter Pin in Clutch half Remember a 5/8” cotter pin does not fit a ¾” shaft.

193 Ver. 25

The only alternative to contact adhesive for the corks is 5 Minute Araldite. Beware though, Araldite is an epoxy adhesive and can be difficult to remove once it has set.

Newly installed drive clutch cork, Source Jason Brindley, SBR Mod 33 Clutch cork clamped after gluing Credit Kym Schrapel, SBR. Do not use products such as Liquid Nails or Silicon sealants to affix the corks – they WILL fail. Correctly fitted clutch cone cork Credit GeorgeBonMods, SBR Fit cork against the lip (circled) Credit Henry Ranks SBR

sections.title: How do I replace the clutch corks
sections.text:
192 Ver. 25 How do I fit the cotter pin in the clutch half? Because of the shape of the cotter pin (below picture) you cannot simply insert the pin and tap it into position. You cannot put the cotter pin in whilst the clutch is attached to the engine shaft -. do not even try to hit it. The Cotter pin is inserted into the clutch before putting on the engine shaft. Either remove the engine or clutch halves, (clutch will be easier), place the pin in the hole with the notch lined up so it fits around the shaft and put back on the shaft. When doing this make sure the housing is a tight fit on the shaft before tightening the lock bolt because a loose housing will cause wear and damage to the shaft.

How do I replace the clutch corks There are two corks on the Model 45’s (primary and landroll/drive); one on the Model 40 (landroll). Model 33’s either have just the landroll clutch cork for the belt/chain models or both the landroll and primary clutch cork on the chain driven models. Irrespective of the model the principal remains the same. It is not mandatory to replace corks in a rebuild. If it is still intact and is fully adhered, a light sand to take the glaze off the surface may suffice. However, if it is contaminated with oil, replace it. Considering they are an inexpensive item and if you are doing a rebuild, then why not whilst the mower is in pieces. They do not have to be replaced annually. Please search the SBR page for a video by Glenn Russell on changing the corks. Firstly, remove the old cork with a paint scrapper (I use an old chisel) and clean the surfaces ready for gluing. Use a contact adhesive suitable for both hard and porous surfaces. Brands such as Selleys Kwik Grip or Parfix Fast Grip are ideal. Apply a thin, even coating of contact adhesive to both cork and metal surfaces and allow to dry to touch as per the product instructions. (Personally, when applying contact to porous surfaces such as cork, I apply two coats. One coat, allowed to dry; then a second coat.) After about 20 minutes bring both surfaces together, being sure they are correctly aligned. Clamp and allow to set/cure. When installing the clutch cone cork, ensure the cork is against the lip. That way, the ends should meet and form a cone shape without the need to trim the cork. Cotter Pin Cotter Pin in Clutch half Remember a 5/8” cotter pin does not fit a ¾” shaft.

193 Ver. 25

The only alternative to contact adhesive for the corks is 5 Minute Araldite. Beware though, Araldite is an epoxy adhesive and can be difficult to remove once it has set.

Newly installed drive clutch cork, Source Jason Brindley, SBR Mod 33 Clutch cork clamped after gluing Credit Kym Schrapel, SBR. Do not use products such as Liquid Nails or Silicon sealants to affix the corks – they WILL fail. Correctly fitted clutch cone cork Credit GeorgeBonMods, SBR Fit cork against the lip (circled) Credit Henry Ranks SBR

sections.title: How do I fit the clutch fork spring?
sections.text:
194 Ver. 25 How do I fit the clutch fork spring? Page members have reported several ways to fit this spring. • Remove the lever so you can lay it over with less tension on the spring. • With handle in, pre-bend it to save a bit of heart ache. • Take handle off, lay bracket flat and put pin through frame spring then frame, lift back up and put handle back in. Installing the shaft rings on the clutch fork pivot The shaft rings that hold the clutch fork pivot or pin in place are made of spring steel to retain tension on the pin. To remove or fit, gently tap the pin through the fork assembly. They still may break but that's your best shot. It is advisable to replace them. • When installing them it is best to fit one on the clutch fork pivot first (whilst off the machine). • Then with either a small socket (8 mm) or ring spanner hammer it on. Put another hammer on the other side so it doesn’t move.

Clutch fork spring fitted Shaft ring on clutch fork pivot Credit Brett Glancy, SBR

sections.title: Clutch counterweight
sections.text:
195 Ver. 25 Clutch counterweight People have encountered the item opposite, a clutch counterweight. They may be found on a clutch half with two grub screws (item 7 as per the below parts diagram). They will only fit in one place on the clutch so you cannot move them around as there is a locating pin on the clutch. Physically they cannot be installed in the wrong place, going opposite the grub screws to counteract the weight of the screws. Rover apparently made them to compensate for out of balance clutch housings. They were only fitted if the clutch vibrated. There is a thread on Outdoor King which discusses the clutch counterweight. It appears that the counterweights were mainly encountered in WA, although they were found elsewhere.. For further information you may read more on Outdoorking.com (https://www.outdoorking.com/forum/ubbthreads.php/topics/52931.html?fbclid=IwAR2NNQ4dt_raCQZ dbge8wQoxTpYjcv8tyTGgxrYRQEtMteDOnJlsvecPi7U)

Below is a 1991 Rover Mowers Technical Bulletin dealing with the clutch counterweight. Clutch counterweight Credit Alex Dougan, SBR Clutch counterweight fitted. Credit Nev Gordon, SBR No. 7. Clutch Counterweight.

196 Ver. 25

Rover Mowers Technical Bulletin Credit Michelle N Peter Castellucci-Gagliardi

sections.title: Drive Clutch issues
sections.text:
197 Ver. 25 Drive Clutch issues How tight should the clutch operating bolt be? Refer to the below parts diagram extract of the Drive System. The clutch operating bolt (38), the bolt that goes through the large intermediate sprocket assembly (27) and bearing (28) should not be tight. In fact, it needs to be loose to allow the clutch to operate. If it is done up tight the clutch cable will not be able to be move the operating lever (15) the 2 – 3 mm needed. If this bolt is too tight the clutch will be engaged all the time and the mower will drive without squeezing the clutch lever. It should be loose when drive clutch is disengaged and then tight when drive clutch engaged. You can adjust clutch slippage by loosening/tightening.

So, how do you adjust the operating bolt? • Adjustment should be the last thing to adjust before putting the chain cover on. • Make an adjustment to get the 2 – 3 mm movement. o make sure you have play on the other side where the clutch cable is situated and attached. • Run the motor, engage the primary clutch and fine tune to suit. o Each mower will be slightly different. Install the chain cover and happy mowing. SBR member Jason Mihatsch has prepared a video which explains the adjustment. The video may be viewed at https://www.facebook.com/jason.mihatsch/videos/10156963657986225/ (accessed 1 October 2019). Clutch cable adjustment Firstly, on the clutch itself with the cable disconnected ,adjust the clutch operating bolt until the outer plate is just not touching the cork (as previously described, above). Next, connect the cable back on and adjust the cable using the adjusting screw/nut. Clutch operating bolt, Credit Ryan Goodwin, SBR Model 45 Parts Diagram extract

sections.title: Stretched clutch cable.
sections.text:
198 Ver. 25 If you are having trouble finding the sweet spot on the clutch lever because your mower wants to really take off; it may be a simple clutch cable adjustment. At the end of the cable (circled opposite) wind the thread towards the frame then wind the cable, thus loosening the tension on the lever

Stretched clutch cable. SBR member and experienced renovator, Bluey Cullen, advises after numerous restorations he has encountered many with a stretched clutch cable. Instead of replacing the cable, inserting a bicycle brake adjuster for $1.50 is a simple, cheap fix. Easy to cut down to suit each cable and works a treat.

Clutch cable end Credit Bluey Cullen, SBR Left - Bicycle brake adjuster Credit Bluey Cullen, SBR Above - Repaired clutch cable with bike adjuster fitted. Credit Bluey Cullen, SBR Drive clutch cable Credit Kym Schrapel, SBR)

199 Ver. 25 Another simple and easy fix for a stretched clutch cable has been provided by SBR member Steve Pegler: • Source a nut that will fit inside the clutch operating lever. • Make an angle cut through one side of the nut as shown opposite. • Remove the clutch cable from the assembly and slip the clutch cable through the cut so the cable is inside the nut. • Re-install the clutch cable as in the below picture.

Left - Making a cross or diagonal cut on a suitable sized nut. Credit Steve Pegler, SBR Nut fitted inside the clutch lever Credit Steve Pegler, SBR

sections.title: Reel issues
sections.text:
200 Ver. 25 Reel issues Cutter bearing seal RH. Prior to sealed bearings, there was a foam pad placed between the chassis and the right-hand cutter bearing as shown below. The purpose was to keep debris and dirt from getting into the bearing. Sealed bearings make the need for this foam bearing seal somewhat redundant. However, the potential still exists for debris/dirt to enter the space. Should you wish to fashion one yourself, its dimensions are: • 41mm diameter X 13mm thick

How tight should my reel be? It should spin but still be firm, if you spin it fast it should spin 3-4 times. The reel should almost spin freely if you spin it by hand with a light “zing” noise. An example of how this should sound is demonstrated in a short clip by Brock Bearsby34. Be careful when you spin the reel that you do not get your fingers caught.

How do I get the height adjuster spring and washer back in? This question is possibly the one that causes the most frustration to people (next to removing the blade from the soleplate). Like other questions this one has a few answers supplied by contributors. • With the washer in the right spot (centre), push the spring up using the washer. Then put one end of the circlip into the groove and let the downward spring pressure hold that in place. Then, use your fingers to push the other side of the spring/washer up and get the other side of the circlip in the groove. Then simply push it into the groove. o Wear safety glasses in case the circlip wants to take off (they have a habit of doing that). • A pair of vice grips if you have them. Otherwise multi-grips will do the job but a bit harder. Clamp from the top of the bracket and under the washer. Should be able to get it enough to start the clip in the groove then remove the clamp and push the clip the rest of the way. • Use two sets of small vice grips. One on each side. If you use vice grips it is suggested that a rag is placed over the jaws so as not to damage the height adjuster, especially if it is a brass one. • Flat screwdriver in the groove and pry the spring up. Put the clip in and push in with pliers. • Use your fingers and just get the clip into the groove. Then use a small flat head screwdriver to get the other side in. Then tap it in with a hammer. • Use a gear puller two prong.

34 https://www.facebook.com/groups/1801521490103765/permalink/1950336878555558/ accessed 21/11/2019 Cutter bearing seal RH Credit Simon Henry, SBR

201 Ver. 25 • Only got two hands but really need a third one. Simple, two small clamps or using a gear puller and it is done in no time. This method is like the above vice grip methods, the benefit is it does not run the risk of marking the adjuster. • Lay the frame on the side (ideally without the engine fitted) and using multi grips pull everything together and slide the circlip in - easier to push circlip downwards than to push it sideways.

• Compress the spring using two small cable ties, then fit on the adjuster with the washer and insert the circlip. Finally, use side cutters to cut the cable ties.

Left - Two clamp method Credit Kym Schrapel, SBR Right - Gear Puller method Credit Shane Kosteszyn, SBR Cable tie method. Credit Steve Westerhuis, SBR

sections.title: Which way does the Cutter Bearing Shield go?
sections.text:
202 Ver. 25

Which way does the Cutter Bearing Shield go? So, you are about to fit the bearings onto the reel and have forgotten which way the cutter bearing shields go. These are the large, beveled or conical washers. The below is a simple representation where the red bracket represents the shield. [](=|\\\\\\\\\|=)[]= If you fit the shield the wrong way you will bind the bearing and it will not spin freely.

Loose Cutter Bearing

SBR member Shane Kosteszyn advises the non- drive side bearing is an interference fit to the shaft, as that's all that holds the inner race to the shaft. Interference fit means that a shaft is machined to a larger diameter than the ID of the bearing and that amount is determined by the bearing and shaft size. The best way to fit a bearing like that is to gently warm the inner race so that it grows then fit it to the shaft very quickly by hand and when it cools it will grip the shaft. Don’t bother lubricating the shaft – it will just be wiped off when the bearing is put on.

The drive side is a clearance fit so the bearing should slide on the shaft (no need to lubricate the shaft). What stops the inner race from turning is the compression of the sprocket to the shaft created when you tighten the nut up to tension. If that nut stays tight the inner race of the bearing will not spin on the shaft Cutter Bearing and Shield Credit David Curnow, SBR Bearing Shield Cutter bearing on the shaft Credit David Curnow, SBR

203 Ver. 25 Bearings have a nominal ID; it's the shaft that's machined to size. Clearance fit is both the bearing ID and the shaft size for size. If the non-drive bearing slides on but there is not much play when you “wiggle it on”, a retaining compound such as Loctite 641 may be enough. However, if it is too loose the options are: • Centre punch evenly around the shaft where the bearing will sit than tap the bearing on. Albeit a rather crude “bush fix” method this is effective. • Alternatively, rather than centre punching, the better option is get it knurled (on a lathe) to increase the diameter. A good machine shop can do that. • If the shaft is beyond centre punching or knurling, then have the shaft welded and machined down.

The principle of a bearing is to spin in the centre on the greased balls (the ball bearings) the other two surfaces should be fixed in position and not move. If it is loose, the bearing and shaft will wear and cause problems with the reel vibrating. Ultimately this will cause wear and damage to the reel (blades and shaft) and the bottom blade.

Repaired reel shaft with pin punch marks Credit Dion Moran, SBR Example of knurling on a shaft. Credit Wikipedia

However, if it is too loose the options are: • Centre punch evenly around the shaft where the bearing will sit than tap the bearing on. Albeit a rather crude “bush fix” method this is effective. • Alternatively, rather than centre punching, the better option is get it knurled (on a lathe) to increase the diameter. A good machine shop can do that. • If the shaft is beyond centre punching or knurling, then have the shaft welded and machined down.

The principle of a bearing is to spin in the centre on the greased balls (the ball bearings) the other two surfaces should be fixed in position and not move. If it is loose, the bearing and shaft will wear and cause problems with the reel vibrating. Ultimately this will cause wear and damage to the reel (blades and shaft) and the bottom blade.

Repaired reel shaft with pin punch marks Credit Dion Moran, SBR Example of knurling on a shaft. Credit Wikipedia

sections.title: Reel does not line up with bedknife.
sections.text:
204 Ver. 25 Reel does not line up with bedknife. “My reel isn’t 100% aligned with the bedknife, needs to move right 3mm more”. This problem can be explained by the incorrect bearing fitment on the reel. Remember to correctly fit the cutter bearing shields as described above. Furthermore, it is imperative that the bearing is tight with the washer. If the bearing is tight on the shaft you may have to tap it home, against the washer. Below pictures show an example of a tool that may be used, alternatively a tube spanner could be used. Do not try and fit a 6203 bearing, they are slightly larger, ensure you have fitted a RLS5 bearing.

Bearing fitting tool when the bearing is tight on the shaft Credit Lincoln Mead, SBR Left - Reel not aligned to bedknife Credit Damien Cooke, SBR

sections.title: Rear roller issues
sections.text:
205 Ver. 25 Rear roller issues How does the rear roller go together? Check the video section of the page and the rover diagram in the files section or see the video by Glenn Russell35. Rear rollers are identical, it does not matter which goes on the left or right side. Another method is to: • place one roller on a milk crate, ratchet box upwards and inset the shaft with the pawl holder fitted. • Place the pawls into the pawl holder (the pawls dimple faces down). • Do not grease the pawls or where they sit as they must freely move. Grease will cause them to stick and malfunction the drives. • Slide the other roller down on top and line it up to fit correctly. • Fit the collar and lock it down. • Flip the rollers and do the same on the other end. Do the following test before putting the assembly back in the frame. It is a simple test to see if it is working. • The 6 pawls will all face forward [left] from the drive side on re-assembly. • Lubricate the roller shaft and drum bushings with grease. • On assembly, rotate the drive-side sprocket anti-clockwise. o the pawls will engage in the rollers, and the shaft will lock. • Turn the drive-side sprocket clockwise and there will be free travel of the shaft. • You will hear the pawls 'ticking' on their respective ratchets as they rotate in the drums.

35 (https://www.facebook.com/glenn.russell.127/videos/10156325306924647/) Installing pawls into pawl holder Credit Luke Duke, SBR

sections.text:

sections.title: How do I get the rear roller to roll backwards?
sections.text:
206 Ver. 25 How do I get the rear roller to roll backwards? Question: “have an issue with rear roller. It goes forward fine but does not go backwards. Anyone know what might be wrong? Answer: There are several possible causes (refer to the above parts diagram). • The clutch may be too tight. o That is the drive clutch bolt (38), if that is too tight it will grab the clutch and you will be chasing after the machine. Refer to How tight should the clutch operating bolt be? • The chain (20) from the rear roller up to the clutch may be too loose It will roll forward then bind up when trying to roll backwards. • The drive chains are not adjusted correctly, undo the 2 bolts (35) on the slide bar (16) and adjust. • Another area to check is part “29” - clean the inside , and lightly grease. If there is any sign of corrosion or pitting, also give it a light sand with fine sandpaper. They tend to get rusty and bind up instead of sliding freely. • Finally, if you have taken the rear roller apart hopefully you did not put grease on the pawls. If they have been greased the rear rollers will not work mechanically.

Model 45 Parts Diagram extract - Drive System.

sections.title: Stripped thread on rear roller – ratchet box bolts.
sections.text:
207 Ver. 25 If you have checked all the above and the problem persists, there is one last possibility. Does it feel like the clutch will not disengage? If so, in addition to checking that the collar with the roll pin is lubricated and slides smoothly on the slotted shaft look for wear or notches in the shaft slot (16) where the roll pin locates as indicated in the opposite picture. If so clean up with a up with a thin file.

Stripped thread on rear roller – ratchet box bolts. When reassembling the rear roller, you may be unlucky to encounter one of the ratchet box bolts (1/4" X 1/2" UNC) slipping straight into the hole (i.e. the threaded holes are stripped or worn) in the rear roller. The fix is not a case of going to the next size bolt as the bolt head may foul on the edge of the drum, and if it does not, the socket will. There are several solutions: • Use helicoils. • Rotate the ratchet box, drill and tap new holes. • Use a longer bolt & tighten with a nyloc nut on the inside. • Go left field - pop rivet it. Sound silly? Well not so, that’s how the Model 430 Diplomats were done. Easy to get out, just drill them Beware, if you replace the bolts, make sure the heads do not protrude above the edge of the roller otherwise they may clash with the bolts on the opposite roller.

Stripped thread Rotate ratchet box and drill/tap new hole in the rear roller Rear Roller, Credit Kenny Festing, SBR. Check for wear where the roll pin locates. Credit Matthew Harper, SBR

sections.title: How do I position the Pawl Holder?
sections.text:
208 Ver. 25

How do I position the Pawl Holder? The Pawl Holder is fitted to the middle of the rear shaft, held in place by small grub screw (same as used on the drum shaft collar) that tightens on a machined flat spot. When/if you remove the Pawl Holder you should pay attention to where it was – you may see a mark where the grub screw was. Replacing the Pawl Holder, simply line it up with the flat spot on the shaft. If this is not in place correctly your rollers will be off centre. Pawl Pawl Holder on rear roller shaft Credit Josh Smith, SBR Model 430 rear roller showing pop rivets used on the ratchet box Credit Kym Schrapel, SBR

sections.title: Which way does the rear roller bearing fit?
sections.text:
209 Ver. 25

Which way does the rear roller bearing fit? When reinstalling the bearings into the rear bearing holders it has been asked which way to fit the sealed bearings. That is the metal or rubber side facing in or out. Reviewing various pictures on the SBR page I have found one which appears to show an original bearing with what appears to be the metal seal facing the roller. Conversely I have found pictures where members have placed the bearing such that the rubber faces the drum. In short, there is no definitive view. Either way the bearing is sealed on both sides (the rubber seal has a metal backing). Since the roller only moves at a brisk walking pace – does it really matter?

Inside the rear roller. Credit Glenn Russell, SBR Sealed rear roller bearings showing the rubber and metal seals. Credit Nev Gordon, SBR

sections.title: Rear roller bearing holders.
sections.text:
210 Ver. 25

Rear roller bearing holders. There are two rear roller bearing holders. Remember: • the holder with the slotted holes goes on the right-hand side i.e. non-drive side with the slots to the rear otherwise you will not be able to adjust rear roller up/down. • the drive side bearing holder (i.e. non-slotted holes) has the ring spacer as shown below. The ring spacer goes into the bearing housing before the bearing.

Installing rear roller bearing on the shaft “What is the best way to persuade the rear roller bearing on to the shaft? It’s really tight and I don’t want to bash the outer sleeve off”. At times difficulty has been encountered installing bearings to the rear shaft. Ensure you have the correct bearings, they are not available from the usual bearing suppliers, rather you will have to get them from the parts suppliers commonly found on the SBR page. Suggestions offered to fit the bearings include: • Make sure the shaft is free of burrs. o A bit if emery cloth if it is tight, and not a problem to just break the edge of the shaft with a fine file or slip-stone. Drive side rear bearing holder with spacer. Credit Rodney Fordham SR Non drive side rear bearing holder with slots Credit Josh Goldfinch Rear roller bearing Credit Michael McGerr, SBR

sections.title: Installing rear roller bearing on the shaft
sections.text:
210 Ver. 25

sections.title: Rear Roller Bushes
sections.text:
211 Ver. 25 • Lubricate the shaft and inner race of the bearing. • Tap on the bearing with a tube or socket on the inner race. • Heat the bearing (careful it is sealed) and cooling the shaft (put in the freezer) When fitting the bearing into the bearing holder make sure it is fitted fully in the bearing holder i.e. such that it is flush or inside the bearing holder.

Rear Roller Bushes Within the rear roller there are brass bushings in the ends of each roller and the Ratchet Box. These bushes are brass bushings. They are not normally removed or replaced during a rebuild unless showing visible signs of wear. Simply inspect them, check the shaft for fit and excess movement, if they are in good condition there is no need to replace. If they are okay then simply apply some grease.

Incorrect fitting of the bearing - should not protrude from the bearing holder. Credit Nathan Sincock, SBR Brass Bushing Rear Roller Credit Lukas Barron, SBR Ratchet Box with new Bush Credit Fabian De Angelis, SBR

sections.title: Rear Roller shaft with different end diameters
sections.text:
212 Ver. 25 If a bush is to be replaced, ideally a press should be used to remove and fit the new bush. However not everyone has access to a press. In lieu of a press simply tap out the old bush using a suitable sized socket. To make replacement easier it is suggested to place the new bush in the freezer to chill it and make installation easier by shrinking it. Then the bush should fit enough to use a nut, bolt and washers to slowly pull the new bush in. Alternatively place a piece of wood or a suitable sized socket over the bush and carefully tap it in.

A piece of trivia - On a 17” Model 45 the shaft is 7/8” diameter. Rear Roller shaft with different end diameters Occasionally it has been found that the diameter of the rear roller shaft (where the bearing fits) from one side to the other is different. The bearings usually fit easily on the drive-side; however, they are too tight for the non-drive side (even considering that this side is an interference fit to the shaft). Oddly, some have been seen with a shaft diameter of 15.93 – 15.95 mm which fits between 5/8” (15.87mm) and 16mm. There are three different bearing size ID’s (Inside Diameter) for that same bearing. The 6202 supplied bearings are 16mm and may just ‘flop’ on. The correct bearing is a 5202 (ID 15mm X OD 35mm X W 15.8mm) with a 5/8” ID. There is a third bearing which at 15mm is too small. The shaft should be 5/8” (15.88mm). Before you hit the panic button, check the shaft diameter with vernier calipers. It may be that a light sanding with emery paper may be enough. Worst case , the easiest solution is to get another rear shaft or machine the offending side to 5/8”. Why does this occur? Perhaps when manufactured Scott Bonnar received a batch of bearings with the incorrect ID; and rather than throw them out they matched the non-drive shaft side of the shafts to suit Socket to tap out the bush Credit Fabian De Angelis, SBR Using a suitable bolt, nuts and washers to press the bush in place. Credit Fabian De Angelis, SBR

sections.title: Adjustment issues
sections.text:
213 Ver. 25 the bearings. In any event it appears that “back in the day” tolerances were all over the place. So, if you encounter this issue, you are not the only one. See https://www.facebook.com/groups/1801521490103765/permalink/2882031772052726/ Adjustment issues Chain tight spots Problem: “What's up with the varying chain tension? Starts off slack firms up considerably and then slackens off again.” Explanation: Sprockets have high and low spots and chains get tight spots. Even a new chain with new sprockets can have a tight spot. It is called tolerance stack up. No spinning assembly is perfect. They are all off just a little and then when you put them together and add it all up you get something you can notice. Ask yourself this. Is the centre of the shaft in the centre; is the sprocket centered on the shaft, are all the cuts in the sprocket the exact same depth and are the chain links equally lubricated? Reality is NO. Solution: The best that can be done is to lubricate, tension and align them correctly to minimise the effect. Because of this you must tension your chains to the high spot, but a touch backed off from being super tight.

Chain Tension A critical part of reassembly is getting the chains tensioned. However, adjustments can be awkward as you need to move the sprocket bracket up/down and side to side at the same time as it adjusts two chains at the same time. Not as easy as it sounds especially if the chains are covered in grease. Before attempting to adjust the chains make sure they are on the sprockets correctly. Simple though it may seem, you must push in both directions at same time and tighten. Loosen both bolts on the bar piece. Stand behind the mower and grab the large sprocket and pull it up and up towards you; then with the other hand tighten the top bolt. Chain adjustment, Credit Glenn Russell, SBR

sections.text:

sections.title: The level of the rear roller - Or my mower cuts lower on one side, why?
sections.text:
214 Ver. 25 You should allow 6mm of up and down movement of the chain. If you still have slack in the chain after adjusting or excessive sideways moment you probably need new chains. It is recommended that you view Glenn Russell’s video which can be found at: https://www.facebook.com/glenn.russell.127/videos/10156596932989647/idorvanity=1801521490103 765 The level of the rear roller - Or my mower cuts lower on one side, why? If when mowing you find one side of the cut appears to be consistently lower than the other, the rear roller may not be level with the bedknife and reel. (Note: this is not applicable to Models 33 and 40 as their rear rollers are fixed i.e. not adjustable). It is important to adjust the level of the rear roller to the bed knife and front roller, otherwise one side will be lower, and the cut will not be level. Before even contemplating adjusting the rear roller, it is important that the front roller does not have any play, it must roll true.

Old front rollers wear or flat spot where the shaft and end caps contact, meaning they have play and do not roll true. The front roller shaft shown opposite was worn on each end such that the roller had nearly 4 mm movement. Imagine driving your car with a flat front tyre. If you have the original front roller you may consider upgrading to a new, stainless steel front roller with bearings. It may be that the rear roller is correctly set, but the worn front roller is the cause for the cut “digging" in. Often overlooked are the front roller pivot nuts; if you fail to tighten the roller pivot nuts, you may get uneven cuts. If they are left loose the front roller may move between 5 – 10 mm and cause uneven cuts even with a front roller that has no vertical play in it.

Correctly tensioned chains should be slightly slack, not tightly stretched. Worn front roller shaft. Credit Kym Schrapel, SBR Excessive wear

215 Ver. 25 Unlike the Model 45, Models 33 and 40 do not have an adjustable rear roller; their rear roller level is fixed. Therefore, for these models uneven cutting will be caused by either: • play in the front roller; or • uneven setting of the front rollers’ height. For Model 45’s, to fix the uneven cut you must adjust the rear roller up or down. Below are several methods for setting the level of the rear roller. • Put a piece of plywood strip under the soleplate on the floor or bench. Drop or raise roller to parallel with the surface your machine is sitting on. • Lift the mower onto a couple of milk crates so the rear drums hang free. With the three mounting bolts on the RHS backed off then place a homemade gauge from drum to bed to the blade on LHS - the fixed side. Then put gauge on RHS and adjusted the drum. In this case raising drum with the jack. Finish off with tightening of the bolts. Adjusted without chains on. This method negates tilting the mower on the rear drum which requires the engine oil to be drained. Downside with this method is having to get on your back to do the gauge measurements, the upside is no oil change and having to lift the mower onto the bench by yourself. A tool/gauge can be made from square section rod or angle with a threaded hole complete with a dome headed screw and nut that is located closer to one end of the rod than the other (see below) • The idea is to lay the machine back on its handles (careful here as the engine oil will displace throughout the engine and can cause problems. It is best to do this while doing an oil change, so the sump is dry) You place the rod so that it contacts the rear roller and the front roller with the lip (edge) of the dome headed screw contacting the top edge of the bottom knife blade. Then you slide the rod from one side of the machine to the other, checking as you go that the lip of the screw is in contact with the bottom knife blade if it is then the cut will be level and perfect. Again, it is advisable to replace the front roller shaft if badly worn on both areas where contact is made by the end caps, and the plastic roller ends if possible before you do the check, this will assure that there is no play in this area and negate your efforts36.

36 www.outdoorking.com/forum/ubbthreads.php/topics/20590/adjustment-of-rear-drum.html Adjusting level of rear roller. Credit Kym Schrapel SBR

216 Ver. 25 Another gauge design has been provided by a regular SBR contributor37. The below pictures show the gauge and how to place it. The aim of adjusting the level of the rear roller is so it is parallel with the rail above it or on a horizontal plane with front roller.

The rebuild is an ideal time to adjust the rear roller level before mounting the engine.

37 Brock Bearsby Above Alignment tool (can double to measure the HOC) Credit Brock Bearsby, SBR Above: Placement of alignment tool, Credit Brock Bearsby, SBR

217 Ver. 25 With the rear roller, bedknife and complete front roller assembly fitted, invert the mower as shown below.

Place the Alignment Tool as shown, adjust the level the rear roller and if you have the reel installed you could set the Height of Cut. Another simple tool or jig has been provided by an SBR member to set the rear roller to bedknife level, simply made from 9mm MDF. Basically, you just eyeball it until the bedknife is sitting level.

Above - Rear roller alignment jig Credit Manuel Halicos, SBR Setting the rear roller level Credit Nev Gordon, SBR

218 Ver. 25

One SBR member encountered a mower where even with new front rollers, bushings and roller shafts there was still slight differences from one side to the next. It is possible that the frame was out of alignment. An SBR member38 reports when Rover bought out Scott Bonnar, they did not employ the workers from Scott Bonnar that set up the dies and form work, resulting in out of alignment frames which took some time to rectify. If you want the level millimetre perfect you may have to loosen the soleplate and make fine adjustments to its level, there may be enough play in the mounting holes.

38 https://www.facebook.com/groups/1801521490103765/permalink/2400503366872238/ accessed 13/11/2019 Rear roller positioning and alignment, Credit Manuel Halicos, SBR Mower on MDF alignment jig Credit Manuel Halicos, SBR

sections.title: Reel to the Bed knife/Blade
sections.text:
219 Ver. 25

Reel to the Bed knife/Blade As the saying goes, a picture is worth a thousand words, accordingly for this topic it is recommended that people take the time to view the YouTube video by “junkyarddog87” titled “Scott Bonnar fitting and adjustment of reel and soleplate” (https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=YBbjls92vJQ )39. There are two schools of thought as to the correct setting of the reel to the blade:

1. that you need slight contact and can cut paper; or

2. that you have NO contact but minimal clearance. You do not need to cut paper - the cylinder reel is designed to cut grass. I do not intend to discuss the virtues of these conflicting views rather refer to the manufacturer’s instruction. Scott Bonnar advise in their Model 45 owner’s manual the following: Cutting Setting With use it will become necessary to adjust the cutter closer to the bottom blade. The cutter is correctly set when it LIGHTLY touches the bottom blade along its FULL length. Hard bearing or uneven setting causes excessive or uneven wear to the cutting edges, and places undue load on the engine. Checking the cutting setting

1. Drain the petrol from the fuel tank.

2. Remove the spark plug lead from the spark plug.

3. Tilt the mower back on the rear roller and support the lower handlebars 250mm off the ground. Note: If the mower is to be tilted back any further than this the oil must be drained from the engine sump to avoid possible carburetor and air cleaner damage.

4. Hold a narrow strip of newspaper between the cutter and the bottom blade while carefully rotating the cutter by hand. The setting is correct if the paper is cut when tested at several points along the full length of the blade. The cutter must be free to rotate without binding. Lower the mower back to the ground, refuel with petrol and replace the spark plug lead onto spark plug.

39 https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=YBbjls92vJQ accessed 15/10/2019

sections.title: Adjusting the cutter setting
sections.text:
220 Ver. 25 Adjusting the cutter setting

1. Loosen the clamping nuts (A) on both sides of the mower (see opposite)

2. To set the cutter closer to the bottom blade, turn the adjusting screws (B) on both sides of the mower anti-clockwise until the cutter just contacts the bottom blade at both ends. (see opposite).

3. If the cutter bears down too hard on the bottom blade, turn the adjusting screws clockwise at least one turn, then repeat Step 2 until the blade is set correctly.

4. Retighten the clamping nuts (A).

5. Retest the cutter setting. After resetting the cutter, the cutter drive chain will need to be checked and adjusted. If your reel and bed knife are set up correctly there should only be a light amount of contact, and this will not be a problem. The reel should almost spin freely if you spin it by hand with a light “zing” noise. An example of how this should sound is demonstrated in a short clip by Brock Bearsby40. Some pointers when making the above adjustment, especially if you have just reinstalled the reel: • Note – Original height adjuster threads are left hand threaded on earlier vintage Model 45’s however some adjuster screws now available e.g. from GA Spares are threaded opposite (clockwise to lower) to the originals. Some later ones are right hand threaded from the factory. (LH thread and RH thread refers to the direction of the thread – NOT the side of the mower that the adjuster screw is fitted.) o The rationale of the left hand thread on the adjuster screws is that when you tighten with your screwdriver it unthreads which lowers or tightens reel to bedknife. • Buy yourself a nut spanner, makes turning the nut on the adjusting screw so much easier. Alternatively, an 11mm open end spanner will do. • Make sure the washers for the adjustment screw are on top of the brackets/right beneath the screw head. • Reel placement within the housings, ensuring it is sitting correctly, and you have the correct top half of the bearing holders matching the bottom half.

40 https://www.facebook.com/groups/1801521490103765/permalink/1950336878555558/ accessed 21/11/2019 A B Adjusting the Cutter setting Credit Kym Schrapel, SBR Cutter bearing shield. Credit Luciano Melito, SBR

sections.title: Inconsistent contact between the reel and bedknife
sections.text:
221 Ver. 25 • Cutter bearing shields (washers) need to be tight or they will cause damage to the reel shaft. Both need to spin with the reel and not touching the housing. • The Reel and bed knife need to be a “matched set” i.e. both require grinding for the perfect cut. Furthermore, the bed knife needs to be sharpened whilst attached to the sole plate to ensure it is square.

Inconsistent contact between the reel and bedknife Should you find that in the process of making the above adjustment, not all the blades on the reel contact the bed knife or contact is not made in e.g. the middle of the bedknife; then you have an issue. There are several possible causes: • a single blade grind may have been conducted on the reel blades. You will need to have the reel re-ground by an experienced professional doing a spin grind so that all reel blades will evenly contact the bed knife. • There may be a bend/bow in the bedknife. Place a straight edge along the blade to check if it is straight. o Even new bedknife blades may have a slight bend/bow, that is why it is recommended that they be ground. • Less likely, this could also be an indicator of a slight bending in the reels’ shaft. A final word of warning - DO NOT try and bring the reel down on the bedknife and let the knife sharpen the blades by contact – it will end in tears, a damaged blade and probably a damaged reel.

Adjusting screw set up – note washer position. Credit Jason Mihatsch Use nut spanner on clamping nut After adjusting the reel to bedknife

- check and adjust the chain tension Damaged bedknife Credit (removed), SBR

sections.title: Primary clutch setup/adjustment
sections.text:
222 Ver. 25 Primary clutch setup/adjustment Please refer to the above clutch issues in relation to the required gaps between the clutch, engine and thrust pad etc. This is a final summary of the steps in adjusting the primary clutch:

1. Make sure clutch body is 5 mm away from motor.

2. Make sure the square headed locating bolts or the cotter pin are tight.

3. Turn the clutch lever/handle to engage the reel.

4. Undo the locking nut (on the clutch fork) ,turn the bolt inwards or outwards to achieve a point where the thrust pad meets the clutch cone. There should be a 1 mm gap between the thrust pad and the cone.

5. Tighten the locking nut.

Remember – The clutch fork starts its life correctly adjusted. (1 – 2 mm gap). As the cork lining in the primary clutch wears slowly over time the cork gets thinner. The thinner it gets the closer the cone moves towards the thrust pad, plastic or brass, as this gap becomes thinner you then create friction/heat when the lever is in the cutting position. Brass or aluminium deal with the heat better however it’s not a set and forget setup. Adjust the fork to allow that 1 – 2 mm gap again and no problem until down the track the cork is worn to the point of being unserviceable then simply replace the cork. Drive Clutch Question about drive speed - is it based on engine speed? i.e., the faster the engine is running, the faster your drive speed? Answer – it’s about clutch adjustment and even to the point of "feathering" the clutch using the lever, rather than have it pulled in all the way. Experienced SBR member Clinton Honor sums up the question simply:

1. The clutch cable and how tight it is and (if you feel that that lever is too tight and it is very responsive, take the screw adjustment in a tough more. Beware, only small adjustments (e.g. ¼ turns are needed to make a big difference.

2. The long 7/16 bolt with the loose nut on the bracket on the inside of the frame. Tighter it is, the more the intermediate sprocket is nearly touching the drive cork, so it becomes very touchy. The balance is the clutch sprocket is nearly touching and the cable allows the position of where you feel comfortable lifting the lever to wherever you want. Also, these adjustments allow for wear with the cable stretching over time.

If you have an engine with an air flap governor , they don't respond so well to load, so people tend to run the engine fast, and feather the drive clutch.

sections.title: Parts Compatibility
sections.text:
224 Ver. 25 Parts Compatibility The following is a guide as to some of the parts which are/are not compatible across Models 33, 40 and

45. Supercut and Diplomat models have no main compatible parts with the Model 45. Part Model 33 Model 40 Model 45 Cutter bearing housing

Cutter bearings

Cutter bearing shield

Rear roller bearing

Pawl

N/A

Clutch cone lining (cork)

Only for later model Lawn Kings fitted with Mod 45 Clutch N/A

Chain tensioner

Only for Lawn Kings with all chain drive. SB Part number 451258 N/A

Only for solid deck models. SB Part number 451258 Clutch lining (cork) Landroll/drive clutch

Thrust pad

N/A

Thrust race

Clutch fork assembly incl fork spring

N/A

PTO Bearing 1202 self-aligning

Mod 40 has 2 self aligning bearings

Chains

Cylinder Reels

Front roller end caps

Front roller tube 14” ONLY.

Nut/bolt set

Majority from Mod 45 solid are compatible

Majority from Mod 45 solid are compatible

sections.title: Clutch Cork parts compatibility
sections.text:
225 Ver. 25 Front roller endcaps are compatible across Models 33, 40, 45, 430 and 590. Reels, top decks and side plates are NOT interchangeable across models, they are model specific. Clutch Cork parts compatibility The clutch cork for the landroll clutch (drives the rear roller) is the same across Models 33, 40 and 45.

Belt sizes Edger attachment • A38BL Models 430 and 590 • Gates 6840 powerated belt is preferred to A38 or OEM No. A05456 which has been reported as being too tight. • Truflex 2400(4L400) • Dayco 13A1015 • SBR member Richie Thain has tried the above belts and has found the 13A 1005 to be a better fit. It has not loaded the tensioner spring excessively; the cog belt forms around the PTO pulley better than a solid V belt and the 10mm shorter length gives solid, positive drive. The belt ejector works fine and the reel does not run on. Model 46 • A29 Model 38 edger • M21 (L 210 - 3L is the section and 210 the circumference). Model 690 • A35 Model 33 • Either a 15325 or A32 belt fits.

Above - Mod 38 OEM belt Edger attachment Credit Steve Berghofer

sections.title: Edger Attachment
sections.text:
226 Ver. 25 Edger Attachment The edger attachment was available on Models 33, 40, 45 430 and 590’s. They were not popular. Despite coming standard ON THE Diplomats it has been reported that most were removed when they were sold, probably because their “toe cutter” potential was realized by the mower shops. Belt size On the Model 45 it is a A38BL. It is not known whether the same belt fits the attachment on the other models. Seized shaft As per the opposite picture, the shaft in the pulley may be seized. There is a key/keyway. As so often is the case heat and liberal amounts of penetrant are advised. If you set it up so you carefully tap the shaft downwards (with nut on so as not to damage the threat). Pulley for bolt on edger attachment? The question has been asked “Does anybody know how to remove the pulley attachment from the actual mower? The hole between the chain case and the pulley needs to be undone and replaced with a nut on in its place. If it is on tight, place a piece of wood between the reel and bed knife and then with a large screwdriver or rod placed into the hole undo it.

Right - Disassembled pulley/shaft Credit SB Renos, SBR Left - Seized chat and pulley Credit Peter Hayes SBR Pulley for edger attachment, Credit Jordan Wall, SBR. Insert a rod or large screwdriver in this hole

sections.title: MODEL 33
sections.text:
227 Ver. 25 MODEL 33 How do I remove the clutch off the engine?

Not all clutch half’s will be secured with a cotter pin or grub screws. The standard Model 33 (not the Lawn King variant) has a nut that tightens a crankcase taper sleeve within the engine clutch body. Removal in this case is simple, undo the nut, give the clutch a tap towards the engine will help to release the sleeve (use a piece of wood and knock the clutch housing back in towards the engine using a hammer) and then use a gear puller.

Model 33 clutch half, Credit Kris John, SBR Taper sleeve inside clutch body Credit Kym Schrapel, SBR Crankcase taper sleeve in the clutch body Credit Kym Schrapel, SBR Crankcase taper Credit Kym Schrapel, SBR

sections.title: Which clutch?
sections.text:
228 Ver. 25 Which clutch? Model 33 parts diagrams for the more common belt drive model 330413 show no cork lining. This clutch is simply metal on metal. However, the model 330026, shows a cork lining for the chain drive Lawn King variant which has the Model 45 clutch setup. The Model 33’s Lawn King variant had a Model 45 cone, rearward facing clutch lever with a spacer (circled opposite) - the spacer was needed because the Model 45 cone is shorter than the more elongated Model 33 cone. In this case the clutch lining is the same as a Model 45. Standard or non Lawn King Model 33’s have a rearward facing lever. It is possible (unconfirmed) that a Model 45 clutch lever could be used on the Model 33 however it will be forward facing and may work. Most people wouldn’t spot the difference. Clutch Thrust Race The drive clutch setup for Model 33’s is different to the Model 45”s . Squeezing on the clutch lever causes the clutch ramp to move outwards and the clutch thrust race contacts the clutch plate resulting in the rear roller drive. The Clutch Thrust Race is shown as part 61 below. I have been reliably informed that the clutch thrust race is no longer available. However, all is not lost. SBR member Adam Blaylock has found the Timken “T77-904A1” fits41. Alternatively awork around exists using the engine thrust race (the Model 33 and 45 part is the same). The internal diameter of the Engine Thrust Race is slightly less than that of the clutch thrust race. Therefore, the clutch bushing needs to be machined down fractionally so that it will fit in the engine thrust race.

41 https://www.facebook.com/groups/1801521490103765/permalink/2823510284571542/ accessed 26 May 2021 Model 33 with Mod 45 clutch and spacer. Credit Gumtree Clutch Thrust Race Clutch Bushing Clutch Bushing Clutch ramp Model 33 Rear bearing holder showing clutch bushing and clutch ramp Credit Kym Schrapel, SBR Model 33 Parts Diagram extract

sections.title: PTO Bearing Shield
sections.text:
229 Ver. 25 PTO Bearing Shield If when rebuilding your Model 33 you find you have the self-aligning PTO bearing exposed to the elements, you have a problem. Do not forget to put the bearing shield in and the circlip behind the shield. Even if you have a sealed bearing you still need the bearing shield to prevent debris entering the bearing.

Removal of non-drive rear bearing holder. “How does the non-drive bearing cap come off?” This question relates specifically to the Model 33. With reference to the opposite parts diagram, the bearing (43) fits tightly inside the bearing holder (45) so firstly lubricate through the oil hole in the holder. I used the two-claw hammer method with couple of jemmy bars for extra leverage with the rear shaft still bolted together at the drive side.

Model 33 extract from parts diagram. Source SBR PTO self-aligning bearing missing the bearing shield Credit Fabian De Angelis, SBR Bearing shield Credit Fabian De Angelis, SBR

230 Ver. 25 Note the oil hole is facing upwards, come time to put it back together remember to fit the bearing holder with the oil hole upwards.

Trouble removing the rear rollers The Model 33 drive clutch lever is a challenging piece of engineering to work around (I am joking). On a more serious note, difficulty can be encountered removing the real rollers especially when the woodruff key is stuck in the drive side of the rear roller shaft. At best you need to remove the woodruff key off the shaft, remove the bearing shield that sits inside the housing in front of the lever plate and awkwardly slide the plate off the shaft. If the key is stuck use heat on it. If that fails then as I have done - remove the front end(roller, soleplate and reel), remove the engine plate (deck) then you are left with just the rear roller and side plates. At this stage you can work the non-drive side out of the bearing holder and work towards the drive side taking the roller apart and so on.

Model 33 rear bearing holder Credit Lloyd Cornish, SBR Oil hole Model 33 drive clutch lever and rear roller bearing holder

sections.title: Which way does the Bearing housing cover go?
sections.text:
231 Ver. 25

Which way does the Bearing housing cover go? The bearing housing cover goes on the left hand or drive side. The tab that sticks up fits inside the bearing housing.

Lubrication – Model 33 • Clutch Thrust Pad - apply three drops of oil in the thrust pad oil hole. • Clutch inner cone - apply three drops of oil in the clutch inner cone oil hole. Note: it may be necessary to engage the clutch and gently pull on the engine starter cord to align the inner cone oil hole with the clutch cover hole. • Front roller - apply six drops of oil to each side of the shaft. • Reel Bearing holders – move the metal tabs on the top of the holders to expose the oil hole - apply oil. • PTO Bearing – move the metal circular ring to expose the oil hole - apply oil. • Rear bearing holders - move the metal circular ring to expose the oil hole - apply oil. • Drive chains - every 10 hours of operation remove the chain case side cover and apply a ‘chain oil’ to the drive chains. Replace side cover. • Rear rollers – on each roller (except the Lawnking which has the Mod 45 rear rollers) there is a screw which if it has not been removed for some time may require some impact driver love taps to free up. The screws cover a feeder tube that you pour a small amount of oil into to lubricate the inner shaft of the rear roller. Alternatively, with a long tube, spray WD40 or similar into the feeder tube. Above left and right - Placement of the bearing housing cover Credit Fabian De Angelis, SBR

sections.text:

Note: it may be necessary to engage the clutch and gently pull on the engine starter cord to align the inner cone oil hole with the clutch cover hole. • Front roller - apply six drops of oil to each side of the shaft. • Reel Bearing holders – move the metal tabs on the top of the holders to expose the oil hole - apply oil. • PTO Bearing – move the metal circular ring to expose the oil hole - apply oil. • Rear bearing holders - move the metal circular ring to expose the oil hole - apply oil. • Drive chains - every 10 hours of operation remove the chain case side cover and apply a ‘chain oil’ to the drive chains. Replace side cover. • Rear rollers – on each roller (except the Lawnking which has the Mod 45 rear rollers) there is a screw which if it has not been removed for some time may require some impact driver love taps to free up. The screws cover a feeder tube that you pour a small amount of oil into to lubricate the inner shaft of the rear roller. Alternatively, with a long tube, spray WD40 or similar into the feeder tube. Above left and right - Placement of the bearing housing cover Credit Fabian De Angelis, SBR

sections.title: Rear roller bearings
sections.text:
232 Ver. 25

Rear roller bearings The respective parts diagrams for Models 33 and 45 show identical part numbers for the rear roller bearings (1129017). These are not available from bearing suppliers as they are Scott Bonnar specific. They can be sourced from regular SBR suppliers. Irrespective of where you get them from they are not cheap as a machined sleeve is added to a standard bearing to fit the larger outside diameter. SBR Member Adam Blaylock has found the ‘Nice’ “NO-400-33” bearing fits the rear roller, albeit they are not sealed bearings as the originals were. Fixings list Please refer to the Model 45 solid deck fixings list as a guide for the Model 33’s. Most can be applied to the Model 33 except for the likes of the handlebar plate and the reel bearing holders.

Model 33 rear roller- screw for oil feeder tube. Credit Kym Schrapel, SBR

sections.title: Adjusting the reel to the bedknife
sections.text:
233 Ver. 25 Adjusting the reel to the bedknife The middle purchase block is fixed so the top adjuster moves the reel up or down. Understanding that, make your adjustment up or down with the top adjustment screw then tighten the bottom one onto the middle purchase block whilst holding the top one; and finally use the top lock nut to lock it all.

Missing Taper sleeve pin The pin in the taper sleeve may break or be missing in a tear down. If so, it is unlikely that one will be available from a mower store. The pin is reported as 3/16’ diameter (unconfirmed) and the best suggestion made is to match up a drill bit the same size and cut it to the correct length.

Purchase block Adjuster screw Purchase block Adjuster screw Lock nut Pin Above left and right - Model 33 and 40 - reel to bedknife adjustment Credit Kym Schrapel, SBR

sections.title: Model 33 PTO modification to support a model 45 clutch.
sections.text:
234 Ver. 25 Model 33 PTO modification to support a model 45 clutch. If you have a Model 330413 (not Model 330026, the Lawn King version) and want to use a Model 45 clutch, you should consider changing the PTO shaft. The reason being the Model 33 clutch cone is longer than the shorter Model 45 clutch. The Model 45 clutch will therefore be a good 25 mm from the engine if the original shaft is used. It is workable but not ideal. SBR member Fabian De Angelis sought the assistance of Trident Mowers in Perth to fabricate a new PTO shaft where the key is moved up and then put a spacer in. This in effect is the Model 33 Lawn King setup. Trident Mowers have the measurements for the PTO shaft and the spacer. Contact them (Lincoln Mead) for ordering and pricing.

Left: The PTO shaft on the left is the newly fabricated version for the Model 45 clutch, the one on the right is the standard Model 33 version. Credit Fabian De Angelis, SBR. Above: New PTO shaft and spacer Credit Fabian De Angelis, SBR

sections.title: Belt jockey assembly
sections.text:
235 Ver. 25 Belt jockey assembly The belt jockey assembly consists of two pulleys which enables the tensioning of the drive belt. Referring to the below parts diagram extract, the jockey is disassembled by: • Unscrew/remove the nut (120) • Pull the assembly (116 - 119) off the shaft. The washer (103) and shield (101) should remain on the shaft of (115). If it will not come off easily then a gear puller may need to be used. • The bearing (118) is held in place by a circlip (119). Remove the circlip and remove the bearing - may have to push it out. • Remove the spacer (117) from the pulley (116). • You may now clean up the parts and check the condition of the bearing. Installation is the reverse procedure.

The bearing (118) is a 6202 bearing.

Belt jockey or tensioner Credit Kym Schrapel, SBR Belt Jockey - Extract of Mod 33 parts diagram Credit SBR Files

sections.title: Model 38 edger
sections.text:
236 Ver. 25 Model 38 edger Series 1 or Series 2? Model 38 guru Shane Kosteszyn advises he is not sure whether there was a Series 2 other than a model 38 which just earned the “Series 2” nickname. The Series 1 had a wider front track, Kirby engine, no handlebar throttle control, hand lever on the handlebar for fine adjustment of the cutting blade, white top handlebars and thumb wheel with either a single brass ID plate or an alloy plus brass ID plate or just a single alloy plate on the very last of them. If there is a model 38 Series 2, then there was a Series 1 1/2 which had the Series 1 wide track chassis with the 3hp Briggs engine (not the 2hp as is common on the 38), the starter cord facing the opposite way to all the other Briggs engines, top bars white as is the thumb wheel. Shane thinks these would be the rarest of the Scott Bonnar edger’s. The Series 1 and the early model38's were painted in the darker green whilst the later model 38's were in the Olive Green. Things like the paint colour, stickers and ID plates mirrored those of the model 45's. In addition to the “standard” green colours, some also came out in black, with orange Briggs & Stratton motor, which were called a “Rebel”. Replacement Bearings The original belt tensioner bearing is Koyo 6003RS (Japan) the other 2 bearings in the blade shaft arm are the same. Worn idler. A common problem with the Model 38 Edger is wear to the idler, grooves wear in the bearing sleeves. SBR member Shane Kosteszyn, has made a new sleeve from machine grade nylon instead of steel changing the width slightly to make better contact with the belt and be kinder on the belt.

New and worn Mod 38 idler sleeve. Shane Kosteszyn

sections.title: Engine plate/bracket removal
sections.text:
237 Ver. 25 Engine plate/bracket removal The only thing holding the bracket on is the circled (opposite) 11mm or 7/16 bolt. Simply remove the bolt and the engine plate should pivot easily, give it a squirt of penetrant and it should just slide out. If it doesn’t, slide a bar or wooden dowel thru the front hole and give it a tap, it should be that easy. That said, experience by several people indicates a good soaking with penetrant or even an application of heat may be required after removal of the bolt.

Blade Cover Bolt size The blade cover is held on by 3/16 bolts.

Front belt pulley removal The front belt pulley is screwed on to the shaft. There is a small hole in the pulley track; wedge something solid in the pulley and clamp the blade side tight and unscrew the pulley with the leverage or vice versa hold the pulley and turn the blade boss. If it is hard to remove the application of heat to the pulley centre may be required. You then can tap the centre out with a nylon hammer from the pulley side . Inside are two bearings and a spacer inside, need to remove circlips to get the bearings out.

Front belt pulley. Credit Rodney Unicomb, SBR Model 38 engine bracket Credit Adam Kavanagh, SBR

sections.title: Fitting New Blades Or Brushes
sections.text:
238 Ver. 25

Fitting New Blades Or Brushes The following is as per the Scott Bonnar Operating Instructions for the Model 38 Edger.

1. Turn the rotor to allow access to cutter bolts

2. Assemble parts in the same sequence as illustrated (opposite)

3. Make certain that the washer with the round hole is placed on the bolt before inserting the bolt in the rotor.

4. Tighten the nut so that the blade or brush is firmly held but can be pivoted Always replace both blades or brushes renewing one only will cause unbalance and excessive vibration. The steel cutting blade is designed for general purpose edging, such as trimming between a concrete strip and the turf or deep cutting a raised or trenched edge. The brush cutter requires less accuracy in control as it will 'give' on contact with concrete. It is most suitable for trimming growth that has encroached on paths or where contours make it difficult to accurately follow an edge. Unfortunately, the brush style blades are no longer available. Belt pulley Credit Becky Privitera, SBR Blade/Brush Assembly sequence. Source Scott Bonnar Operating Instructions 38 Series Power Edger

sections.title: Fitting a new engine
sections.text:
239 Ver. 25 An alternative to the above blades is to use a Rover Edger Blade (230mm dimeter, 19mm center hole, OEM Part No. A09022 ME63222G)

Fitting a new engine Installing a new (Briggs and Stratton) engine can be done however it requires some care and thought. The issue is the wheels foul on the new motor if fitting on the original mounting holes. New engine mount holes on the base plate need to be drilled to allow the wheels to work. Leave the wheels on, just put the new engine on and move back towards the handlebar until you’ve got clearance. You will also have to modify the “L-shaped” bracket between the base plate and body of the edger.

Rover edger blade on a Model 38. Credit Kym Schrapel, SBR Left: New engine mount holes on base plate Credit SB- FabandParts, SBR Above: Modified L-bracket fitted on top of the engine. Credit SB-FabandParts, SBR

240 Ver. 25

New B&S engine being fitted to a Model 38, Credit Darrall Suter, SBR New B&S engine fitted to Model 38 Credit George SB- FabandParts, SBR

sections.title: Model 40
sections.text:
241 Ver. 25 Model 40 Removal of Rubber Friction Roller A common problem with the Model 40 is the rubber friction roller wears and does not make enough contact with the Friction Drum. New or refurbished the roller is around 55mm OD, but if it’s down to about 44 mm OD then it’s worn too much to make enough contact. The following is how I removed the rubber roller: With the drive unit cover removed you will see the rubber friction drive roller attached to the shaft. Problems arise with its removal due to the minimal gap between the crankcase and the back of the roller, and the rubber of the roller. Care needs to be taken to ensure the rubber is not damaged. • Lubricate the centre of the drive roller with CRC, WD40, Inox or whatever (I prefer Loctite Freeze and Release) because it will be tight. • Use a spin spanner or, • With a set of (preferably) old pointy nosed pliers remove the inner centre roller key retainer (normal thread direction). You may have to hold the rubber wheel with multi grips, if so wrap a rag around it so you do not damage the rubber.

Now comes the challenge to remove the friction roller. • Because the rubber drive is so close to the engine the ideal tool to use is a bearing puller (not a gear puller – they are different. See Chapter 4, Tool List) • If you do not have access to a bearing puller all is not lost. Source a large flat washer and cut it out as opposite. I beveled the points to enable it to be Above - Rubber friction drive wheel Credit Kym Schrapel, SBR Rubber friction drive wheel and roller key retainer, Credit Kym Schrapel SBR Right - Fabricated washer Credit Kym Schrapel, SBR

sections.title: Removal of Rubber Friction Roller
sections.text:
