I beveled the points to enable it to be Above - Rubber friction drive wheel Credit Kym Schrapel, SBR Rubber friction drive wheel and roller key retainer, Credit Kym Schrapel SBR Right - Fabricated washer Credit Kym Schrapel, SBR

sections.title: Engine swap - Villiers to Briggs & Stratton
sections.text:
242 Ver. 25 forced between the crankcase and the rubber friction roller. • The centre of the washer must be greater than 19mm i.e. the crankshaft diameter and ideally so that it covers the centre and rubber of the friction roller. • Work the washer over the crankshaft between the crankcase and the friction roller. You may have to use a thin cold chisel or even a screwdriver to hammer the washer in position. • Once in position set up a gear puller as per the mockup below. The gear puller should grip the washer rather than the friction roller. Use a small gear puller i.e. a 3 inch as any larger and you will not get in between the crankcase and the rubber drive roller. Remove the friction roller with the gear puller. Mockup of washer/gear puller on the drive wheel. Credit Kym Schrapel, SBR Engine swap - Villiers to Briggs & Stratton The Model 40 originally came with a Villiers 75cc, 4 stroke engine and later with a Briggs & Stratton (B&S) engine. Due to persistent problems with the worn-out Villiers engine I replaced it with a Briggs and Stratton 550; 3.5 HP I/C engine. Changeover of the engines is not a simple matter of bolt off/bolt on because of different mounting hole patterns between the two engines and the presence of the rubber friction wheel. The following are the steps I took to swap the engines. This is provided for information only, to offer suggestion/s. In no way do I profess to be offering or recommending the best method, this is just what I did. If you choose to follow and it goes wrong, then be it on your head. Step 1 – engine removal Removal of the Villiers engine is a simple process achieved by: • removing the four engine mounting bolts • disconnecting the throttle cable • removing the cover over the drive mechanism

243 Ver. 25 Step 2 – friction roller removal With the engine removed you will see the rubber friction roller attached to the shaft. This must be removed for installation on the new engine. above for removal of the rubber drive. You are now ready to refit your mower with the new engine. A couple of things to note. The Villiers engine was mounted onto the deck with a spacer. This spacer raised the engine, so the shaft was high enough to enable the drive wheel to engage. The new B&S drive shaft should not require a spacer unless the friction roller is worm significantly. Further, the B&S drive shaft is longer than the old Villiers which will be addressed below.

Step 3 – install friction roller on new engine • Because the B&S shaft is longer I cut a tube steel sleeve to fit over the shaft as shown above. A hole was drilled in the sleeve to allow the sleeve to be lubricated. Ensure the exposed shaft is the same length as the drive wheel. This sleeve is intended to prevent the rear roller moving back towards the engine (yes it has happened). • The friction roller may now be placed on the drive shaft, remembering to use the key to ensure the wheel is locked. • The B&S shaft unlike the Villiers shaft has a threaded hole in the end of the shaft which takes a ¼” bolt. Fix a bolt as shown above with a spring and flat washer to ensure the friction roller and key does not move. This is a must do!

B & S shaft showing sleeve cut to length to allow the rubber drive to fit on the shaft Credit Kym Schrapel, SBR Drive mechanism with drive unit operating handle circled. Credit Kym Schrapel, SBR Drive unit tensioner Credit Kym Schrapel, SBR Friction drum Sleeve on new crankshaft Credit Kym Schrapel, SBR

244 Ver. 25 Step 4 – new engine mounting Now we are getting to the stage of working out where to place the new engine however there are some preparations to be done. • Ensure the drive mechanism is in place as above left. • As per above right, back off the drive unit tensioner, a tube spanner is the ideal tool. • Using thin cardboard (breakfast cereal box works) make a template of the base of the new engine, including the mounting holes. • Mask the deck where the engine is to be going, (do not want to scratch the new paint) it makes it easier to mark where the engine will go. • Place to engine on the deck with the friction drive wheel inside the red friction drum. • Check that the pull side of the engine does not contact the side of the mower(below). If the cover of the pull start is resting on the mower side you will have to file/grind the side down, so the engine sits flat on the deck. • Check the engine does not touch the handlebars. • Engage the drive unit operating handle (circled in above picture) ensuring the friction drum engages with the friction roller and locks down. May require adjustment of the drive tensioner. • Note at this stage how well the friction roller engages with the drum will determine whether the engine will need to be shimmed up. This will depend on how worn the rubber on the friction roller has become. A re-vulcanized rubber roller will NOT need the engine to be shimmed. The friction roller does not have to be lined up dead center with the red drum, it may be forward on the deck (and the engine) but MUST be parallel to the drive mechanism i.e. no “toe in/toe out”. • When you are happy that the engine is in the correct position, trace around the base of the engine. • Remove the engine and place the engine template on the deck over the marked area and mark the hole positions. • Centre punch where the holes are to be. o CHECK and DOUBLE CHECK, the next step is a “no return’ step if you are wrong. • Drill the holes. • Mount the engine.

Pull start guard showing the mower side plate which was filed down. Credit Kym Schrapel, SBR

sections.title: Removal of cutter or reel sprocket
sections.text:
245 Ver. 25 Step 4 (a) If the rubber friction wheel has worn such that it is not sufficiently engaging with the drum you may need to raise the engine to compensate for the wear. This I did, using flat steel strap Use the base template to mark the holes, center punch and drill. I suggest you prime and paint before fitting. Strategic placement of the shims may also hide the mounting holes of the old Villiers engine. SB Fabrication and Parts can re-vulcanise the rubber of the friction drive, negating the need to raise the engine. Step 5 – connect throttle cable. The engine has now been mounted. Whether you use the engine throttle lever, or the original throttle lever and cable attached to the engine is a personal choice. Should you have a damaged throttle cable do not fret, there is a simple and cheap solution available from a bicycle shop. Cables with various ends are available which fit the original throttle lever. Some types have barrel ends, you may have to file them down slightly to fit. The cable outer and caps can also be bought at the same time. Suggest that you apply lubricant e.g. thin film of grease over the cable before inserting it in the outer. Fixing the cable to the carburetor is a simple matter, discussed previously. Removal of cutter or reel sprocket Model 40 reel sprockets are screwed on, there is no retaining nut as with the Model 45 – do not attempt to remove it with a gear puller.

• Place a piece of wood between the reel and bedknife to stop it from rotating.

• Insert a metal rod or large screwdriver through the hole in the collar of the sprocket shaft and give it a tap - reverse direction. o The reel rotates anticlockwise. If the reel sprocket's thread were right hand, it would unscrew itself when the reel spins and cuts grass, so it must be a reverse or left hand thread. Close up of engine mounted on shims Credit Kym Schrapel, SBR Cable end from bicycle shop Credit Kym Schrapel, SBR Model 40 reel or cutter sprocket Credit Kym Schrapel, SBR

sections.title: Which way does the Bearing housing cover go?
sections.text:
246 Ver. 25

• Unscrew the sprocket rotating it clockwise, as if you were tightening a normal right hand thread. Which way does the Bearing housing cover go? The bearing housing cover goes on the left hand or drive side. The tab that sticks up fits inside the bearing housing.

Adjusting the reel to the bedknife The middle purchase block is “fixed”, so the top adjuster moves the reel up or down. Understanding that make your adjustment up or down with the top adjustment screw then tighten the bottom one onto the middle purchase block whilst holding the top one; and finally use the top lock nut to lock it all.

Above left and right - Placement of the bearing housing cover Credit Fabian De Angelis, SBR Purchase block Adjuster screw Purchase block Adjuster screw Lock nut Above left and right - Model 33 and 40 - reel to bedknife adjustment Credit Kym Schrapel, SBR

sections.title: Adjusting the reel to the bedknife
sections.text:

• Unscrew the sprocket rotating it clockwise, as if you were tightening a normal right hand thread. Which way does the Bearing housing cover go? The bearing housing cover goes on the left hand or drive side. The tab that sticks up fits inside the bearing housing.

Understanding that make your adjustment up or down with the top adjustment screw then tighten the bottom one onto the middle purchase block whilst holding the top one; and finally use the top lock nut to lock it all.

Above left and right - Placement of the bearing housing cover Credit Fabian De Angelis, SBR Purchase block Adjuster screw Purchase block Adjuster screw Lock nut Above left and right - Model 33 and 40 - reel to bedknife adjustment Credit Kym Schrapel, SBR

sections.title: Original Villiers engine parts
sections.text:
247 Ver. 25 Original Villiers engine parts The original Villiers 75cc, 4 stroke engine is a 60 year old engine and parts are now hard to source. Two potential parts sources are: • DG Engine Parts Address: 19 Faulkner Avenue, Belmont, Western Australia 6104 Phone: (08) 9277 7733 Email: dgengineparts@bigpond.com.au Web: https://www.dgengineparts.com.au/ • Vintage Air Cooled Engine Spare Address: 8 Boat Harbour Close, Summerland Point NSW 2259 Phone: 02 4976 1653 Mobile 0408 377 213 Email: stephen.woods1@bigpond.com Web: https://vaces.com.au/ Below is a list of some Villiers parts for the original 75 cc engine from DG Engine Parts

Villiers 75 cc rebuild part numbers. Credit Arthur Ray, SBR

sections.title: Can I replace the friction roller with a Model 45 clutch?
sections.text:
248 Ver. 25 If you have the original 75cc Villiers engine and want to replace the air filter, a Ryco “A245” will fit. Can I replace the friction roller with a Model 45 clutch?

Question: Can I do away with the rubber friction clutch and replace it with a SB45 clutch? Answer: The short answer is NO. Just get the friction roller re- vulcanised. The explanation is relatively simple. Have a look at the two shafts, the PTO and crankshaft do not line up. They are offset with the crankshaft forward of the PTO shaft so the friction roller and the drum work. It is not possible to bolt on a model 45 clutch (or any other centrifugal clutch) as the two shafts don’t line up. Even if the engine is moved rearwards to line the shafts up the engine would overhang the rear of the engine deck and foul on the handlebars.

Air filter for Villiers 75 cc. Credit Arthur Ray, SBR

sections.title: Models 430 and 590
sections.text:
249 Ver. 25 Models 430 and 590 SBR member Shane Kosteszyn succinctly describes the difference between the Model 430 and 590 as follows: “The Model 590 is not a Diplomat, but a standalone model proceeded by the Model 430 called the Diplomat. They are both belt-driven however the Diplomat has the more conventional cork clutch whereas the 590 is direct drive meaning squeeze the hand lever and the reel goes as does the rear roller.” Rear roller pawls from Model 45’s are compatible. Note: the clutch cables of Models 430 and 590 mowers are NOT interchangeable. Piece of trivia – the 430 and 590 use metric fixings (nuts/bolts). Reel to bedknife adjustment Unlike the Model 45, the reel in the Diplomat and Model 590 is fixed and the sole plate is adjustable. The reel has no adjustment like the Model 45. Below are the instructions copied from the Scott Bonnar Instruction Book for the Model 590.

Unlike the Model 45, the reel in the Diplomat is fixed and the sole plate is adjustable. Cutter setting instructions, Instruction Book for the Model 590 Credit Harry Prosser SBR (Files)

sections.title: Reel to bedknife adjustment
sections.text:

They are both belt-driven however the Diplomat has the more conventional cork clutch whereas the 590 is direct drive meaning squeeze the hand lever and the reel goes as does the rear roller.” Rear roller pawls from Model 45’s are compatible. Note: the clutch cables of Models 430 and 590 mowers are NOT interchangeable. Piece of trivia – the 430 and 590 use metric fixings (nuts/bolts). Below are the instructions copied from the Scott Bonnar Instruction Book for the Model 590.

Unlike the Model 45, the reel in the Diplomat is fixed and the sole plate is adjustable. Cutter setting instructions, Instruction Book for the Model 590 Credit Harry Prosser SBR (Files)

sections.title: Loss of drive
sections.text:
250 Ver. 25

Loss of drive Commonly encountered with the Diplomats (Model 430 and Model 590) is a loss of drive. Check the following: • Condition and tension of the drive belt • Finally, and the most probable cause is a worn or cracked plastic drive gear within the rear roller. Cracks in the drive gear such as shown below will cause it to slip on the center ring and that’s how you drive is lost. (These are available from SBR member Rob Terzo (also sold via eBay).

OEM 3D printed drive gear Credit Rob Terzo, SBR Left - Diplomat - check drive belt condition and tension Credit Ryan Anthony, SBR Cracked drive gear can cause loss of drive. Credit Stephen Brown, SBR

sections.title: How to remove the rear roller.
sections.text:
251 Ver. 25 How to remove the rear roller. Referring to the opposite and below pictures; there is a bolt or an Allen key screw on the underside of the circled area (you can see if you look though the back) - remove that and the whole pulley slides out. The whole alloy pulley & clutch come off as one, normally look where the catcher goes on - just above the left hand rear roller is the Allen head screw - take it out, spray CRC (or whatever you use) in the hole and let it soak, then gently lever the clutch off. It can be very tight and possibly corroded on. The plastic drive gear is inside the rear roller.

Drive gear removal There is basically one option to remove the plastic drive gear. Break it, use a hammer and screwdriver AND wear eye protection as pieces tend to fly off. If you can’t get the center of the gear off the shaft it is easier to pull the drum apart from the other end. Then you can slide the main shaft out of the drum. Then it is easy to just tap the center hub off and file it down ready for the new gear. And yes, just smash the old plastic gear off to get the center ring out. Unfortunately the boss that the drive gear fits on can be hard to remove off the shaft. Model 430 rear roller Credit Luke Lawrence, SBR Allen head screw - remove and the clutch assembly slides out. Model 430 rear roller removal Credit Daniel Eves, SBR

252 Ver. 25 It is a tight (interference) fit, it will pull off easily. From personal experience I found a combination of Inox, heat and a gear puller affixed and kept on overnight under “load” was required

When installing the new gear (from Rob Terzo) the boss (center metal section out of the old gear) needs to be kept and glued on so that it locks into the shaft. File a flat section so the plastic can grip on the metal. You may be able to press it in with a vice. When fitting the new drive gear – remember it is plastic and does not have to be “super” tight, otherwise you are likely to break the new one (it has happened).

Drive gear removed showing the boss. Credit Stephen Brown, SBR New drive gear with the boss ground to lock it in position Credit Tim Sharpe, SBR

sections.text:

sections.title: How do I remove the pulleys?
sections.text:
253 Ver. 25 How do I remove the pulleys? WARNING: Before attempting to remove the following pulleys it is strongly recommended that you liberally apply your preferred penetrant and give plenty of soak time. You may even have to apply heat to break the rust “seal”. Use care in the removal as the parts are NOT readily available. You have been warned! Before commencing to remove the pulleys take the time to make a pin spanner tool like the examples shown below to fit on the respective pulleys. The tool should be about 30cm long, to give sufficient leverage. Whilst making the tool, drill a third hole that can be used similarly on the larger, drive pulley (discussed below).

Engine Shaft Pulley (UPDATED) How do I remove the pulley from the PTO shaft? Model 590: The pulley comes in two parts. The outer half screws onto the shaft which holds the inner half. The inner half fits onto the shaft with a woodruff key to stop it from spinning. The outer pulley half will probably be on tight so you may have to hold the shaft with multi grips rather than relying on the engine’s compression (if you still have the engine connected). Use the pin spanner tool in the holes in the pulley. Alternatively use a large punch to spin the pully. The outer part of the pulley is screwed on, on A RIGHT HAND THREAD. Don't use "vice grips" etc they will damage your pulley. Model 430: the PTO shaft pulley(the little one) is screwed on ,on a LEFT HAND THREAD! Beware when removing the pulleys, there has either been from the factory or afterwards a mixing of pulleys between the 430 and 590 models. Once you remove this pulley you can then remove the PTO shaft.

Home-pin spanners Credit Joe Rogers, SBR (left) and Kym Schrapel, SBR (above) Model 430/590 pulley Credit Brendan Nielsen, SBR

sections.title: Cutter Reel Pulley
sections.text:
254 Ver. 25

Cutter Reel Pulley The cutter reel pulley is also threaded on, has two holes that can be used with a pin spanner to undo as with the PTO pulley. You can also use a punch to turn the pulley, just be careful as it’s an alloy and may break. (yes, it has happened!) This pulley is clearly marked with the undo direction – reverse thread i.e. anti- clockwise. Wedge a piece of wood between the reel and bedknife to lock the reel before attempting to loosen the pulley.

Drive pulley/clutch After removing the rear drive gear you will have the drive pulley and clutch plate to remove as shown below.

Cutter reel pulley Credit Kym Schrapel. SBR. Outer side of the drive pulley and clutch plate Credit Rodney Unicomb, SBR Inner side of the drive pulley showing the clutch cable and pinion Credit Rodney Unicomb, SBR

255 Ver. 25 To inspect and replace the clutch cork this must be fully disassembled. Firstly take the clutch/drive cable off, use a small screwdriver to slip it out of the groove - don’t lose the u shaped cable clamp. Next stop is to remove the pinion which again is reverse threaded onto the shaft (clockwise to undo).The two holes in the outer clutch plate are to be used to undo the pinion. I placed the pinion, wrapped in a rag to protect the teeth, in a woodwork vice (i.e. one with wooden jaws which the teeth of the pinion tend to go into the wood). If you have a steel jawed vice, add extra padding and be careful. Use the pin spanner on the two holes or the alternative large punch to turn it. Again the pinion is reverse threaded on the shaft i.e. right to loosen. Once the pinion is off the shaft, as shown below is what you have. On the right side there is a circlip - remove it and the washers and spring type washers come off, then the bearing. Keep the washers in order. Inside the left hand end of the below picture there is a smaller bearing still to get out - you can punch it out from the right side.

Circlip 6005 Bearing 6202 bearing in here Partially disassembled rear drive Credit Kym Schrapel, SBR

sections.text:

sections.title: Drive clutch cork
sections.text:
256 Ver. 25 Below is the order of the parts once the rear drive pulley is fully disassembled.

Drive clutch cork Once the rear drive pulley is disassembled the clutch cork can be checked and replaced if required. Fitting the cork is as discussed previously in Chapter 9. The clutch cork is smaller than a Model 45’s. The example opposite shows a trimmed down Model 45 cork - missing about 10mm on the inside. You can buy a specific cork or source 3 mm cork and cut to size.

Model 430 and 590 Bearing Numbers If you want to replace all the bearings you will need: • 5 x 6202 (reel, rear roller and PTO) • 1 x 6005 (drive pulley) • 1 x 6003 (belt idler/tensioner) (UNCONFIRMED)

Order of parts from the rear drive pulley Credit Kym Schrapel, SBR Diplomat drive clutch cork Credit Kym Schrapel, SBR

sections.title: Reel bearing spacer ring
sections.text:
257 Ver. 25 Reel bearing spacer ring On the drive (belt) side, the cutter bearing there is a spacer ring inside the bearing holder. (This is like the setup on the Model 45 rear roller non drive side bearing holder). As per the below parts diagram, it is fitted to the same side as the height adjuster i.e. drive side.

Bearings off the shafts The bearing housings are made of pressed metal, like the rear roller bearing housings on the Model 45. They are susceptible to bending when attempting to use a gear puller to remove the bearing. A better option than a gear puller is a bearing puller. See Chapter 5 – “Bearing is stuck on the rear roller shaft” for advice. The best advice is to use a combination of plenty of penetrant (e.g. Inox), soak time, heat, even leaving the bearing puller on under pressure overnight, and with a socket tap the bearing “on” to break the “seal”. Finally, persistence and patience is required. If they become bent, they can easily be hammered back into shape.

Cutter setup parts diagram showing part 25, spacer ring. Credit Peter Lyons, SBR Model 430 Reel bearing and holder Credit Mat Tehan, SBR

sections.title: Bearings off the shafts
sections.text:
257 Ver. 25 Reel bearing spacer ring On the drive (belt) side, the cutter bearing there is a spacer ring inside the bearing holder. (This is like the setup on the Model 45 rear roller non drive side bearing holder). As per the below parts diagram, it is fitted to the same side as the height adjuster i.e. drive side.

They are susceptible to bending when attempting to use a gear puller to remove the bearing. A better option than a gear puller is a bearing puller. See Chapter 5 – “Bearing is stuck on the rear roller shaft” for advice. The best advice is to use a combination of plenty of penetrant (e.g. Inox), soak time, heat, even leaving the bearing puller on under pressure overnight, and with a socket tap the bearing “on” to break the “seal”. Finally, persistence and patience is required. If they become bent, they can easily be hammered back into shape.

Cutter setup parts diagram showing part 25, spacer ring. Credit Peter Lyons, SBR Model 430 Reel bearing and holder Credit Mat Tehan, SBR

sections.title: Which way does the reel go
sections.text:
258 Ver. 25

Which way does the reel go At first glance the cylinder reel could easily be put in the wrong way. Have a closer look and you will see the drive side end of the shaft is slightly longer. The different lengths of the shaft ends is consistent with the spacer ring mentioned above, also being on the drive side.

Smoking belts – Model 590 Model 590’s have a direct drive setup, that is pull the clutch lever and both the reel and rear rollers engage. With the clutch hand lever released the drive belt should be loose enough to allow no drive.

Referring to the above picture, when you release the tension on the belt the rectangular plate attached to the top pulley (marked “1”) moves toward the centre of the pulley which then prevents the belt griping in the pulley. It pushes the belt away from the pulley, so no drive takes place. If this part is sticking then the belt will be engaged and will possibly be smoking. Also, adjustment (part marked “2”) needs to be correct otherwise it will not push the plate up to disengage the belt.

Cylinder reel shaft ends Credit Kym Schrapel SBR “1” “2” Model 590 clutch Credit Shane Kostezyn, SBR

sections.title: Smoking belts – Model 590
sections.text:
258 Ver. 25

The different lengths of the shaft ends is consistent with the spacer ring mentioned above, also being on the drive side.

Smoking belts – Model 590 Model 590’s have a direct drive setup, that is pull the clutch lever and both the reel and rear rollers engage. With the clutch hand lever released the drive belt should be loose enough to allow no drive.

Referring to the above picture, when you release the tension on the belt the rectangular plate attached to the top pulley (marked “1”) moves toward the centre of the pulley which then prevents the belt griping in the pulley. It pushes the belt away from the pulley, so no drive takes place. If this part is sticking then the belt will be engaged and will possibly be smoking. Also, adjustment (part marked “2”) needs to be correct otherwise it will not push the plate up to disengage the belt.

Cylinder reel shaft ends Credit Kym Schrapel SBR “1” “2” Model 590 clutch Credit Shane Kostezyn, SBR

sections.title: Fitting NH130 engine to Model 430 or 590
sections.text:
259 Ver. 25 For the part to work it relies on three things: • all the pivot points are lubricated, free moving and not worn excessively. • the spring on the end of the clutch cable is intact, not weak or broken. • the adjustment is correct. SBR member Shane Kostezyn advises having several Model 590’s with an additional return spring fitted which assists in helping the mechanism return. See below picture. Fitting NH130 engine to Model 430 or 590 The Predator NH130 engine doesn't fit from factory with the air filter hitting the ear of the chassis. It does however fit with the air filter rotated 90°. A 3D printed adapter has been printed by SBR member Braden Chapman as shown below.

Retrofitted return spring Retrofitted spring on Model 590 clutch Credit Shane Kostezyn, SBR Left and right. NH130 engine fitted with air filter adapter Credit Braden Chapman, SBR

sections.title: Diplomat clutch adjustment
sections.text:
260 Ver. 25 Diplomat clutch adjustment If after fitting a new clutch cork and cable on Diplomat, the clutch plate and everything is tight you have an adjustment issue. With the clutch cable disconnected the pulley should spin freely. When the cable is connected, and the lever pulled the pulley moves outward to grab the drive plate . If it’s engaged all the time , try adjusting the cable till the pulley can turn freely.

Bottom Blade Types (NEW) Two different types of bottom blades may be encountered on the Diplomat Model 430 and the Model 590 as shown below. • Type 1 is a replacement flat bedknife normally with 7 screws. • Type 2 is an original bedknife on the Model 590. It is a riveted lipped bedknife with curved edges which can be turned over and then reuse the unused side. Unfortunately the rivet holes don’t match the spacing of the 7 holes in Type 1

Type 2 – Model 590, Lipped Bedknife Credit Hamish McKenzie, SBR Type 1 – Diplomat, Flat bedknife Credit Hamish McKenzie, SBR

sections.text:
261 Ver. 25 Model 46 Basic steps in swapping a motor Remove: • belt guard • the belts • 4 engine bolts • Pulley • Snorkel • drive shaft Wheel Bearings On the Model 46 Scarifier each wheel has 2 two bearings. The replacement bearing is a LJ5/8-2RS or RLS5 2RS sealed bearing 5/8 x 1.9/16 x 7/16. Rear Flap (NEW) Height and length of the rubber flap - 100mm x 570mm.

Model 690 How do I remove the drive gear? The Model 690 drive gear/sprocket is held on by a roll pin as shown below. Simply punch it out.

Mod 690 drive gear Credit Tony Greipl, SBR Model 45 Wheel Bearing. Credit Tim Hugo, SBR.

sections.title: How do I remove the shaft coupling?
sections.text:
262 Ver. 25 How do I remove the shaft coupling?

Firstly, remove the grub screw and then apply liberal quantities of Inox or WD40. Remove the cover at the end which will expose two slots. With a screwdriver pry the cover apart. The remaining piece attached to the shaft can be tapped off. (Advice courtesy of Michael Orton).

Remove grub screw Cover to be removed Model 690 coupling separated Credit SBR Model 690 coupling separated Credit SBR Left - Model 690 coupling between output and PTO shafts Credit SBR

sections.title: Model 690 - Masport Parts Compatibility
sections.text:
263 Ver. 25

Model 690 - Masport Parts Compatibility The reel holders are susceptible to breaking as they are made of a hard plastic material. Masport replacement holders are cast alloy and come with a grease nipple. Housing RH is part no 002576, housing LH is 002573. Masport Olympic MK 1, Models 400, 500 and 600 have some compatible parts with the Model 690. Check out the following link for a Masport Olympic parts diagram: http://dealers.fgm.uk.net/PDFFiles/Masport/Masport%20Olympic%20parts%20list%202008.pdf?fbclid=I wAR3gi7w0MWuP8fXFfQbAY1cxIib6iYLkPJFAdkq4NmoS-7MwkcREUPDdcaY.

Model 690 reel bearing holders which are susceptible to breaking can be replaced with a Masport part. Credit Kym Schrapel, SBR

sections.chapter: CHAPTER 11 – TIPS FOR USING A REEL MOWER
sections.title: CHAPTER 11 – TIPS FOR USING A REEL MOWER
sections.text:
264 Ver. 25

CHAPTER 11 – TIPS FOR USING A REEL MOWER

sections.chapter: CHAPTER 11 – TIPS FOR USING A REEL MOWER
sections.title: Tips for using a reel mower.
sections.text: 265 Ver. 25 Tips for using a reel mower. The restoration is complete, or you have purchased your first reel mower and want to use the mower for the first time. The following are a collection of tips for the safe operation of your mower. • DO NOT START THE MOWER ON CONCRETE OR PAVERS o Start the engine carefully, with feet away from the blades. • Start the mower WITHOUT the clutch engaged. o ENGAGE THE CLUTCH WITH THE ENGINE AT IDLE SPEED. • DO NOT mow while barefoot or wearing open sandals or thongs. Wear long trousers and heavy shoes. • DO read the owner’s manual (https://lookaside.fbsbx.com/file/SB%2045%20Owners%20Manual%20Reel%20Mower%20Mod els%2045058%20- %2045148.pdf?token=AWwg4Buzq7Fi56uyk_joX28HKusEYfayKVAV0qG4exZ5iKy_XK19CjZzgRC49 EGuDF08vcjhT3mW28plu2RLCEo74dP- q9lP3s90_XWzh8mYwfldbNz2aiqUAhNpvrlhc0xXdnHgDJrles6nfXuk6Dyz7MExjZj2TNhZIe2DDzcM FQ) • DO know how to stop the engine quickly in any emergency. • Ensure the lawn is clear of sticks, stones, bones, wire and debris. They could be thrown by the blade or cause severe damage to your mower. o Debris is the enemy of your reel and blade. • Stop the engine before clearing blockages, checking or working on the mower. • Before using, always visually inspect to see that the blades, blade bolts and cutter assembly are not worn or damaged. Replace worn or damaged blades and bolts in sets to preserve balance. o DAMAGED BLADES AND WORN BOLTS ARE MAJOR HAZARDS. • DO check all nuts, bolts and screws often, always be sure the mower is in safe operating condition. • Stop the engine whenever you leave the mower, even for a moment. • Stop the engine, disconnect the spark plug lead and inspect the mower o the mower begins to vibrate abnormally or o after striking a foreign object. • Stop the engine before pushing the mower across gravel drives, walks or roads. • If you must lean the mower back - do it when you are changing the oil with a dry sump; or DO NOT lean it past 45 degrees. Mowing tips Cylinder mowers are renowned for delivering a quality of cut far more than rotary lawnmowers. They are designed to cut at lower heights. The combination of the reel and roller produces the striped effect. Cylinder mowers are designed for the frequent cutting of fine lawns. • To achieve stripes: o Begin by mowing the perimeter of your lawn, creating a buffer zone for turning. ▪ Mow anti-clockwise i.e. so the chain cover is not on the “outside” and susceptible to damage due to lawn edging etc.

sections.chapter: CHAPTER 11 – TIPS FOR USING A REEL MOWER
sections.title: Mowing tips
sections.text: 265 Ver. 25 Tips for using a reel mower. The restoration is complete, or you have purchased your first reel mower and want to use the mower for the first time. The following are a collection of tips for the safe operation of your mower. • DO NOT START THE MOWER ON CONCRETE OR PAVERS o Start the engine carefully, with feet away from the blades. • Start the mower WITHOUT the clutch engaged. o ENGAGE THE CLUTCH WITH THE ENGINE AT IDLE SPEED. • DO NOT mow while barefoot or wearing open sandals or thongs. Wear long trousers and heavy shoes. • DO read the owner’s manual (https://lookaside.fbsbx.com/file/SB%2045%20Owners%20Manual%20Reel%20Mower%20Mod els%2045058%20- %2045148.pdf?token=AWwg4Buzq7Fi56uyk_joX28HKusEYfayKVAV0qG4exZ5iKy_XK19CjZzgRC49 EGuDF08vcjhT3mW28plu2RLCEo74dP- q9lP3s90_XWzh8mYwfldbNz2aiqUAhNpvrlhc0xXdnHgDJrles6nfXuk6Dyz7MExjZj2TNhZIe2DDzcM FQ) • DO know how to stop the engine quickly in any emergency. • Ensure the lawn is clear of sticks, stones, bones, wire and debris. They could be thrown by the blade or cause severe damage to your mower. o Debris is the enemy of your reel and blade. • Stop the engine before clearing blockages, checking or working on the mower. • Before using, always visually inspect to see that the blades, blade bolts and cutter assembly are not worn or damaged. Replace worn or damaged blades and bolts in sets to preserve balance. o DAMAGED BLADES AND WORN BOLTS ARE MAJOR HAZARDS. • DO check all nuts, bolts and screws often, always be sure the mower is in safe operating condition. • Stop the engine whenever you leave the mower, even for a moment. • Stop the engine, disconnect the spark plug lead and inspect the mower o the mower begins to vibrate abnormally or o after striking a foreign object. • Stop the engine before pushing the mower across gravel drives, walks or roads. • If you must lean the mower back - do it when you are changing the oil with a dry sump; or DO NOT lean it past 45 degrees. Mowing tips Cylinder mowers are renowned for delivering a quality of cut far more than rotary lawnmowers. They are designed to cut at lower heights. The combination of the reel and roller produces the striped effect. Cylinder mowers are designed for the frequent cutting of fine lawns. • To achieve stripes: o Begin by mowing the perimeter of your lawn, creating a buffer zone for turning. ▪ Mow anti-clockwise i.e. so the chain cover is not on the “outside” and susceptible to damage due to lawn edging etc.

sections.chapter: CHAPTER 11 – TIPS FOR USING A REEL MOWER
sections.title: My mower cuts lower on one side, why?
sections.text: 266 Ver. 25 o Start at one side of your garden then mow up and down towards the other side using the straight lawn edge as a guide, this will help to achieve a tidy stripe. o Finish off by going over the perimeter once more to tidy up any irregularities leftover from turning at the end of each row. o Train your grass – the next time mow the same “stripe” the same way. • Mow across the face of slopes, not up or down the slope. Do not mow excessively steep slopes. • Always keep the blades on your mower sharp • Mow frequently o helps to discourage weeds. o produces a tight sward for a neater finish, while also ensuring that your lawn stays healthy. o if you need to reduce the height of the grass on your lawn it is best to do so gradually over a few cuts. • Never mow more than one third of the grass height at any one time. o If you make a large change in height of cut, you may notice some visible clip marks after the first mowing. This will go away after the grass is maintained at this height over a period. • Do not mow when the grass is wet as it clogs your mower. o The clippings tend to clump, and you end up with an uneven cut. • Different turf varieties have different characteristics. o Therefore, they require different frequency of mowing. o And difference height of cut. Your mowing regime will to a large extent be dictated by the grass type and what you want from it. The type of grass chosen for a lawn is an important decision to make, especially when considering different lawn types behave differently in certain weather conditions and react differently to various levels of traffic and use. My mower cuts lower on one side, why? There are two general possible causes, simply either it’s your grass or your mower. Firstly have a look at the condition of the grass and your height of cut. It may simply be that you are cutting too short through long/thick grass with a buildup of thatch. Thatch is a buildup of dead grass leaves, stolons and rhizomes in your lawn which can leave your lawn looking unsightly after it is mown. Lawn thatching can result from uneven water penetration, insufficient mowing or neglecting to maintain your instant turf. A buildup of thatch sees a “spongy” lawn which the mower tends to sink or dig into. If thatch is the problem it is advisable to dethatch the lawn. Depending on how bad the thatch is ultimately you may have to perform a lawn renovation to remove it. The other possible cause is your mower. Specifically movement in the front roller and frame, worn bushes or alignment between the rear and front rollers. See Chapters 7 (Movement in the front roller frame) and Chapter 9 (See under Adjustment Issues The level of the rear roller).

sections.chapter: CHAPTER 11 – TIPS FOR USING A REEL MOWER
sections.title: Grass types
sections.text:
267 Ver. 25 Grass types The following is a summary of the common, popular grass types42. Couch is common on Tennis Courts, Croquet Lawns, Bowling Greens, some Golf Course Fairways, and some Golf Course Tees and Greens. Most Greens have Bent Grass, which is a high maintenance grass, and would not be on many domestic lawns. Couch will go dormant during the Winter months, especially in cold climates, and goes brown during periods of frost. It can be cut as low as 2mm on Golf Greens, and probably 4-8mm on good home lawns or Golf Tees but regular mowing will be required. Two to three times a week is desirable. It keeps the grass compacted and gives it a carpet-like appearance. However, it will need to be cut back, or scalped just prior to Spring to achieve the best appearance during Spring and Summer. It will grow back quickly and lush. Kykuyu is the main choice on Fairways as it grows well and is generally tougher than couch. Most Golf Courses cut their fairways between 10-12mm with a reel mower. This look can also be achieved by the homeowner with a reel mower, and using the same reel mower height settings, but regular mowing will be required. Two to three times a week desirable. Like couch, Kikuyu can be kept compacted and gives it a thicker carpet-like appearance. Also, like couch, it will need to be cut back, or scalped just prior to Spring to achieve the best appearance during Spring and Summer. Again, like couch, it will grow back quickly and lush. Ryegrass Is used on most football fields because it grows upwards more than other grasses, and when mowed with a Reel Mower, it lays over in the direction that it is mowed, and gives the best striping affect, like you see on T.V. It also grows best in the colder months, hence the choice for football fields. Because it grows from a central root clump, and does not have runners, it can create quite a clumpy lawn and will not grow across the ground to cover bare patches. Re-seeding after a hot Summer is probably a must to maintain a good cover of grass. It is generally mowed at a height between 25mm and 40mm, with either a Reel Mower or a Rotary Mower. Buffalo is a vigorous-growing lush looking grass which looks best when cut much higher than Couch or Kykuyu, and generally is cut with a rotary mower. It can successfully be cut with a reel mower. It is not a grass that can be scalped each year, as it does not have runners that grow beneath the soil, so it does not re-generate easily from a “cut-back”. It is highly unlikely to be seen in a commercial application, because of its low wear tolerance, and poor recovery rate in high use areas. Zoysia grass is starting to gain recognition as a viable and even attractive option for Australian lawns. It is known for being incredibly soft, with no chance of irritating or scratching the skin. It also grows incredibly slowly, making it attractive for those who are not a huge fan of mowing. Slow growth also means that managing potential invasion of surrounding garden beds is made much easier. Zoysia is an incredibly tough variety of grass, ideal for sports fields and schools. However, its slow-growing nature means that if it does become damaged, it will take longer than other grasses to repair itself. Tall Fescue is a popular choice for “instant lawns”. It is an incredibly indiscriminate type of grass, being equally happy in sun or shade, and being able to adapt to a wide range of soil conditions and climates. Tall Fescue has no runners, which means that it cannot invade surrounding garden beds or your neighbor’s

42 https://www.allmowerspares.com.au/blog/handy-tips/how-low-or-high-should-i-cut-the-grass-in-my-lawn.html , and https://www.canstarblue.com.au/home-garden/varieties-of-lawn-in-australia/. Accessed 10 September 2019.

sections.chapter: CHAPTER 11 – TIPS FOR USING A REEL MOWER
sections.title: Guideline for ideal mowing heights
sections.text:
268 Ver. 25 yard. It can cope with moderate levels of traffic, being relatively hardy and durable. However, it is not quite as durable as other grasses such as Zoysia and Kikuyu.

Ultimately you should pick a grass type based on what will work best with your yard. Take the levels of traffic and sunlight into account first, as they are arguably the most important factors to consider when deciding.

Guideline for ideal mowing heights The following guidelines have been sourced from Lawn Solutions Australia43. The right length of grass is critical to its health and weed minimisation. The below is provided as a guide to help you determine the height of lawn. The heights are only a guide, you can, as many do, cut lower than these heights. Do what works best for you and for your lawn. The “Rule of thumb” in lieu of specific heights is: • shorter for summer, longer for winter Grass Type Height Buffalo grass (Sir Walter) 30 to 50 mm Green couch 5 to 30 mm Kikuyu 30 to 50 mm Zoysia matrella (Sir Grange) 5 mm to uncut Queensland blue couch 5 to 30 mm Broadleaf carpet grass 30 to 50 mm Fescue 30 to 50 mm Hybrid Bermuda (TifTuf) 5 to 30 mm

There are plenty of online lawn care sites offering lawn care plans and products. They are a good starter to gain information however ultimately they are generic in the advice they give. Whatever the lawn you have, get to know it, know your local conditions, your local weather, soils and treat accordingly.

43 https://lawnsolutionsaustralia.com.au/lawn-care/lawn-mowing/cut-grass- properly/?fbclid=IwAR1wuwE1p7MH__uSnCz4xB-b56SfmlSyG1hoRdW7ogqSFYmkuZxZOaPqb5Y accessed 12 December 2019.

270 Ver. 25 Troubleshooting matrix The following has been copied from the Scott Bonnar/Rover Model 45 User Manual. PROBLEM POSSIBLE CAUSES CORRECTIVE ACTION Engine loses power

1. Oil level in crankcase is low

2. Cooling fins and air passages under engine blower housing are blocked

3. Engine load is excessive

4. Air cleaner is dirty

5. Dirt or water is in fuel system

6. Carburetor is adjusted incorrectly

7. Spark plug is fitted, fouled or defective in some other way

1. Add oil to crankcase

2. Remove obstruction from passages

3. Select a lower speed to reduce load

4. Clean air cleaner element

5. Have machine services by Authorized Service Dealer

6. Have machine services by Authorized Service Dealer

7. Install new correctly gapped spark plug Engine overheats

1. Cooling fins and air passages under engine blower housing are blocked

2. Carburetor is adjusted incorrectly

3. Oil level in crankcase is low

4. Engine load is excessive

1. Remove obstruction from passages

2. Have machine services by Authorized Service Dealer

3. Add oil to crankcase

4. Select a lower speed to reduce load Mower vibrates abnormally

1. Engine mount bolts are loose

2. Loose cutter blade

3. Cuter assembly is unbalanced

4. Cutter assembly is loose

1. Tighten mounting bolts

2. Replace cutter assembly

3. Replace cutter assembly

4. Tighten securing nut Engine does not start, hard to start, loses power, or fails to keep running

1. Fuel tank is empty

2. Drive is engaged

3. Cutter drive is engaged

4. Spark plug is loose

5. Spark plug lead is loose or disconnected from spark plug

6. Spark plug gap is incorrect

7. Spark plug is fitted, fouled or defective in some other way

8. Wrong spark plug is used

9. Electrical connections are loose 10.Carburetor is adjusted incorrectly

1. Fill fuel tank with petrol

2. Disengage drive clutch

3. Disengage cutter drive

4. Tighten spark plug

5. Install spark plug lead on spark plug

6. Set gap between electrodes at 0.8mm

7. Install new correctly gapped spark plug

8. Install correct spark plug

9. Check electrical system to ensure good contact

10. Have machine services by Authorized Service Dealer

Engine issues The following is provided to try and cover the multitude of engine problems asked on the SBR page. It is not exhaustive but provided as tips/hints as to what may be the issue. If you are having trouble starting the engine, remember there are several basic requirements for an internal combustion engine to run: Fuel + air + timing + compression = START Therefore, it is a process of elimination (In the case of Briggs and Stratton’s, they have fixed timing). In any event I strongly recommend you search online for solutions. Briggs and Stratton for example have a particularly good troubleshooting section which can be found at: https://www.briggsandstratton.com/eu/en_gb/support/faqs/browse/engine-problem-solving-tips.html Forget Something? As silly as it may sound: • take the fuel cap off and check to make sure there's fuel in the tank. • make sure the fuel shut-off valve is open and all operating switches are in the "on" or "run" position • Check the sump for oil o both quantity and quality. Fuel Do you have fresh, clean fuel in the tank?

• If it is empty, fill it up. If it is full, check that the fuel shut-off valve is open and that it is clean. Stale fuel - Fuel deteriorates fast • Adding a fuel stabilizer to fresh fuel is a good practice and cheap insurance to keep it stable longer, but won't bring back your fuel once it's gone off.

sections.title: Air
sections.text:
272 Ver. 25 • Old or degraded fuel deteriorates rubber seals, fuel lines and metal parts forming a "gum" that is difficult to remove. Cleaning the carburetor is advised to remove the “gum”. Dirt/debris in the fuel system Check that the fuel tank and carburetor are clean of any dirt/debris. Deposits inside the carburetor can clog fuel and air passages.

• Often cleaning can be done without needing to pull down the carburetor using a commercially available carburetor cleaner (convenient spray cans) for periodic cleaning of both inside and outside the carburetor. Carburetor Linkages • Linkages attached to the carburetor's throttle and choke plates can bind or stick when dirty. Constant vibration and wear can affect the setting of the carburetor's mixture screws (if equipped). • Check any springs in the linkage system Too Much Fuel Most commonly a result of improper starting, "flooding" results from various causes, including over- priming, closed choke, stuck valve, gummed carburetor, or immediately trying to restart an automatic choke engine. • The easiest way to tell if your engine is flooded with fuel is to remove the spark plug. o If it is wet, it is flooded, and you will need to let the cylinder dry out before trying to restart. Compressed air can help speed up the process. Not enough fuel If the spark plug is not wet and you do not even smell fuel • Check the carburetor is clear and there are no blockages, and the needle moves freely • Check the fuel cap is not sealing causing a vacuum – there is a small breather hole that needs to be clear. Leaking fuel • Check the condition of fuel lines and fuel stop valves • Particularly for OHV Briggs and Stratton engines - dirt in the carburetor can cause the needle to stay slightly open, take the carburetor bowl off, let some fuel flow through the needle and seat and it should clear. Air Arguably the easiest maintenance item to perform is changing the air filter. • All it takes is a small amount of dirt to become shrapnel in the engine to render it useless. Spark If your engine is getting enough fuel and air and still not starting, the issue could be a lack of spark. Even though spark plugs should be replaced yearly, they can fail for several reasons (can fracture internally after

Even though spark plugs should be replaced yearly, they can fail for several reasons (can fracture internally after

sections.title: “Wet” spark plug
sections.text: 273 Ver. 25 being dropped or during heavy vibrations, the electrode may be improperly gapped, or the spark plug wire might be faulty). Disconnected, dirty or fouled spark plugs are common causes for engines that will not start. • You should check that the spark plug gap is set properly. If your spark plugs look good, problems with your ignition system can prevent a spark. • These can range from a faulty spark plug lead, shorted kill switch or flywheel key damage (See How do I check and inspect the flywheel key for replacement). If your engine stops once it gets hot, but will then start again once it has cooled down • this may indicate the coil is breaking down and needs replacing. If after you replace the spark plug the engine still will not start, check the ignition. • A quick check is to remove the spark plug boot ( or remove the spark plug and with the lead connected to the earthed plug) pull the starter cord and see if there is an electrical arc created after pulling the pull cord a few times. The arc will be created in the gap between the boot and the end of the spark plug (or from the electrode in the alternative method). If there is no arc, then the ignition coil may need to be replaced. “Wet” spark plug Problem: “I have to take the spark plug out and clean/wipe it after each start”. Firstly, look at the spark plug and see if it is oil or fuel. If it is fuel: • Check the tune – may be running rich, adjust as required • Check the spark plug – is it producing a good spark, if not replace • Check the condition of the air filter, replace if necessary • check the carburetor o look for blocked air corrector jets; and o high float height. If the plug is oily: • you are looking at piston rings and maybe valve guides o it might be easier to get a new motor. Engine will not start after sitting for a while. Often reported is the problem of starting a mower which has been left sitting for an extended period. One of the causes is old, stale fuel resulting in “fuel gum” and the sticky varnish in carburetors and fuel tanks. One suggestion offered is Carburetor Cleaner, but that at times may not be enough. SBR member Ben Parry advises after a lot of testing he has found the below pictured products will easily dissolve fuel gum and the sticky varnish you get in carburetors and fuel tanks. Spray or blow through with compressed air =

sections.title: Engine will not start after being leant over
sections.text: 274 Ver. 25 resurrected carburetor. All these products are for upper cylinder cleaning, all dissolve old, brown fuel gum where carby cleaner won't do a thing. Engine will not start after being leant over Here is the scenario: “I did a back lap and tilted the mower back to do so as well as adjust the cut. Now when trying to pull the cord there is huge resistance and it basically won’t pull at all.” What has probably happened is the engine oil has displaced throughout the engine and the oil probably has run into the cylinder, past the rings, and fouled the plug. Further, oil may have made its way into the carburetor and breather. Action to remedy the problem: • If you have the patience; let it sit upright for a day for the oil to drain down to the sump. • Remove the plug, pull the cord a few times. (If it is hard to turn over you probably have hydro- locked it). Lookout for oil to spray out. Pull it over some more. Place a rag over the hole. • Check and if necessary drain and flush the carburetor with petrol. Some have a plug in the bottom of the bowl. • Check that the oil has not made its way to the air cleaner – replace if necessary • Check oil level and install a new spark plug and try starting it • Run for a while, will probably be smokey for 10-15 minutes till the oil in the cylinder head burns off. Unless you know what you are doing, and if you must lean the mower back - do it when you are changing the oil with a dry sump; or do not lean it past 45 degrees. Upper engine cleaners Credit Ben Parry, SBR.

sections.title: The engine stops after running over an object
sections.text:
275 Ver. 25

The engine stops after running over an object Not a common issue but it does happen, and the question has been asked “Advice needed - ran over an object caused blades to cease spinning then engine cut out. Now cannot pull start at all engine seems stuck. Brand new Briggs”. The engine may not run or if it does may exhibit symptoms such as experienced by an SBR member of an oiling plug; running very rough and hard to start. Obviously, you should check the condition of the blade and reel and treat accordingly. It is unlikely that a crank or rod in the engine will break before the clutch cork slips. What is likely to happen is for the flywheel woodruff key to break. If a lawn mower hits a rock or curb, the flywheel key can sometimes absorb the damage, reducing repair costs significantly. A quick check involves removing the starter housing and taking the spark plug out, turn the flywheel to see if it will become unstuck. Briggs and Stratton recommend you always check for damage by removing the flywheel to inspect the key and the keyway, the key's slot on the crankshaft. The soft metal key must eliminate play between the flywheel and crankshaft44. The following instructions have been obtained from the Briggs and Stratton website45. How do I remove the flywheel key? • Disconnect the spark plug lead and secure it away from the spark plug. Then, remove the bolts holding the shroud in place and remove the shroud. • If the engine is equipped with a flywheel brake, remove any cover, and disconnect the outer end of the brake spring • If your engine is equipped with a flywheel clutch, remove it while holding the flywheel with a flywheel holder or a flywheel strap wrench. If it is attached with a nut, remove the retaining nut with the appropriate socket. • With the flywheel nut threaded onto the crankshaft, install a flywheel puller so its bolts engage the holes adjacent to the flywheel's hub. If the holes are not threaded, use a self-tapping flywheel puller, or tap the holes using a 1/4 X 20 tap. • Rotate the flywheel puller nuts evenly until the flywheel pops free. Then, remove the flywheel and key. CAUTION! Never strike the flywheel. Even a slightly damaged flywheel presents a safety hazard and must be replaced. How do I check and inspect the flywheel key for replacement? • Inspecting Flywheel and Keyway • Check for cracks, broken fins and crankshaft damage. Replace if you find such damage. The tapered sections must be clean and smooth, with no play between the two. • Inspect the keyway and flywheel for damage. Slight burrs may be removed with a file. Then, make certain there is no play or wobbling when the flywheel is placed on the crankshaft.

44 https://www.briggsandstratton.com/na/en_us/support/faqs/browse/inspecting-flywheel-key.html accessed 29/8/2018 45 Ibid

sections.title: How do I remove the flywheel key?
sections.text:
275 Ver. 25

The engine stops after running over an object Not a common issue but it does happen, and the question has been asked “Advice needed - ran over an object caused blades to cease spinning then engine cut out. Now cannot pull start at all engine seems stuck. Brand new Briggs”. The engine may not run or if it does may exhibit symptoms such as experienced by an SBR member of an oiling plug; running very rough and hard to start. Obviously, you should check the condition of the blade and reel and treat accordingly. It is unlikely that a crank or rod in the engine will break before the clutch cork slips. What is likely to happen is for the flywheel woodruff key to break. If a lawn mower hits a rock or curb, the flywheel key can sometimes absorb the damage, reducing repair costs significantly. A quick check involves removing the starter housing and taking the spark plug out, turn the flywheel to see if it will become unstuck. Briggs and Stratton recommend you always check for damage by removing the flywheel to inspect the key and the keyway, the key's slot on the crankshaft. The soft metal key must eliminate play between the flywheel and crankshaft44. The following instructions have been obtained from the Briggs and Stratton website45. How do I remove the flywheel key? • Disconnect the spark plug lead and secure it away from the spark plug. Then, remove the bolts holding the shroud in place and remove the shroud. • If the engine is equipped with a flywheel brake, remove any cover, and disconnect the outer end of the brake spring • If your engine is equipped with a flywheel clutch, remove it while holding the flywheel with a flywheel holder or a flywheel strap wrench. If it is attached with a nut, remove the retaining nut with the appropriate socket. • With the flywheel nut threaded onto the crankshaft, install a flywheel puller so its bolts engage the holes adjacent to the flywheel's hub. If the holes are not threaded, use a self-tapping flywheel puller, or tap the holes using a 1/4 X 20 tap. • Rotate the flywheel puller nuts evenly until the flywheel pops free. Then, remove the flywheel and key. CAUTION! Never strike the flywheel. Even a slightly damaged flywheel presents a safety hazard and must be replaced. How do I check and inspect the flywheel key for replacement? • Inspecting Flywheel and Keyway • Check for cracks, broken fins and crankshaft damage. Replace if you find such damage. The tapered sections must be clean and smooth, with no play between the two. • Inspect the keyway and flywheel for damage. Slight burrs may be removed with a file. Then, make certain there is no play or wobbling when the flywheel is placed on the crankshaft.

44 https://www.briggsandstratton.com/na/en_us/support/faqs/browse/inspecting-flywheel-key.html accessed 29/8/2018 45 Ibid

sections.title: High pitched “squeal” from the engine.
sections.text:
276 Ver. 25 • Inspect the flywheel key. If there are any signs of shearing or if you have doubts about its condition, replace it. It is simple and inexpensive. • How do I install my new flywheel or key? • First, obtain a new flywheel key designed for your make and engine model • Place the flywheel on the crankshaft and look through the flywheel hub to align the keyways on the flywheel and crankshaft. With the flywheel in place, place the key in the keyway; it should fit securely. Debris can also prevent the key from seating in the keyway. • Once the key and flywheel are securely in place, reattach the flywheel nut or clutch. Consult your authorized dealers for the torque specifications for your make and model. High pitched “squeal” from the engine. Or starter clutch problems The following issues have been posted by members: “Mowing … when a high pitch squeal came from it, so I shut it down could not find any problem so started it up and the squeal had gone …” “Pulled the pull start and it just keeps on spinning”. The probable cause is a dry starter clutch. On older Briggs and Stratton motors the starter clutch may become dry from lack of oil. They have a small oil hole in end of the shaft which allows oil to flow to a felt washer in the shaft to keep the starter cup and shaft lubricated. Check YouTube for videos on how to fix or refer to the following fixes.

Brett “Changa” Chain offers the following fix: • Remove the centre clutch spindle. Using some very fine wet and dry clean the crankshaft so it’s spotless(like polished). • If you want to re-use the old clutch you need to clean the inside of it as well. Remove the felt block up the inside tip of the spindle. Using wet and dry on something cylindrical and smaller than the crankshaft, clean the inside spotless. • Re fit the felt block after dipping it in engine oil(don’t overdo it) Before re fitting spindle onto the crank, smear just a couple drops new engine oil on the shaft. That’s the only lubrication a starter clutch has/needs. • Balls should be spotlessly clean, dry and free of any grease/lube etc.

Starter clutch. Credit Jimmy Condon, SBR Spray lubricant in this hole Starter clutch Credit Darragh Foley, SBR

sections.title: Engine leaks oil
sections.text:
277 Ver. 25 Troy Barnet and David Scott have offered the following fix: • Remove the 2 screws. • Hit the biggest lugs with a pin punch (it is a right-hand thread). • Split the seal cover where the screws were. Take care that the ball bearings do not go everywhere. Give it a good clean, there should be a felt oil holder up the center of the clutch • Clean the engine crank shaft where the clutch fits on (using emery tape or fine wet n dry) • Put a drop of oil on the felt pad in the top part that slides over the crack shaft. o Do not lubricate anything else other than the crankshaft • Reassemble In addition to the above the following tip has been provided after reassembly: • Sit it upside down so you are looking at it from the threaded side and put about 10-20 ml of SAE30 or equivalent oil down the centre till it is almost at the thread. • Then place your thumb over the little hole on the outside end tight and push it down over the end of the crankshaft o this will cause a hydraulic effect and you will see the oil seeping through the porous metal • Tighten it back on the crank and you will be good to go. No more screaming and sticking starter clutch. The oil now impregnated in the steal keeps it lubricated. Engine leaks oil “After every mow, my Kirby turns into a weeping …. statue but I cannot see where it is leaking. Assume it is a gasket somewhere? Anyone had the same issue” Whilst the question specifically mentions the Kirby engine, the same can and does occur with the Briggs and Stratton motors. The Villiers on the Model 40is also prone to oil leaks, particularly through the crankshaft /crankcase oil seal. It is advisable to degrease the motor to help identify the source of the leak. Leaks are likely to occur from the following areas which should be checked: • Oil seals e.g. crank case/shaft seal • Gaskets e.g. crankcase, head and breather • Breather • Oil filler • Sump plug The oil leak depicted in the picture above (appears to be a common issue), more than likely originates from the crank case breather.

Leaking engine. Credit Michael McGerr, SBR

sections.title: Leaking petrol cap
sections.text:
278 Ver. 25 Depending on the make and model it may be the breather: ▪ Gasket ▪ Pipe; or ▪ the actual breather/valve itself. Behind the breather cover is a part as depicted below which has a valve set up which may become blocked open or weak and allows oil through. It is a positive crankcase ventilation valve. They have a disc valve which is supposed to be able to move back and forth as the pressure in the crankcase pulses. There are a few You Tube videos showing replacement of the breather on Briggs and Stratton motors which you may care to search for and view.

Whatever the problem, check and repair/replace, as necessary.

Leaking petrol cap Firstly, do not over fill the tank, they have a hole/s in the top to “breath” thereby preventing a vacuum lock occurring when fuel is used. If your engine leaks petrol from the fuel tank cap there are two options: • Cut out a piece of gasket material to fit the outside diameter of the neck of the tank and pierce a small hole at the side of the gasket with a tapered pin. The hole should look like a “volcano”. Place one each side and put the gasket in the cap with the top of the “volcano” hole facing the fuel. If this fails to fix it, • Buy a new cap (particularly if its rusty)

Cover of breather removed on Kirby motor showing internal valve/breather and gauze filter. Credit Kym Schrapel, SBR Breather on Kirby motor. Credit Kym Schrapel, SBR Breather pipe Breather valve Gauze or fine mesh Fuel Cap Credit Micko Heyward, SBR Vent hole

sections.title: Engine tuning
sections.text:
279 Ver. 25

Engine tuning There are a variety of engine makes/models (Villiers, Briggs, Kirby, BSA, Honda and even SB’s own engine) with different throttle linkages and carburetors on Scott Bonnar mowers. For this reason, I have not included a tune up guide in the Bonnar Bible. Which one would I start with?

Tune-up “how to” guides and videos can readily be found online with a simple Google search; or go straight to the Briggs and Stratton home page.

sections.title: CHAPTER 13 – TROUBLESHOOTING –WORKING MOWER
sections.text:
280 Ver. 25

CHAPTER 13 – TROUBLESHOOTING –WORKING MOWER

sections.title: Troubleshooting matrix
sections.text:
281 Ver. 25 Troubleshooting matrix The following has been copied from the Scott Bonnar/Rover Model 45 User Manual. PROBLEM POSSIBLE CAUSES CORRECTIVE ACTION Mower vibrates abnormally

1. Engine mount bolts are loose

2. Loose cutter blade

3. Cuter assembly is unbalanced

4. Cutter assembly is loose

1. Tighten mounting bolts

2. Replace cutter assembly

3. Replace cutter assembly

4. Tighten securing nut Cutter does not rotate

1. Cutter drive chain is loose

2. Cutter drive chain is broken

1. Tension drive cutter chain

2. Install drive cutter chain Mower does not drive

1. Drive chain is loose

2. Drive chain is broken

3. Unable to engage drive clutch

1. Tension drive chain

2. Install drive chain

3. Have machine services by Authorized Service Dealer

Ragged or uneven cut If your mower is giving you a ragged or uneven cut it does not necessarily mean the reel needs sharpening. In most instances it means the cutting cylinder needs adjusting. With use the cutting cylinder and the bottom blade gradually wear away from each other, thereby reducing the cutting efficiency. The cutting cylinder blades maintain a better edge when they are very lightly touching the bottom blade. They become blunt and dull when they are no longer making contact, caused by the grass being “dragged” through and not being cleanly cut. Please refer to Chapter 9 for information on how to adjust the Reel to the Bed knife. If your problem is “my reel is cutting sweet on both sides but not in the middle” then you have a slightly different problem. It is probably not running square i.e. the blade is not true/straight or even the blade is not sitting flat on the sole plate . Do not try and hammer the blade flat on the soleplate. A grind of BOTH the reel and blade is recommended.

sections.title: How do I stop the reel from constantly engaging?
sections.text:
282 Ver. 25 How do I stop the reel from constantly engaging? Question: “What would most likely be the cause of my reel spinning with the clutch either engaged/disengaged? Answer: Tighten the bolt (shown below) under the clutch fork so the clutch cone is pushed further into the clutch, thereby allowing the cork to be free. Also, when setting up the clutch, a good rule of thumb is to initially set the clutch fork in the vertical position when the clutch is engaged. Whilst the above suggestion is the usual solution, if it does not solve the problem you should check the clutch half engine side, clutch bearing, cone wear and PTO shaft wear to name the main ones. Also check if there is wear in the cone keyway. With the engine off, check by moving the PTO shaft and see how much play is in the cone. Engine cuts out when I engage the reel Check the following: • Check the reel is not too tight on the blade – if it is, adjust accordingly. • Chains may be too tight, check and adjust o Remember if you wind the reel down to the knife, you tighten the chains. Mower drives without engaging the clutch The most likely cause is a tight clutch operating bolt which goes through the large intermediate sprocket Being tight, it is causing the drive clutch to be engaged full time. The clutch operating bolt needs to be loose to allow the clutch to operate. If it is done up tight the clutch cable will not be able to be move the operating lever the 2 – 3 mm needed. Refer to “How tight should the clutch operating bolt be?” for the parts diagram.

Clutch fork - adjustment nut/bolt. Credit Trent Clues, SBR When engaging the primary clutch – do it with the motor at low revs (you are not trying to do a burnout!) Clutch operating Bolt Clutch operating bolt Credit Kym Schrapel, SBR

sections.title: Melted Thrust Pad
sections.text:
283 Ver. 25 Melted Thrust Pad What causes a plastic thrust pad to melt? Or for that matter, what causes a brass thrust pad or the rear face of the cone to wear? So often people say because it’s not lubricated. Is that just because that’s what they have read or is there a reason for the statement?

I disagree with the lubrication/oil excuse. Where is the thrust pad oil hole? On the top above the shaft - to lubricate the thrust pad on the spinning shaft and ensure smooth horizontal movement when the clutch lever is engaged.

Does that oil point apply oil to the face of the thrust pad and the cone? No, it doesn’t.

Where does the thrust pad wear or melt - on the face against the cone because of friction from the spinning cone. Is this oiled - no.

So what is the cause? Simple, it’s setup. Get the spacing between the cone and thrust pad right and you’ll remove direct contact between the parts, no friction equals no melting or wear. When you are mowing, the thrust pad should have clearance, hence very little friction. If the engine is running and you use the clutch to stop the reel, then the thrust pad has a spinning clutch being pressed against it by the clutch spring.

The melting of the thrust pad is caused by • Poor setup. In other words, it is a maintenance issue, or lack of. A bucketful of oil won’t save the thrust pad if the setup is wrong. The oil helps with lubrication between the PTO shaft and thrust pad, but the heat is caused by the fork holding the pad tightly against the clutch cone while its engaged. Setup is therefore the secret. There should be a 1mm gap between thrust pad and cone on the PTO shaft, when not engaged.

To replace simply • remove the 4 bolts on the motor. • Slide the motor and clutch off. • Take the keyway out. • Slide old thrust off and slide new one on. Remember: the oil hole on the thrust pad faces upwards. A melted thrust pad. Credit, Mitch Kambanos Carly Laver SBR Oil hole

284 Ver. 25 • Key back in motor back on. • Then adjust the clutch to suit. If your thrust pad and PTO shaft get hot to touch after mowing for only a short time, (even after oiling) then this is a prelude to a melted thrust pad. Check your clutch setup. Whether you choose to replace the thrust pad with a plastic or brass one that is a personal choice. On the Model 33 and early 14” Model 45’s the thrust pads were brass. It is strongly recommended that when the thrust pad is replaced that a fibre washer is also fitted. The brass ones are the same as the plastic ones. Yes, brass looks nice and will last in the long run, but if a drop of oil and a slight gap is set, they are the same. If the brass ones are set too close and are missing the fibre washer, they too will destroy themselves (see above picture) and the tip of the cone.

Rookie mistake - upside down Thrust pad. The oil hole must be on top. Credit (withheld) SBR.

Even brass thrust pads can wear! Credit Kym Schrapel, SBR

sections.title: Rear roller slipping
sections.text:
285 Ver. 25 Rear roller slipping Question: “My rear roller keeps slipping and gripping as I am mowing? The right side will spin then the left side will eventually grip?” When one drum of the rear roller engages, and the other drum does not (or appears to slip), this indicates it is not a drive clutch related but a drive problem inside the roller. Specifically, the pawls are not moving freely and therefore are not properly engaging with the ratchet box. It is likely they are sticking due to debris/grass cuttings fouling their movement or they have been greased and are sticking. Remove the rear roller, separate the drums and check the ratchet box and pawls. Clean and re- install. DO NOT GREASE THE PAWLS OR PAWL HOLDER. The pawls must move freely. Grease will cause them to stick and malfunction the drives. Grass deflector keeps slipping The deflector tab feeds into the slot in the bracket (see opposite image) with the deflector screw loose. As you tighten, it lifts the bracket, clamping the deflector bracket against the front rail of the mower. You need to have the wingnut loosened right off, so the slot aligns with cutout in the front rail. The deflector screws hold the small L Shaped bracket which clamps onto the tab on the deflector underneath the deck/rail. If the deflector support tang is bent out of shape, the L shaped bracket will not be able to clamp onto it. The support tang must be straight and flat. The tang must go through the hole in the bracket, if it goes underneath then it will not be held by the deflector screws Another possible solution is fit and shape a bit of a plate to the arms on the deflector plate to make it a bit thicker.

Pawl Drum shaft Ratchet Box Ratchet box with pawls Credit Kym Schrapel SBR Deflector L-shaped bracket. Credit Steve Brown, SBR

sections.title: My clutch does not run true, it wobbles.
sections.text:
286 Ver. 25 My clutch does not run true, it wobbles. Because there are several possible causes it is recommended that you apply a problem solving methodology and investigate the following possibilities: • Firstly, check that the clutch is 5 mm from the engine. • To see if the clutch is true or warped make a mark on the cone and rotate the engine half slowly and check that it rotates truly. Then check: • remove the clutch, take the key out of the clutch body and slide the clutch body back on the engine shaft. If that clutch body is “rocking” it is worn and probably will need replacing. • the cone for wear in the keyway • check the engine shaft and keyway condition • condition of the clutch thrust bearing. The clutch bearing should be fitted such that it is not flush but below the rim. The rounded edge sits on the inside of the cup, and they are supposed to go down past flush by about 4mm. If the bearing is not below the lip it will make the clutch wobble. • Check for wear or cracks in the clutch half. • Also check the PTO shaft does not have play due to a worn Woodruff key.

Also see “How do I remove the clutch off the shaft?” in Chapter 7. Tight height adjuster “The height adjustor is tight; it just doesn't turn much either way. I've lubed the spring and inside the plastic cap.” This is a “newbie” problem. If you have lubricated the height adjuster the first thing to check is the pivot nuts - have you backed them off?” That is the L-shaped adjuster rod that connects it to the front roller (inside of frame). If you have then check to see if the adjuster rod is bent, then yes, it will be difficult to adjust and remove. Please see “How do I take the height adjuster apart” in Chapter 7 and “How do I adjust the cutting height” in the Chapter 12, Maintenance. Clutch bearing in clutch half. Credit Tyrone Nebel, SBR Bearing to sit below the lip/edge

Check gap and bearing Clutch half’s fitted Credit Gav Smith, SBR

sections.title: Squeal in the drive clutch side – worn PTO bearing
sections.text: 287 Ver. 25 Squeal in the drive clutch side – worn PTO bearing “I have a squeal in the drive clutch side. Corks are new, adjusted, still when the clutch lever is engaged, the squeal starts”. The probable cause is a worn PTO or engine shaft bearing. The shaft may still spin, but inside the inner race of the bearing. Remove the PTO shaft and bearing, check both and replace, as necessary. Excessive rear roller gap Question: Why does the real roller moves apart in the middle? Answer: There should be a gap roughly the thickness of a 50 cent piece. If it opens more than that then you have loose locking nuts on the drum shaft collar either side of your rear rollers. The fix is relatively simple, just remove the chains and the drive sprocket to get to it. If the gap does not appear to be consistent and the rear rollers feel “lumpy”, you may have a slightly bent rear roller shaft. Remove, check and replace if necessary.

sections.title: Excessive rear roller gap
sections.text: 287 Ver. 25 Squeal in the drive clutch side – worn PTO bearing “I have a squeal in the drive clutch side. sections.title: CHAPTER 14 – MAINTENANCE
sections.text:
288 Ver. 25

CHAPTER 14 – MAINTENANCE

sections.title: How do I maintain my mower?
sections.text:
289 Ver. 25 How do I maintain my mower? Lubrication The following is provided as per the Scott Bonnar/Rover Model 45 User Manual (complete version is available from the Files section). To gain the optimum performance from your mower, the following areas need to be oiled every five hours of operation. The same grade oil as used in the engine is suitable. • Clutch Thrust Pad - apply three drops of oil in the thrust pad oil hole. • Clutch inner cone - apply three drops of oil in the clutch inner cone oil hole. Note: it may be necessary to engage the clutch and gently pull on the engine starter cord to align the inner cone oil hole with the clutch cover hole. • Front roller - apply six drops of oil to each side of the shaft. • Height adjustment knob - remove the plastic cover plug to gain access and apply six drops of oil. • Drive chains - every 10 hours of operation remove the chain case side cover and apply a ‘chain oil’ to the drive chains. Replace side cover. For the owners of late model Rover 45’s, that is those with the plastic cover over the primary clutch. The cover was introduced for safety reasons. However they also tend to hide the lubrication points which people tend to forget about i.e. the thrust pad. If you are likely to forget to lubricate your mower; either remove the cover and store it somewhere or take it off periodically and lubricate as above and check the thrust pad and washer; fork adjustment and locating screws on the clutch. Engine For detailed cleaning and maintenance instructions, refer to the engine manufacturers operating manual or go to their website. Which spark plug? Briggs & Stratton • Champion CJ8 is pretty much the standard small engine spark plug (Gap .020”) As per the B&S website you’ll see Champion CJ8, RCJ8, RJ19HX, J19LM, and RJ19LM • The equivalent NGK is BM6A or even BPM7A Briggs & Stratton recommend that you change your spark plug every 100 hours or every season, whichever comes first. Kirby • Champion CJ8, NGK is BM6A

Rover 45 with cover over clutch that should periodically be removed. Credit Ollie Morris, SBR
