sections.title: Engine Oil
sections.text:
290 Ver. 25 Villiers 75 cc 4 stroke (as on Model 40) • Originally was a Champion 7Comm - a D16 now Engine Oil Single grade SAE30 was the sole recommended oil for Briggs and Stratton engines. In 2020 they updated their recommended46 oil to include synthetic oil e.g. 10W30 for all temperature ranges. My personal preference is to still use SA30 for the older Briggs and Stratton’s, they were designed for that grade oil. Honda recommend 4-stroke automotive detergent oil. SAE 10W-30 is recommended for general use Foam Air Filter Briggs & Stratton advise the “foam only” air filters works by trapping dirt and debris using motor oil spread throughout the oil foam holding medium. A dry or non-oiled oil foam filter will trap only the largest particles. If the air filter element is foam only, it MUST be oiled and serviced regularly. Your foam filter requires replacement every 3 months or after 25 hours of use, whichever occurs first, more often under dusty conditions. Dab the filter with clean motor oil (SAE30). Roll it up and work the oil throughout the filter. Too much oil can harm the exhaust, causing it to emit black smoke. You want enough oil to saturate the filter, but it should not be dripping off. If you have too much oil wrap a paper towel around the filter and squeeze out excess oil. Tip: get a zip lock bag put some oil in it with the sponge filter and squeeze away. Cleaning a foam element can be done by cleaning in either petrol or warm soapy water. Squeegee the filter with a paper towel. Remove as much water or petrol as possible and then re-oil. Paper element air filter Tap the paper filter on a hard surface to remove any buildup on it. Do not use an air compressor to clean the paper filter as you may damage the filter. If the paper filter is torn or so soiled it cannot be cleaned, replace it. General • Keep the mower free of grass and debris build up. o Some people hose off then "blow dry" with a compressor. Some just blow cuttings etc. off. Personally, I do not like water near my mower, I wipe it down after every mow and lubricate and periodically get to it with the air compressor. That way its ready for the next use. Some spray the reel with e.g. vegetable oil to keep it clean, CRC or similar works as well. • Clean any spilt oil or fuel from around the engine. • Regularly check that all nuts and bolts are secure. • After use and cleaning, lubricate the blade edges with oil to prevent rust build up on the cutting edges. • Check clutch cone and thrust pad gaps and adjust accordingly.

46 https://www.briggsandstratton.com/na/en_us/support/faqs/browse/mower-oil-type-and- capacity.html?fbclid=IwAR3WfAL-KPVr26ZTGlRWuG40S5X43FWlgVJpeotAK9vx7-ChtcjxAE7i5D4. Accessed 25 June 2020

sections.title: Basic engine service:
sections.text:
291 Ver. 25 Basic engine service: • Change spark plug • change air cleaner element and if foam one then clean and oil • change the oil (run for 5 minutes, then drain and replace) Do the 3.5hp Briggs have a dipstick to check oil levels? If not, how do you check? In short there is no dip stick on older models but new models, yes there is. Simply fill it to just below the top of the outlet. They take 600 ml of oil, which coincidently is the size of the bottle by Briggs & Stratton. How do I adjust the cutting height? The front roller determines height of cut. The adjuster lifts the roller higher or lower and thus gives different heights. The knob with the red grommet controls the front roller, it is as easy as that. Well, almost, at times the red grommet will need to be removed to lubricate the shaft because the knob will be tight to turn. Periodic lubrication of this shaft is advisable.

Loosen the nuts on the roller pivot to allow the height adjuster to operate. After adjusting the height, re-tighten the pivot roller nuts. If you fail to tighten the roller pivot nuts, thinking they need to be loose for "on the go" height adjustment you are likely to get ugly uneven tram track cuts especially if the bushes are worn. DO NOT leave them loose as the front roller may move between 5 – 10 mm and cause uneven cuts.

Reel Issues When does my reel need replacing? The question has been variously asked “when do you know you may need a new reel or a sharpen? Answer: • When the cutting edge gets close to the welds, and you cannot get any more grinds out of it; it needs replacing • If you no longer have any adjustment left when adjusting the reel to the bedknife, it probably needs replacing. Reel blade near the welds. Credit Kym Schrapel, SBR. Pivot nuts to be loosened. Credit David Rake, SBR Pivot roller nut

Reel Issues When does my reel need replacing? The question has been variously asked “when do you know you may need a new reel or a sharpen? Answer: • When the cutting edge gets close to the welds, and you cannot get any more grinds out of it; it needs replacing • If you no longer have any adjustment left when adjusting the reel to the bedknife, it probably needs replacing. Reel blade near the welds. Credit Kym Schrapel, SBR. Pivot nuts to be loosened. Credit David Rake, SBR Pivot roller nut

sections.title: When does my reel need replacing?
sections.text:
291 Ver. 25 Basic engine service: • Change spark plug • change air cleaner element and if foam one then clean and oil • change the oil (run for 5 minutes, then drain and replace) Do the 3.5hp Briggs have a dipstick to check oil levels? If not, how do you check? In short there is no dip stick on older models but new models, yes there is. Simply fill it to just below the top of the outlet. They take 600 ml of oil, which coincidently is the size of the bottle by Briggs & Stratton. How do I adjust the cutting height? The front roller determines height of cut. The adjuster lifts the roller higher or lower and thus gives different heights. The knob with the red grommet controls the front roller, it is as easy as that. Well, almost, at times the red grommet will need to be removed to lubricate the shaft because the knob will be tight to turn. Periodic lubrication of this shaft is advisable.

Loosen the nuts on the roller pivot to allow the height adjuster to operate. After adjusting the height, re-tighten the pivot roller nuts. If you fail to tighten the roller pivot nuts, thinking they need to be loose for "on the go" height adjustment you are likely to get ugly uneven tram track cuts especially if the bushes are worn. DO NOT leave them loose as the front roller may move between 5 – 10 mm and cause uneven cuts.

Reel Issues When does my reel need replacing? The question has been variously asked “when do you know you may need a new reel or a sharpen? Answer: • When the cutting edge gets close to the welds, and you cannot get any more grinds out of it; it needs replacing • If you no longer have any adjustment left when adjusting the reel to the bedknife, it probably needs replacing. Reel blade near the welds. Credit Kym Schrapel, SBR. Pivot nuts to be loosened. Credit David Rake, SBR Pivot roller nut

sections.title: How can you tell if sharpening the reel is necessary?
sections.text: 292 Ver. 25 • Measure the overall diameter and it should be around 143 to 144mm when new. The individual blades are about 25mm wide when new. o About 130 - 131 mm is the minimum cylinder diameter before you start to run out of thread on the height adjustment. • Have a look at the grass blade after a cut. If it is a clean cut like you would get with scissors, then it is nice and sharp. If it is a rough edge like it has been torn, then it may need a minor reel to blade adjustment before a sharpen. • If it cannot cut paper even after adjusting, then you probably need a sharpen. o (I do not intend to engage in any discussion about the benefits (or lack thereof) of backlapping (see below). This is a contentious issue even within industry insiders). When it comes to the condition of the reel it is best to seek the advice of a professional blade/reel sharpener. When having the reel sharpened, it is wise to also have the blade sharpened at the same time. Remember: “Good stuff isn’t cheap, cheap stuff isn’t good”. Not all grinds are equal so find a reputable service provider. How can you tell if sharpening the reel is necessary? A reel gets ground or sharpened for two basic reasons; to put a cutting edge onto the individual blades of the reel and to return the whole reel into a cylindrical shape so that all blades make even contact with the bottom blade. The following advice has been sourced from the Toro, “Reel Mower Basics”. • The grass is not cut cleanly. • The color of the grass o it may have a dull appearance or o have brown tips. • Streaks. • Stragglers. • The cutting unit is noisy. One sure way to determine the answer to this is by checking the cutting edges of the reel and bedknife, to see if they are dull, nicked, bent, or being run without light contact against one another. Reel blades, and the edge of the bedknife, should be checked for damage visually and by carefully feeling the edges. Of course, this should only be done when it is certain that the reel cannot be started, and never slide your fingers down the edges lengthwise. Rounded edges on the reel blades and bedknife will cause the grass blades to be pinched and torn rather than sheared off cleanly. When light contact is not maintained, dull edges will soon result. Just a reminder, when you get a knife and reel grind or adjust the reel height, check the chains again. Worn cutter bearing housing Credit Keith Ratcliffe, SBR

sections.title: Reel moves from side to side
sections.text:
293 Ver. 25 Some people have found when they lower their reels with properly adjusted chains, then things get tight and visa- versa, new knife and a reel change, chains are loose. Reel moves from side to side If the reel has horizontal or sideways movement there are several things to check:

1. Condition of the Reel bearings

2. Cutter Bearing housings, for wear or the top/bottom halves are not on the right sides

3. Check the bearing shields are on the right way (if wrong way around they may foul on the bearing housing). Damage to the reel If your mower suffers a major hit to the reel or worse a blade breaks (see above pictures), you are not up for a new reel. Damaged, bent, or broken blades can be straightened or even replaced. It is cheaper to repair than replace a reel. In the below pictured example of a bent reel blade, firstly remove the reel from the mower. Do not hammer it whilst it is in the mower as you will be hammering against alloy bearing carriers which could break. Three options are suggested: • Place the blade in a vice and tighten the vice to straighten it • Use a hammer and dolly (or two hammers) to hammer the bend out. • The final and the best option is to take the reel to a reputable reel sharpener, they will straighten it as part of the sharpening process.

Replacing the reel - How many blades do I need? Reels come with different number of blades from six, eight, ten and even up to twenty blades on commercial or professional models. Originally reels came standard with six blades which are still available secondhand and new. If you are contemplating replacing a worn or damaged reel with a new reel, the number of blades will be the primary consideration after price. You should also consider the type of grass to be cut and the quality of cut you are after.

Damaged reel. Credit Chad Powell, SBR Broken reel blade. Credit Jason Brown, SBR.

sections.title: Backlapping
sections.text: 294 Ver. 25 Today the eight blade reel is a popular replacement option because they offer greater cuts per metre and are competitively priced compared to six blade reels. The eight blade reel seems to be a happy medium for couch, buffalo, zoysia, rye, kikuyu etc. If you have any doubt as to the suitability of the intended reel; then ask the supplier. Backlapping Backlapping is a term often bandied around as part of a maintenance regime for your mower. There are some who regard backlapping as not being a necessity within either domestic or commercial operations and will simply recommend getting a good grind and others will recommend facing the bedknife edge. So, what is Backlapping? The rationale behind backlapping starts at mowing; grass and twigs will bent roll the leading edge of your cylinder and bedknife metal. Simply put it is polishing the cutting edge to keep it sharp. It is the backward rotation of the cylinder whilst applying a fine granular paste (80 grit compound appears to be the favored paste although some go to 120 grit). Doing so helps remove/reduce the rolled effect on the cylinder and bedknife; re- sharpens and levels the blades on the reel; and puts a new edge on the bedknife. It is supposedly not as effective if the bedknife is too worn. Proponents of backlapping advise it should always be done when a new bedknife is fitted or the cylinder has a machine sharpening. Personally, I fail to see the need to backlap on a freshly ground reel as the edge will be sharp. In deciding whether to backlap consider this, think of the bedknife as a consumable that cost about $50. When you backlap most of the time you are taking material off the $450 reel and lastly, contrary to popular belief, not backlapping doesn’t mean more grinding (if anything not backlapping extends the time between grinds) it just keeps a better cut on your mower. When it comes to backlapping; ultimately it is your choice. I recommend that you search the SBR page or YouTube for more information so that you are more fully informed. If you do choose to backlap: • your mower must be in good condition, with blades in a relatively good condition and cutting reasonably well. • you should have knowledge of how to set the mower • you will need: o lapping/grinding paste compound o long handled 50 mm paint brush for application of paste o 1 x drive coupling to be used with a cordless drill and a 19mm or 3/4" socket. • use a paste which is water based, not oil or silicon based which will damage turf and not wash off easily. • remember to clean the reel of the paste. A good hosing, drying (compressed air) and light oiling of the reel is recommended. Backlapping cannot be used to sharpen a dull reel or bedknife. Backlapping is an edge maintenance practice.

sections.title: Bedknife issues
sections.text:
295 Ver. 25 Bedknife issues Maintaining the bedknife edge Grass is cut scissors-fashion by the reel blades sweeping the grass back against the bedknife. The top of the bedknife needs to be ground flat and kept flat, and its front edge needs to be flattened in some suitable way and kept both flat, and at right angles to the top surface. The square edge at the front of the bedknife contacts the grass and wears away in the process. Occasionally you may need to file or grind back the vertical front surface of the bedknife until its top corner is sharp again, instead of rounded by wear. Traditionally this was done by careful use of an oilstone. There is a simple tool sometimes called the Bedknife Buddy which attempts to do it without requiring as much skill as an oilstone does. The front surface of the bedknife must always be precisely flat along its whole length, and precisely parallel to the axle of the reel. The advantage of the Bedknife Buddy is that it keeps the front of the bedknife square to its top surface - keeping the front surface flat and parallel is still up to the person doing the sharpening47. When using the Bedknife Buddy not much force is needed and only three or four passes across the top edge should suffice. Remember, the Buddy was not designed to restore the edge, but rather maintain it. It may also be used after the bedknife has been ground to remove any burring to the edge. The nylon holding block moves the reel out of the way while the (replaceable) file puts a light clean up edge on the front face of the bedknife.

47 https://www.outdoorking.com/forum/ubbthreads.php?ubb=showflat&Number=29940 accessed 12 July 2019 Bedknife Buddy Credit www.accuproducts.com. Using the Bedknife Buddy Credit Darren Tooth, SBR

sections.title: When does the bedknife need replacing?
sections.text:
296 Ver. 25 When does the bedknife need replacing? The determining factor for a standard bedknife or bottom blade is its thickness at the leading edge. If it is 2 mm or less it should be replaced. If it looks like a “serrated edge bread knife”, it needs replacing. Other factors to consider are: • is it straight? • does it have any significant “dings” (as in the below picture) that will not be ground out when sharpened. If in doubt, seek advice from a professional sharpener.

2mm minimum thickness Bedknife thickness Credit David DQ Closey, SBR Bedknife blade damaged beyond repair. Credit Don Bosworth, SBR

sections.title: Relief grinding of Reels
sections.text:
297 Ver. 25 Relief grinding of Reels

Many commercial reels come with a relief grind on the back of the reel blade to improve the quality of cut, reduce metal to metal contact, heat, and wear. However, in the case of the domestic Scott Bonnars (e.g. Models 33,40 and 45) they are not believed to have been sold with relief ground reels. Some may be found with relief ground reels; these are thought to have been done after market. During backlapping the relief supposedly traps paste between the reel and blade, making for more effective backlapping. VNR Mechanical advise relief grinding does have a benefit but in the case of Scott Bonnars, the reel blades are thin to start with and a relief grind would make them more susceptible to damage. As far as stopping heat build-up, you should not have heavy contact between the reel and knife if it is sharp in the first place. When the bedknife is ground, a relief angle is ground into the top surface of the bedknife. Broken Pull Cord Over time due to wear and tear the pull start cord wears and may break, often where it is knotted inside the pull start assembly. Remember the pull start is spring loaded. Have a length of new cord ready and cut to length. Remove cowling, depending on the pull start mechanism replace the central bolt (those with a plastic spool do not), tighten it up. Wind the spool up anti clockwise until it is tight back it off a full turn. Line up pulley with the cord hole then put your cord back in and tie off, put handle on the other end then let go of pulley and you should be good to go. Whilst doing this you will have to maintain tension i.e. hold it to stop the spring from taking over. The other way you can wind it up is to tie the cord off first thru the hole. There is a small notch that the cord can sit in as you wind it up tight then back a turn. Relief ground reel with Inset showing a normal reel. Credit Kym Schrapel, SBR The relief grind Bedknife Relief grind diagram Credit Kyle Murphy, SBR

sections.title: Install recoil spring into older B&S starters
sections.text:
298 Ver. 25 Trim up the end of the knot and burn seal it to prevent the cord from fraying. Turn clockwise so you can pull rope up onto the notch.

Install recoil spring into older B&S starters This is a task that brings fear and dread into the hearts of many people. In this case I will leave the explanation to video links. There are numerous YouTube videos available however SBR member Stephen Moran has provided a short video showing how to install the recoil spring into the older Briggs and Stratton starters. Please see: https://www.facebook.com/groups/1801521490103765/permalink/2574871239435449/

Hole for cord Notch Notch Broken pull cord Credit Matt Burton SBR Replacing pull cord Credit Matt Burton SBR

sections.title: How much does it weigh ?
sections.text:
300 Ver. 25 How much does it weigh ? ▪ Model 45, 17” with catcher – 65 kgs. Will fit on a standard half pallet. They are approximately 480 mm X 555 mm and 490 mm high (depends on the engine) ▪ Model 45, 20” (twin rail and Unicorn) – 170 lbs. or 77 kgs. (Allow approx. 85 kgs for shipping incl pallet weight) 910 mm L x 650 mm W x 800 mm H ▪ Model 65, 24” – 80 Kgs (unconfirmed) ▪ Model 65, (65029) 24” S/Cut with transport wheels – 144 kgs ▪ Model 38 Edger – 23.5 kgs ▪ 24” Queen (Mod 16) – about 190 kgs ▪ Model 46 – approx. 50kgs. Dimensions approx. 800 wide, 1000 long, 900 high ▪ Model 45, 17”rear roller (minus ratchet box) – 12 kgs How much will it cost to give my Scott Bonnar a fresh look and all cleaned up? Translated this question reads "how long is a piece of string that I want". You can spend a couple hundred through to couple of thousand dollars dependent on the extent of the rebuild. sections.title: What is the Height of Cut of a Model 45?
sections.text:
302 Ver. 25 What is the Height of Cut of a Model 45? The minimum height of cut (HOC) for a Model 45 is about 4 – 5 mm (the thickness of the bedknife) and the maximum HOC is 20 – 23 mm using a standard roller. If using e.g. a SB Fabrication and Parts stainless steel front roller, the HOC is increased by 3mm and if using their high cut bracket then a maximum of approximately 33 mm can be obtained. If you want to go higher than the 33 mm, go rotary or train your grass down and turn the grass into lawn. If you must increase the HOC, after marker rollers and high cut brackets are your best options. Do not bother adding thread to the L-shaped height adjuster bracket, that will NOT add to the HOC. Increasing the elongation (see opposite picture) of the slot at the top of slide will, BUT that has its limitations as the angle of the curve takes the L Bracket off centre and causes the spindle to bind. As a general “rule of thumb” one turn on the height adjuster is close to 1 mm in height. Factors affecting the Height of Cut Greenkeepingeu.com advise most turf professionals are aware that the actual mowing height is different to the bench set height. The grass plant offers resistance to the weight of the unit, and this will affect the actual height of cut that is achieved. This effect can easily be seen when a smooth and grooved roller (of the same approximate weight) are laid on a piece of foam. The grooved roller sits further into the material due to its reduced surface area, which increases the actual ground pressure. It should be noted that this effect will change with the growth rate of the turf in question, that is winter and summer and will also vary according to the amount of thatch build up in the playing surface. Fitting a heavier smooth roller can give the same actual mowing height as a lighter weight grooved roller, but with greater surface smoothing effect and less marking, which can lead to a faster, truer playing surface.

Do not over complicate things, your HOC is only relative to your lawn. For me, I am mowing a lawn not a green; so long as it looks how I want it the HOC is meaningless. I start in spring HOC = low, end of summer HOC = higher. In other words, mow to a height that suits and enjoy the grass carpet!

Elongating this slot to increase the HOC has been suggested Side plate. Credit Mark Virgulti, SBR.

sections.title: Factors affecting the Height of Cut
sections.text:
302 Ver. 25 What is the Height of Cut of a Model 45? The minimum height of cut (HOC) for a Model 45 is about 4 – 5 mm (the thickness of the bedknife) and the maximum HOC is 20 – 23 mm using a standard roller. If using e.g. a SB Fabrication and Parts stainless steel front roller, the HOC is increased by 3mm and if using their high cut bracket then a maximum of approximately 33 mm can be obtained. If you want to go higher than the 33 mm, go rotary or train your grass down and turn the grass into lawn. If you must increase the HOC, after marker rollers and high cut brackets are your best options. Do not bother adding thread to the L-shaped height adjuster bracket, that will NOT add to the HOC. Increasing the elongation (see opposite picture) of the slot at the top of slide will, BUT that has its limitations as the angle of the curve takes the L Bracket off centre and causes the spindle to bind. As a general “rule of thumb” one turn on the height adjuster is close to 1 mm in height. Factors affecting the Height of Cut Greenkeepingeu.com advise most turf professionals are aware that the actual mowing height is different to the bench set height. The grass plant offers resistance to the weight of the unit, and this will affect the actual height of cut that is achieved. This effect can easily be seen when a smooth and grooved roller (of the same approximate weight) are laid on a piece of foam. The grooved roller sits further into the material due to its reduced surface area, which increases the actual ground pressure. It should be noted that this effect will change with the growth rate of the turf in question, that is winter and summer and will also vary according to the amount of thatch build up in the playing surface. Fitting a heavier smooth roller can give the same actual mowing height as a lighter weight grooved roller, but with greater surface smoothing effect and less marking, which can lead to a faster, truer playing surface.

Do not over complicate things, your HOC is only relative to your lawn. For me, I am mowing a lawn not a green; so long as it looks how I want it the HOC is meaningless. I start in spring HOC = low, end of summer HOC = higher. In other words, mow to a height that suits and enjoy the grass carpet!

Elongating this slot to increase the HOC has been suggested Side plate. Credit Mark Virgulti, SBR.

sections.title: Height of Cut Gauge
sections.text:
303 Ver. 25 Height of Cut Gauge Measuring the HOC is simple, you do not really need a HOC Gauge, put the mower on flat concrete e.g. a path and measure between the top of the blade and the path. However, if you do want a one, the gauge used to fine tune the reel to rear roller doubles as a HOC Gauge.

Scott Bonnar mower market dynamics For those new to the Scott Bonnar restoration scene it may be a daunting exercise sourcing a mower. There will be mowers in various states of repair for a range of prices. Understanding the market will help navigating through the various online markets. It is worthwhile to consider the type of person advertising the mower for sale. The seller can be broadly classified as follows:

1. The Mower Shop. The mowers will often be priced at the higher end. Even though the seller may be an individual, with a little research they can be identified as a bonafide business. As such, they run a business to make a profit and a profit margin will be included in the asking price. Being a business then consumer law should apply, and they will be providing a warranty as to their goods supplied.

2. The Second-hand Dealer. Again, with a little research and like the Mower shop they can be identified as a business. These mowers appear to be priced from the mid to high range with little or no work done to the mower.

3. The “Flipper”. This is the person who sources mowers cheaply (e.g. the “barn finds”); may do a minor clean up and charges several hundred more than the mower is realistically worth.

4. The Restorer. Sells their restoration project/mower basically to recoup costs and fund the next project. These will in most cases be value for money. The hard work has been done for you.

5. The half informed but uneducated. This is the seller who has done some research and seen the prices for restored mowers and asks similar prices for their un-restored mower. Often these sellers will not budge with the asking price.

6. The uneducated. Knows nothing about the market, perhaps is cleaning out a shed from a deceased estate. These are the ones likely to be selling for less than a fair asking price as they do not know what they are selling.

HOC Gauge Credit Brock Bearsby, SBR

sections.title: How much is it worth?
sections.text:
304 Ver. 25

7. The educated or informed seller. Knows what it is worth; asks a fair and reasonable price.

The above is neither intended as a defense or criticism of any of these classes of seller. How much is it worth? Commonly asked by “newbies” to the page, are questions such as “after guidance on what this is worth?” or “what would you pay for it?” The question is often accompanied with a picture taken from an online site such as Gumtree or Market Place. Before asking this question, take the time to do your own research as to what the market is like in your area. In other words, “know your market”. Would you buy a car without doing homework?. Prices in Perth for example appear to be less than in Adelaide, whereas prices are higher in Victoria. The highly sought-after Unicorn was seeing starting prices around $1000 ranging up to around $2000 for a freshened-up mower circa 2020. It is not uncommon now to see them now advertised for around the $3000 mark for a complete restoration. These prices are having a flow on effect across the country, to other models and sizes. The $50 buy still exists if you are lucky, but they are becoming fewer as demand increases. So, if you are desperate you will pay what is asked, or you will have to bide your time. Below wass a set of realistic prices and value for untouched Model 45’s. These were general prices which were applicable Australia-wide, based on median prices and as of 201948 and 2020. Model 45 - Suggested realistic second hand, untouched prices Model Price $ Apr 2019 Price $ Oct 2020 14” Twin 250 400 14” Solid 300 450 17” Twin 400 500-600 17” Solid 450 550-650 20” Twin 550 750-800 20” Solid 700 900 + The October 2020 prices indicate a significant rise attributed to the increased popularity of Scott Bonnar mowers. Since then prices have increased further and that trend is likely to continue. Supply and demand factors are at play. Again, do your homework.

48 Values as per advice from Glenn Russell on 19/4/2019 Consider this – while you are waiting for a response to your post someone has seen the post, searched for and found where the mower was advertised and has already made an offer for it … and it’s gone.

sections.title: What to look out for when buying.
sections.text:
305 Ver. 25 There are varying standards of a restoration. Some people have more skills, knowledge and resources than others. Remember - anything is only worth what someone wants to pay for it. If your happy to pay the asking price then go ahead and buy it and enjoy, if not then keep looking.

What to look out for when buying. Before you decide to buy, ask yourself: • What do I want, a ready to use mower or a project? • Do I have the skills, knowledge to repair or restore it? • Do I have the time? • Can I afford the repair/ restoration costs on top of the purchase price? If you answer No, realistically buying new may be a better option. If you answer Yes and you have made the decision to buy a second-hand reel mower there are various matters you may (should) consider: • What do you want out of the mower? o Something that is ready or near ready to use; or o A project to restore • Is it missing any parts? o Particularly important for models other than Model 45 o e.g. chain covers for Model 33 and 40 – not available aftermarket o e.g. Model 40 rubber friction drive is a must have – they are not easily sourced. • What is the condition of the reel? o Has it and the blade been recently sharpened o How much life is left on the reel blades and the bottom blade? o Are there any broken/missing or bent reel blades? • What is the condition of the engine? o Original or replaced with a new engine ▪ If new what is it - B&S, Honda or a cheap “Chonda”. o Does it run o Does it leak oil o When was it last serviced (or even used)? • Is there any sign of cracking on the rails or the deck? • Are the clutch halves original? o A differently painted or even an unpainted clutch half may indicate it has been replaced, if in doubt – ask. • What is it like under the chain cover? o Sprocket and chain condition o Chain idler condition • What is the condition of the “drive-line”? o Crank and PTO keyway condition o PTO shaft, inner cone, clutch half

306 Ver. 25 ▪ Can be checked by checking for play in the keyways. Hold the PTO firm and wriggle the inner cone to check for play. Do the same with the crank (pull the starter cord and hold firm while you move the clutch body back and forth. • What is the general appearance? o Paint condition o Paint or powder coat ▪ If powder coated, sand blasting is the better option to strip it. Therefore, factor the cost of sand blasting. o Rust o Condition of the chain cover o Condition of handlebars ▪ Is the handlebar cover plate present? o Condition of rear rollers ▪ Any cracks, are they worn/smooth? o Condition of catcher ▪ Catcher or no catcher? • If you are looking for a project to restore: o Know what you can afford o Have an idea of the likely costs involved (refer to Annex 5 for indicative prices) ▪ Replacement parts ▪ Are you going to outsource sandblasting or strip the paint yourself? ▪ Paint and decal costs ▪ Be prepared for unexpected costs. ▪ Do you have enough basic tools or need to buy tools? ▪ If you are going to use “mates” to assist (e.g. to weld cracked rails), ask yourself “do they have the necessary skill level”, otherwise it may cost more in the long term. • If you are looking to purchase a “Unicorn” o Check it out thoroughly, ensuring it is a genuine solid deck, not a twin rail conversion. ▪ Refer to Chapter 2 “Difference between Solid and Twin Rails”.

sections.title: 20” Model 45 bearing holders
sections.text:
307 Ver. 25 20” Model 45 bearing holders A piece of advice for 20” Model 45 owners (both Unicorn and twin rail versions). Be aware that there are differences between Series 1 and 2 versions. Below is a Series 2 machine which is still running the 3-piece rear roller. One of the major changes with the Series 2 machines was Scott Bonnar opted to go to a two piece roller like the 17” machine. The other difference is the reel bearing sizes were larger in the Series 1 machines, however when Scott Bonnar went to Series 2, they reduced the bearing and carrier to suit the smaller RLS5 bearing. It appears Scott Bonnar considered the 20” machine to be a domestic mower and not part of the commercial field as earlier 20” machines were. If you have the larger units then you should treat those carriers like gold as they are no longer available. You can retro fit back to the smaller size if you must. Just make sure you use the correct cutter sprocket if you retrofit to the smaller housings. The sprocket for the larger housings has a shorter snout than standard sprocket.

Above and below - Series 2, 20" Mod 45, 1972-73 build with 3-piece rear roller. Credit Bluey Cullen, SBR

sections.title: Early Model 45 PTO Bearing Holder
sections.text:
308 Ver. 25 Early Model 45 PTO Bearing Holder

Some early 14” and 17”model 45 solids have been found with the PTO bearing holder shown opposite. They are not commonly seen.

Electric Mowers – Cable Bracket Many Scott Bonnar 240 volt electric mowers still have a bracket attached to the handlebars like the one shown opposite. The purpose of the bracket is to affix the extension cable so it does not pull out whilst allowing the cable to move side to side. After mowing the bracket can be used to wrap the cable around for storage.

Early model 45 solid PTO bearing holder Credit Luke Duke, SBR Extension cable bracket Credit FB Marketplace.

sections.title: Kirby Model Numbers
sections.text:
309 Ver. 25 Kirby Model Numbers Kirby engines are referred to with a “HK” model number, but some will be tagged with a “H4” prefixed model number. Below is a simple reckoner: • H4-4A = HK20 (2 hp) • H4-5A = HK25 (2.5 hp) • H4-6A = HK30 (3 hp)

Kirby specifications Credit Ben Warner, SBR

sections.title: Converting an electric model to a petrol powered version
sections.text:
310 Ver. 25

Converting an electric model to a petrol powered version Due to the 240 volt versions of the Model 40 and 45’s being less sought after they tend to be cheaper than the petrol powered counterparts. Some people are tempted to buy the cheaper electric version with a view to converting them to a petrol powered model. So, what would be involved in such a conversion? You are up for: • An engine; throttle cable and control lever. • Complete clutch assembly. • PTO shaft, thrust pad and complete clutch fork assembly • Positioning and mounting the engine (electric motor has different mounting pattern) and the clutch fork assembly (does not exist on the electric). • Reel sprocket replace (electric and petrol versions are different) • Potentially the above could cost between $1000

- $1200 new. It is also worth considering the electric versions in their heyday were less popular than the petrol versions and as such are rarer to this day. I dare suggest an electric model 45 is even rarer than the “Unicorn”. Yes, the conversion could be done, but please consider the history and do not make a fraudulent mower, it’s a bit like making a Unicorn out of a 20” twin rail model 45. Front roller types The basic groups of ground contacting front rollers are shown opposite49. Full rollers Provide a stable platform using full width contact. Some characteristics of full rollers include: • Increases the effective cutting height. • Rides higher or floats more on the turf. • Can be made heavier or larger to help with cutting unit control. • Less likely to scuff the turf in turns.

49 https://cdn2.toro.com/en/-/media/Files/Toro/Commercial/education-technical-references/service-training- guides/09168sl_5-4-2018.ashx?la=en&hash=1531E3CD624D41D0E5C07157493AFECD0FF67BEE accessed 7 August 2019 Front roller types Credit Toro University Technical Training Kirby engine models Credit David Carter, SBR

sections.title: Wiehle Rollers (or grooved) rollers
sections.text:
311 Ver. 25 • Less likely to damage the turf when lowering onto it. Narrower rear rollers can help address overlap roller marks in cool season grasses. Note that the narrower rollers can reduce the stability of the cutting unit. Wiehle Rollers (or grooved) rollers Provide a stable platform with some different characteristics. • Contact line is broken into individual contact points allowing for greater surface pressure at the contact point. • Individual contact points allow for better incursion into the turf canopy. • Allows seed heads to be cut and gathered better. Note that these rollers can sometimes scuff the turf in turns. There are variations of the Wiehle roller. • Aluminum - lessens contact pressure due to weight difference. • Cast iron - increases cutting unit control by increasing contact pressure. • Wider spacing - tends to increase incursion into the canopy due to increased contact pressure while disturbing less grass. • Narrower spacing - tends to lessen incursion into the canopy acting more as a full roller.

What is the difference between a Groomer, a Verticutter and a Scarifier? Whilst they look similar, groomers and verticutters have quite different uses and are shown below. The third option is the Scarifier. These three turf maintenance operations vary in their objective and the depth to which they are performed. Before purchasing one of these you should consider whether you need to do any or all of them, which will depend on the type of grass in your lawn/turf, its condition and the function of the area. If you are wanting to simply remove thatch a cheaper alternative is scalping your lawn or the use of a thatching rake. Scarification penetrates the surface - into the soil or root zone - in a raking or scratching action to remove fibres, debris, thatch and moss. It can be of benefit where a layer of fibrous material has built up and is beginning to cause problems with water infiltration or during renovation work prior to top- dressing. Thatch build-up is particularly noticeable where there is a prevalence of annual meadow grass, in fine turf and where the grass is mown regularly and cut low.

Underside of a Scott Bonnar Mod 46 Scarifier. Source OutdoorKing.com

312 Ver. 25 The difference between grooming and verticutting has been summarized by Pitchcare.Com50 as follows. Both grooming and verticutting use vertical blades but, from a biological standpoint, the similarities end there. Turf groomers and verticutters are not the same. Here are just a few of the differences:https://d2j31icv6dlhz6.cloudfront.net/L/m77oggUhj7tTihNnEcf9/Grooming-close-up-jpg.jpg • The grooming process utilises narrow vertical blades to periodically clip the advancing growing points of the turf grass. • The verticutter utilises the process of rotating vertical blades which penetrate deep into the crown area of the plant and possibly below, severing stolons and stems. This generally provides an effective means of reducing or removing thatch. • Verticutting can also be considered remedial in that it is a process by which we are trying to reduce, remove or eliminate a problem. The verticutter most often will be a stand-alone rotating shaft. • On the other hand, the groomer, an attachment in combination with the spider reel, makes up the principal cutting units of a reel mower. Verti-cutting, or verti-mowing, uses a machine fitted with blades that reach vertically into the turf canopy and are set to a depth where they can achieve their objective of cutting through lateral stems. The blades may also dislodge a small amount of thatch but should not penetrate the soil or turf root zone. Verticutter reels generally have blades spaced wider apart than groomer reels. Grooming goes less deep than verti-cutting and is really a light combing action usually carried out prior to mowing, to lift prostrate stems ready to be cut. It is important to make sure groomers are set at the exact required height before using - and remember, the setting may vary with the season. To avoid damage to the groomer blades, it is wise not to use groomers for several days after top-dressing applications have been made51. Groomers just cut through runners to make more individual plants meaning a thicker lawn and take out a little thatch if deep enough. In a situation like on a bowling green that gets hammered with a roller, leaf tips can be folded over and mashed into the surface over a period of say 5 weeks (fine blade couch in this case); so you run the groomer through at approx.

50 https://www.pitchcare.com/news-media/whats-the-difference-between-grooming-and- verticutting.html?fbclid=IwAR17KkdDo8tWqsLyYTqqGekyRvLi9PwA8pxCP6HDIPi28zy3z-tyUM4PJpA accessed 10/6/2019 51 https://www.hortweek.com/difference-scarifying-verti-cutting-grooming-i-need-all/parks-and- gardens/article/996824 accessed 29/08/2018. Model 46 Scarifier. Credit Kym Schrapel SBR

50 https://www.pitchcare.com/news-media/whats-the-difference-between-grooming-and- verticutting.html?fbclid=IwAR17KkdDo8tWqsLyYTqqGekyRvLi9PwA8pxCP6HDIPi28zy3z-tyUM4PJpA accessed 10/6/2019 51 https://www.hortweek.com/difference-scarifying-verti-cutting-grooming-i-need-all/parks-and- gardens/article/996824 accessed 29/08/2018. Model 46 Scarifier. Credit Kym Schrapel SBR

sections.title: Conversion to a Groomer or Verticutter
sections.text:
313 Ver. 25 1mm - 5mm or neutral to pick up the leaf or stand it up or create a small non-invasive line for a new leaf to grow into. Upright leaf creates a faster uniform playing surface. SBR member Peter Morison summarized the differences as grooming is going into the canopy, verti- mowing is going to the top of soil to prevent lateral growth and scarifying is deep into root zone. Grooming is, say height of cut 12.5 mm grooming is at 8- or 9-mm height of cut. Promotes density, new growth. Grooming is normally associated with low heights of cut like golf greens, bowling greens. Best results normally 10 to 15% below the height of cut. Never gets close to the ground like verti cut and scarifying. You can use a verticutter as a groomer (adjust the HOC up), BUT you cannot use a groomer as a verticutter. Trident Lawnmowers also have a particularly good section on their website52 which covers the differences between verticutting and grooming with some practical advice when to groom. A final word on this subject; the Model 46 (above) is the only factory built or genuine item. Groomers and verticutters are recent aftermarket modifications which people are making, mainly to Model 45’s.

Conversion to a Groomer or Verticutter So, you have decided to convert your mower into either a groomer or verticutter. Before you outlay your hard-earned money (around $500), consider what you want to do, groom or verticut. If you are after a dual-purpose reel consider a verticutter reel. It can verticut and groom whereas a groomer reel can only groom. The verticutter can take more of a ”hit” because usually they are made from thicker steel. All you do is adjust the height and do a triple pass on your lawn in different directions. Once you have made the purchase it is essentially a case of swapping the cutting reel for the verticutting/groomer reel. However, there are several considerations to be addressed.

52 https://www.tridentlawnmowers.com.au/verticutting- grooming?fbclid=IwAR2p9MknFDotx8RqVOvytiuIOqL4gAEJxZ0HOqftA-3zcxx1p3s5rc68gng accessed 13/01/2020 Tri blade groomer Credit Lachy Hall, SBR A verticutter Credit Lachy Hall, SBR

314 Ver. 25 When installing either blade sets into your mower you may need to lower the verticutter/groomer reel height. If you drop it all the way down to the lowest point in the slot and it will not go any lower then you will have to insert several additional links in the chain to allow the reel to go lower (NB this will depend on the diameter of the verticutter/groomer reel compared to the original cutting cylinder). You also will have to modify the soleplate because the blades will strike the soleplate. Furthermore, the modifications with also be required if you want to keep the grass deflector. The options are: • replace the soleplate with a threaded rod. (Not the recommended option) o However, rod can cause the frame to move, and you are unable to fit the deflector to it. • modify the soleplate i.e. remove the blade and perhaps even cut back some of the soleplate o note: this option means you will not be able to revert to a mower without sourcing another soleplate. • move the original soleplate back to provide the necessary clearance. This will necessitate removing the blade and moving the soleplate back in the frame. New mounting holes in the side plates will need to be drilled. Be mindful of not moving too far back to still clear the rear rollers. o The use of larger diameter flat washers should be enough to hide the original holes. This option allows the mower to be returned to a mower if required later. • customize a new soleplate between the end plates to fit the original mounting holes. o Recommended: Check out some of the popular suppliers of Scott Bonnar parts who can supply custom soleplates for this very purpose. o a SBR member has fashioned his own sole plate replacement out of 6mm x around 50mm flat bar that fits the original mounting holes and also holds the grass defector

Soleplate moved back - 2 washers to cover the old sole plate holes Credit George Bonmods, SBR

sections.title: Setting the height of the groomer/verticutter reel
sections.text:
315 Ver. 25

Final word on engine size. An original 2.5 hp on a 17” verticutter works. If you are considering an engine upgrade to a new 3.5 hp engine then they have ample power to drive a verticutter or groomer. Setting the height of the groomer/verticutter reel Finally, setting the reel will be the last thing to do. • Put the mower on the bench or a flat surface • Adjust the front roller so the mower is in the highest position • Adjust the reel down as low as you can go in the chassis, making sure it’s even and not touching the bench/floor, leave some room. • Put the mower on the lawn and use the front roller to lower the reel to the desired depth. Do I need a catcher with my Groomer/Verticutter? Do you need one? N0, but should you have one? YES. Clean up is so much easier; essentially a catcher picks up what you remove in one go rather than throwing it everywhere. Alternatively, you will need a rotary mower with a catcher to go over it to pick up all the grass etc. When using a catcher be warned, if you have any stones etc. below the grass it will dent the catcher from the inside out. So, a “banged up” catcher may be advisable. Convert to a verticutter/groomer and convert back to a mower? The question has been asked “Is it possible to convert it to a vertimower/groomer and once I've given my lawn a verticut/groom convert it back to a cylinder mower? The answer is yes; however, it would involve a lot of work. You will be resetting the reel every time, also consider whether you would be taking the bedknife off or changing the soleplate to another bracket. If it is a verticutter, the chain may have an added link as well so changing chain length may be required. The simpler option is to buy another mower and set it up as a dedicated verticutter. Do I disengage the reel or leave it spinning when emptying the catcher? Disengage the reel for the following reasons: • if you leave it spinning on the grass you will leave a line • having it spinning without cutting is bad for the reel and bottom blade Custom made replacement soleplate Credit Dylan Hobbs, SBR

316 Ver. 25 o metal on metal heats up without the lubrication of the grass • for safety reasons o if you get your hand in there; or o a child runs up Simply, disengage then shut the motor down. That way you will not start the motor under load of the reel being engaged.

sections.title: How do I follow a post?
sections.text:
317 Ver. 25 How do I follow a post? A Facebook hint about turning on notifications. Do you want to follow a post to see other contributions? Of course, you do! If you want to follow a post, commenting with e.g. a “F” just sends an annoying notification to everyone that the post has an update. It also indicates you do not know the functionality of Facebook by not using the notification feature. Simply go to the three dots at the top right of the post as shown below. This will open options such as “Turn on notifications” and “Save post” as below.

The location and how to activate the box with the 3 dots may differ on various devices, take some time to get to know yours. This way you can follow the post without clogging it with meaningless comments and if you like you can turn the notification off later. The other option is to save the post. Then, when you need it go to your Saved items and all updates will appear.

Three dots - click on Post options - turn on notifications

sections.title: The Scott Bonnar "Twelve Commandments"
sections.text:
318 Ver. 25 The Scott Bonnar "Twelve Commandments" 1. Thou shall crack thee nuts. 2. Search first, ask questions later. 3. Use and maintain your Scott Bonnar regularly. 4. Thou shall not covet thy neighbour’s Unicorn 5. Thou shall not convert twin rails into the image of a solid deck. 6. It is a Scott Bonnar or nothing. 7. Thou shall not accept a higher offer once a price and sale has been agreed to. 8. Thou shall not badger a seller with a higher offer when the seller has already agreed to sell to another. 9. Compliment, encourage and respect the work of others (not everyone has the ability or resources to create “concourse de elegance” builds). 10. Thou shall not wantonly dispose of Scott Bonnars. 11. Thou shalt not commit “Frankenstein” modifications. 12. Restore, renovate and improve, but be true to the spirit of the Scott Bonnar.

One Last Thing

How many times have you seen a “Scott Bonner” (sic) mower advertised? Please, its SCOTT BONNAR.

sections.chapter: Annex 2 – Pictorial reference of various models
sections.title: Annex 2 – Pictorial reference of various models
sections.text:
345 Ver. 25

Annex 2 – Pictorial reference of various models The “Bon” hand roller lawnmower (NEW) Outdoorking53 advise the “Bon” was essentially based upon the Scott Bonnar electric Ace lawnmower, a smaller version of the Standard design of the late 1920s. The Bon reflects Scott Bonnar's experience of Shanks' and Green's lawnmowers they sold (and converted to power) in the 1920s.Available in two sizes, the Bon had a cast iron frame and an 8-blade reel. An interesting feature is the double chain drive, indicating this was a top-end machine.

Above left and Right - The Bon. Credit Craig Hodge, SBR

53 https://outdoorking-forum.com.au/forum/ubbthreads.php/topics/77118/model-bon-a-handmower- c1930s.html?fbclid=IwY2xjawEhQjVleHRuA2FlbQIxMAABHRhw5DBjCvfAgtqD5Z1oPPHhNTttERFN0HWPPXaeiHL2dB YW_t4s_1aW8A_aem_U-3u22yG1iOv9XRFlK_0iA#Post77118. Accessed 8/8/2024.

sections.chapter: Annex 2 – Pictorial reference of various models
sections.title: The Centenary
sections.text: 346 Ver. 25 The Centenary The Centenary mower was made to celebrate the 1936 centenary of South Australia and was in production between 1936 and 1951. The Centenary was replaced by the Supercut. It is rare and a collector’s piece. (Below images sourced from Egan Laruffa, Scott Bonnar Addicts - #SBAddicts)

sections.chapter: Annex 2 – Pictorial reference of various models
sections.title: Greens Roller
sections.text:
347 Ver. 25 Greens Roller SBR member Peter Morison advises the greens roller came in either petrol or all electric versions and had a transporter frame. These were only sold and marketed by Scott Bonnar. It was designed for bowling greens. Similar rollers are now made in Queensland and the USA under the Tru Turf brand. They are regarded as one of the best greens rollers in the world. To operate it you pull the lever to go forward and push it the other way to go back and the pedal is to engage the belt drive.

Restored Scott Bonnar greens roller Credit Rhyan Heselden

sections.chapter: Annex 2 – Pictorial reference of various models

sections.text:
348 Ver. 25 Deluxe Motor Mower(NEW)

Above/Below. Deluxe Motor Mower Circa 1930’s Credit Tom Toad, SBR

sections.chapter: Annex 2 – Pictorial reference of various models
sections.title: Model 14 – Gang Mower
sections.text:
349 Ver. 25 Model 14 – Gang Mower The Model 14 gang mower came with either solid rubber or pneumatic tyres or cast iron tyres towing 3,5 or 7 units. Hydraulic 3,5 or folding 5, 6 blades each 30 inches wide. Originals were horse drawn before tractors. Production ceased in the early 1980’s. SBR member Peter Morison advises the 30 inch were 6 bladed. 10 bladed mowers were made especially for Adelaide Ovals curator Arthur Lance. Each gang weighed 400 lbs. Ray Gerrard was the last assembler on the production point in Thebarton. He built them from start to finish

Above, Below. Model 14 Gang Mower. Credit Michael Majkic, SBR

sections.chapter: Annex 2 – Pictorial reference of various models
sections.title: Fertiliser Spreader
sections.text:
350 Ver. 25 Fertiliser Spreader Scott Bonner made their own spreader but also sold the Dutschke spreader. Both are similar in appearance (shape and size) with the early model Scott Bonnars having a cast iron wheel and the outer rim was about 150mm wide. Later Scott Bonnar versions had a solid centre, and the outer rim was of a galvanized tin and about 250mm wide. Uneducated treasure hunters that spot those spreaders may think they have stumbled on a Scott Bonnar product because they may see " SCOTTS" written on them.

Dutschke Spreader. NOT a Scott Bonnar. Credit Hayden Livingston, SBR Scott Bonnar spreader – note the wheels. Scott Bonnar Catalogue Credit Jesse Walton, SBR

sections.chapter: Annex 2 – Pictorial reference of various models
sections.title: Outboard Motor
sections.text:
351 Ver. 25 Outboard Motor Odd as it may sound, Scott Bonnar sold a 2-stroke outboard motor. The Model number, history or any further details are not known. They are now rarely seen.

Outboard motor Credit Annabel Harkin, FB Marketplace

sections.chapter: Annex 2 – Pictorial reference of various models
sections.title: Aerator
sections.text:
352 Ver. 25 Aerator Scott Bonnar did not just produce mowers; they made a range of professional greens products. One such example was their aerator. These were ID Badged with a “DM” prefix. Very few have survived the scrap heap once the Ogden, Ings & Winston Suttle Drilling Machines hit the market as those brands didn't rely on the operator applying the down pressure on the drills like the Scott Bonnars did. The small cast alloy wheels up front are the same as the original " fifth wheel " on the Model 46 Scarifier.

Scott Bonnar Aerator Credit Josh Miller, SBR

sections.chapter: Annex 2 – Pictorial reference of various models
sections.title: Model 16 – Queen
sections.text:
353 Ver. 25

Model 16 – Queen

The Queen was released circa 1950, also known as the Model 16 in 20” and 24” petrol and electric versions. SBR member Peter Morison and former Scott Bonnar employee advises the first Scott Bonnar Queen was sold to the Sydney bowling club. Story is Mick Bonnar took it over in the dickie seat of an old car At some stage, Model 16 sizes changed to the production of 24” and 30” petrol models. The Model 16 was discontinued around the time of the release of the Model 65 and circa 1958 the Queen was released as the Model 30, specifically for the bowling green market being 30” electric only. Whether as a Model 16 or 30, the Queen is a greens mower, not suited to your normal domestic lawn due to their size, weight and need for a level surface. Electric Queen Credit Cameron Petrie, SBR Queen, Credit Outdoorking.com Model 16, 30" petrol. Note the 4 piece rear roller. Credit David Carter, SBR

sections.chapter: Annex 2 – Pictorial reference of various models
sections.title: Model 17 – Supercut
sections.text:
354 Ver. 25 Model 17 – Supercut

Regarded by some as the Rolls Royce of Australian Mowers. Initially they came with the “SC” ID plate, then later changed to a “17”. The Supercut came out after WW2, replacing the Centenary model They were superseded by the model 65.

20" Supercut Source SBR 24" Supercut Source SBR Supercut - electric Credit Trent Martin, SBR

356 Ver. 25 The Scott Bonnar '16" Bonmow' Model 33 mower was introduced in 1960 with a Villiers 98cc Midget Two Stroke engine. Models 33001 and 330026 were Lawn King variants with the Model 45 Clutch and all chain drive. Model 330413 is the standard version with the belt and chain drive and the Model 33 clutch with the longer cone.

Early Model 33 with 2-stroke Villiers and wooden front rollers. Source - Outdoorking.com A Lawn King variant of the Model 33 Source – Bonnar Bloke, ODK

357 Ver. 25

Model 33’s also came in the Lawn King variant (opposite picture), characterized by the Model 45 primary clutch, Model 45, 17” rear rollers; Model 45 chain tensioner, forward facing clutch lever, spacer between the clutch cone and thrust pad and all chain drive.

sections.chapter: Annex 2 – Pictorial reference of various models
sections.title: Model 43 – 20”
sections.text:
360 Ver. 25 Model 43 – 20”

Model 44 - Tow Tractor The Tow Tractor is perhaps one of the rarest Scott Bonnar mowers to be found in a complete and working condition. They came in either 30” or 36” cut versions. It appears that several versions were sold by Scott Bonnar. In 1963 they sold a “30 inch National Rider Mower” as a Model 44. (National Mower Co of Minneapolis, MN, USA.) Later they sold an Australian made Tow Tractor under the Scott Bonnar badge (actual model number is not known). The below brochure shows “Flymo Scott Bonnar” which indicates these were sold after 1970 (when Electrolux acquired Scott Bonnar). These Tow Tractors were manufactured by D & W Engineering, Dandenong, Victoria.

Model 43 Source Outdoorking.com Scott Bonnar brochure Credit Scott Milay, SBR Mark 2 Tow Tractor Credit Clinton Honor, SBR

sections.title: Model 45 (various)
sections.text:
361 Ver. 25

Model 45 (various)

Model 45, 14" Electric Source Outdoorking.com Mark 2 Tow Tractor label Credit Clinton Honor, SBR

362 Ver. 25 Model 45 – 12 volt electric Model 45 Mains and Battery Electric machines (450440 & 450466). When the Model 45 was introduced for the 1968 Lawnmower Season these versions were the variants of the older Model 40 (400119 & 400215). It would appear, then, that the Mains and Battery 45s were introduced for the 1970-71 Lawnmower Season54. The 12 volt battery version of the Model 45 is believed to have been sold to select bowling clubs. They are perhaps he rarest of the Model 45’s.

54 https://www.outdoorking.com/forum/ubbthreads.php?ubb=showflat&Number=64934 Accessed 26 February 2010 Model 45, 14" Solid Source SBR Above left and right - Model 45, 12 volt Credit Dennis Juran, SB

363 Ver. 25

Later Rover Model 45 “Series 2’s” had the rear facing clutch lever, plastic cover over the clutch, strengthened rails and a hard plastic PTO bearing holder. The top section of the handlebars were slightly narrower. The Model 45 Rover “Select”, model 45158, came with a 10 blade reel.

Model 45, 17" Solid with Kirby motor, Source SBR Model 45, 17" twin rail Source SBR Model 45158, Rover Select, 17" with 10 blade reel. Credit Kevin Wrigley, SBR

sections.chapter: Annex 2 – Pictorial reference of various models
sections.title: Craftsman and Lawn King
sections.text:
364 Ver. 25

Craftsman and Lawn King Within the Model 45 range there were several variants known as the Craftsman and Lawn Kings. The Craftsman Model 450810 was an MSSA (Mower Specialist Association of Australia) brand, a store brand for mowers made for them. The Lawn King Model 450546 was a budget version of the Model 45 using the smallest motor (the 2 HP) and sold through the likes of Retravision and Godfreys Bulk Store . Both variants were fitted with the Pram handlebars.

Left - Mod 45 Craftsman Credit Outdoorking.com

Model 45, 20" Solid "Unicorn" Source SBR Model 45, 20" twin rail Source SBR Right Mod 45 Lawn King Credit Peter Morison, SBR

sections.chapter: Annex 2 – Pictorial reference of various models
sections.title: Professional
sections.text:
365 Ver. 25 Professional Basically, it is a Craftsman, still with pram handles.

Model 430

Unlike the Model 45 which has a deflector to flick the grass backwards, up and over the front of the reel into the catcher; the Model 430 Diplomat simply throws the grass straight back over the rear drum into the catcher. The Diplomat uses the angled upright of the chassis to deflect the cuttings into the catcher.

Model 430 Diplomat showing rear catcher Source SBR Professional version Credit Mark Kirby, SBR

sections.chapter: Annex 2 – Pictorial reference of various models
sections.title: Model 520
sections.text:
367 Ver. 25 Model 520

Model 590

SBR member Shane Kosteszyn succinctly describes the difference between the Model 430 and 590 as follows: The Model 590 is not a Diplomat, but a standalone model proceeded by the Model 430 called the Diplomat. They are both belt-driven however the Diplomat has the more conventional cork clutch whereas the 590 is direct drive meaning squeeze the hand lever and the reel goes as does the rear roller. Note: Both Model 430 and 590 catchers are made of plastic and are rear mounted. They are NOT currently available after market. Model 590 Credit Australia Listed Model 520 Credit Bluey Cullen, SBR

sections.chapter: Annex 2 – Pictorial reference of various models
sections.title: Model 690 – Cross badged Morrison
sections.text:
369 Ver. 25 Model 690 – Cross badged Morrison

The Model 690 has a Scott Bonnar decal on the catcher with a Morrison ID Sticker on the side plate and Morrison hand grips. It is a Morrison of New Zealand built mower. They were fully imported complete from NZ. As per Outdoorking.com information, the Model 690 came about through a collaboration with Morrison Mowers (which itself had dealings with Victa Ltd hence its similarity with the Victa Imperial). In 1973, the New Zealand company Challenge Corporation Ltd, acquired Scott Bonnar. Hence the connection with Morrison Mowers. It is a genuine Scott Bonnar; but one that has Victa and Morrison in its heritage.

The overall quality of the mower is shall I say “cheaper” than a Scott Bonnar Model 45 as indicated by the “lighter weight” rear roller, three wood front rollers, single belt drive and plastic drive gear in the rear roller. But it was sold as a Scott Bonner Model 690. Some parts from Masport - Olympic MK 1, Models 400, 500 and 600 are compatible.

The Model 690 came in 2.5 hp Briggs and Stratton powered 16” version and a 3 hp Briggs & Stratton powered 24” version which had rubber bonded rear rollers.

Model 690 Source SBR Style of Morrison ID Sticker as seen on the side plate of SB690’s. Note: no model number is stated, only a serial number. Credit Shane Smith, SBR

sections.chapter: Annex 2 – Pictorial reference of various models
sections.title: Model 46 - Scarifier
sections.text:
372 Ver. 25 Model 46 - Scarifier

The Model 46 Scarifier was part of the professional range of Scott Bonnar products, at about a 19" cutting width, with cutters enclosed within a cast aluminum body. Initially powered by a 4.2 hp Kirby 4-stroke in the Series 1, (brass ID, tri snout, HK 40, 5th wheel) It would later be powered by 4 hp Briggs & Stratton engines (Series 2, brass ID, quad snout, Briggs, 5th wheel). This machine was introduced in the early 1960s when Scott Bonnar were expanding their professional range. It came with straight (grooming/grooving) or offset (scarifying) blades. The Model 46 produced by Rover were slightly different to the Scott Bonnar produced versions, unlike the Scott Bonnar Series 1 and 2 they do not have the “fifth wheel” as shown below. Discussion as to the variations between the different Series can be found at: https://www.facebook.com/groups/1801521490103765/permalink/2960606144195288/ The Model 46 continued to be produced by Rover after their 1980 takeover of Scott Bonnar55. Specifically, as seen in the below catalogue and price list, it had three different heads from factory; 19 blade scarifier in standard straight blades (grooving) and tungsten tipped or 10 blade offset (scarifying).

55 https://www.outdoorking.com/forum/ubbthreads.php/topics/94767/model-46-scarifier-groover- c1960s.html#Post94767 accessed 16 July 2019 Model 46 (series 2) Scarifier Credit Glenn Russell, SBR Rover Model 46 Credit eBay.com.au

373 Ver. 25

Scott Bonnar Mod 46 showing the 5th wheel. Credit OutdoorKing.com

1964 Factory price list Model 46. Credit Outdoorking.com

Model 46 Brochure Circa 1971. Source Outdoorking.com "Rover" Mod 46 without the 5th wheel Source SBR.

sections.chapter: Annex 2 – Pictorial reference of various models
sections.title: Model 85, Rover Thoroughbred
sections.text:
374 Ver. 25 Model 85, Rover Thoroughbred

This mower is often mistaken as a Scott Bonnar, probably because Rover took over the Scott Bonnar company. The Model 85 Rover Thoroughbred is NOT a Scott Bonnar. The Thoroughbred has no main clutch lever as it has a centrifugal clutch; the reel spins as you increase the revs from idle. There is a separate rear roller drive clutch on the handlebars. Parts are hard to come by. They are not compatible with Model 45’s. Victa Imperial Like the Model 690 the Victa Imperial was manufactured by Morrison of New Zealand. Late 70's early 80's.

Rover Thoroughbred. Credit Jamie Leviston, SBR. Victa Imperial Credit Troy Gatto, SBR.

sections.title: Annex 6 – Model 45 Indicative parts prices
sections.text:
387 Ver. 25 Annex 6 – Model 45 Indicative parts prices The following is a list of various Model 45 parts/items and indicative prices (rounded up)56. The prices are provided as a guide only for new items (unless otherwise specified). You may be able to get them cheaper or they may no longer be available at the price listed. Where research has shown a price variation between suppliers or different sizes or solid/twin rail, a price range is shown. Please DO NOT quote or use this guide for bartering/bargaining purposes when inquiring with a supplier as to their prices. This guide is provided primarily to assist with budgeting. Item Price57 Adjuster screws (pair) $70.00 Bearing Housing Rubber Spacer $5.00 Bearing Shields, reel $15.00 Bearing, Clutch thrust $30.00 Bearing, Self-Aligning Engine Sprocket Bearing and Rubber Spacer/Seal $30.00 Bearings, rear roller (pair) $45.00 Bearings, Reel (each) $15.00 Bottom blade (size dependent) From $30.00 Cable, Clutch $30.00 Cable, throttle $25.00 Catcher $200.00 - $350.00 Catcher Support Rubbers $8.00 Centrifugal clutch (Mod 45) $440 Chain Cover – fibreglass $130 Chain Cover Seal $8.00 Chain Side Reel Bearing Housing and Pivot Bolts $75.00 Chain Tensioner/Idler upgrade From $46.00 Chains (set of 3) $90.00 Chrome Plating $200.00 - $400.00 Clutch Cone $90.00 Clutch Cork Housing (Outer Cone) $40.00 Clutch Corks (set) $15.00 Clutch housing From $80.00 Clutch Lever Pin Set $15.00 Clutch Retaining Bolts $7.00 Clutch Spring (coil) $15.00 Contact Adhesive $10.00 Cotter pin $16.00 Cutter adjusting screws (set) $100.00
